I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to express my concerns regarding the lack of support for owner operators in enforcing their rights under Rule 49 CFR 371.3. As a stakeholder in the trucking industry, I believe it is crucial for the FMCSA to actively assist and advocate for the rights of owner operators. FMCSA has allowed broker fraud and non-compliance to run rampant and has yet to create the comprehensive enforcement program.

The lack of understanding by the FMCSA concerning this issue is astounding. It is imperative to comprehend that unlawful brokerage activities have far-reaching consequences. When brokers operate without proper oversight, it affects carriers' ability to maintain safe equipment, afford required insurance policies, and comply with licensing and permit requirements. These issues directly impact highway safety and the integrity of our nation's transportation infrastructure.

 Proper enforcement of rules deters unethical practices and enhances safety. The FMCSA must enforce regulations to protect the supply chain's integrity and the trucking industry's operational safety. The current process & lack of enforcement has led to high levels of fraud, predatory practices, and all stakeholders involved suffering. The industry is being exploited and is vulnerable as a result. 

The FMCSA has provided no way to report general freight brokers non-compliance. As a result the integrity & safety of the general freight industry is severely compromised. 

As the industry has evolved, so has the increase of identity theft, fraud, and market manipulation. This necessitates the dire need for regulation & oversight of load boards requirements, record keeping, privacy policies, data sharing, security, rate reporting, and data collection. At this time, there is zero oversight & enforcement upon general freight brokers. 

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3. We respectfully request that they do so at this time. 

We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,

I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to express my concerns regarding the lack of support for owner operators in enforcing their rights under Rule 49 CFR 371.3. As a stakeholder in the trucking industry, I believe it is crucial for the FMCSA to actively assist and advocate for the rights of owner operators. FMCSA has allowed broker fraud and non-compliance to run rampant and has yet to create the comprehensive enforcement program.

The lack of understanding by the FMCSA concerning this issue is astounding. It is imperative to comprehend that unlawful brokerage activities have far-reaching consequences. When brokers operate without proper oversight, it affects carriers' ability to maintain safe equipment, afford required insurance policies, and comply with licensing and permit requirements. These issues directly impact highway safety and the integrity of our nation's transportation infrastructure.

 Proper enforcement of rules deters unethical practices and enhances safety. The FMCSA must enforce regulations to protect the supply chain's integrity and the trucking industry's operational safety. The current process & lack of enforcement has led to high levels of fraud, predatory practices, and all stakeholders involved suffering. The industry is being exploited and is vulnerable as a result. 

The FMCSA has provided no way to report general freight brokers non-compliance. As a result the integrity & safety of the general freight industry is severely compromised. 

As the industry has evolved, so has the increase of identity theft, fraud, and market manipulation. This necessitates the dire need for regulation & oversight of load boards requirements, record keeping, privacy policies, data sharing, security, rate reporting, and data collection. At this time, there is zero oversight & enforcement upon general freight brokers. 

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3. We respectfully request that they do so at this time. 

We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3. We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,

The FMCSA has provided no way to report general freight brokers non-compliance. As a result the integrity & safety of the general freight industry is severely compromised. 

As the industry has evolved, so has the increase of identity theft, fraud, and market manipulation. This necessitates the dire need for regulation & oversight of load boards requirements, record keeping, privacy policies, data sharing, security, rate reporting, and data collection. At this time, there is zero oversight & enforcement upon general freight brokers. 

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3. We respectfully request that they do so at this time. 

We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,

I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to express my concerns regarding the lack of support for owner operators in enforcing their rights under Rule 49 CFR 371.3. As a stakeholder in the trucking industry, I believe it is crucial for the FMCSA to actively assist and advocate for the rights of owner operators. FMCSA has allowed broker fraud and non-compliance to run rampant and has yet to create the comprehensive enforcement program.

The lack of understanding by the FMCSA concerning this issue is astounding. It is imperative to comprehend that unlawful brokerage activities have far-reaching consequences. When brokers operate without proper oversight, it affects carriers' ability to maintain safe equipment, afford required insurance policies, and comply with licensing and permit requirements. These issues directly impact highway safety and the integrity of our nation's transportation infrastructure.

 Proper enforcement of rules deters unethical practices and enhances safety. The FMCSA must enforce regulations to protect the supply chain's integrity and the trucking industry's operational safety. The current process & lack of enforcement has led to high levels of fraud, predatory practices, and all stakeholders involved suffering. The industry is being exploited and is vulnerable as a result. 

The FMCSA has provided no way to report general freight brokers non-compliance. As a result the integrity & safety of the general freight industry is severely compromised. 

As the industry has evolved, so has the increase of identity theft, fraud, and market manipulation. This necessitates the dire need for regulation & oversight of load boards requirements, record keeping, privacy policies, data sharing, security, rate reporting, and data collection. At this time, there is zero oversight & enforcement upon general freight brokers. 

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3. We respectfully request that they do so at this time. 

We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,

I hope this letter finds you well. I am writing to express my concerns regarding the lack of support for owner operators in enforcing their rights under Rule 49 CFR 371.3. As a stakeholder in the trucking industry, I believe it is crucial for the FMCSA to actively assist and advocate for the rights of owner operators. FMCSA has allowed broker fraud and non-compliance to run rampant and has yet to create the comprehensive enforcement program.

The lack of understanding by the FMCSA concerning this issue is astounding. It is imperative to comprehend that unlawful brokerage activities have far-reaching consequences. When brokers operate without proper oversight, it affects carriers' ability to maintain safe equipment, afford required insurance policies, and comply with licensing and permit requirements. These issues directly impact highway safety and the integrity of our nation's transportation infrastructure.

 Proper enforcement of rules deters unethical practices and enhances safety. The FMCSA must enforce regulations to protect the supply chain's integrity and the trucking industry's operational safety. The current process & lack of enforcement has led to high levels of fraud, predatory practices, and all stakeholders involved suffering. The industry is being exploited and is vulnerable as a result. 

The FMCSA has provided no way to report general freight brokers non-compliance. As a result the integrity & safety of the general freight industry is severely compromised. 

As the industry has evolved, so has the increase of identity theft, fraud, and market manipulation. This necessitates the dire need for regulation & oversight of load boards requirements, record keeping, privacy policies, data sharing, security, rate reporting, and data collection. At this time, there is zero oversight & enforcement upon general freight brokers. 

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3. We respectfully request that they do so at this time. 

We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,

To Whom It May Concern,

The proposed amendments to FMCSA’s property broker rules reflect an effort to enhance transparency in freight transactions. While transparency is an important principle, I urge the FMCSA to consider the broader implications of mandating the automatic disclosure of brokered transaction rates to motor carriers.

1. Modern Business Practices Have Evolved Beyond CFR 371.3(c)
The current regulation requiring brokers to disclose financial records, including transaction rates, originates from a time when brokers operated under vastly different models. Today, brokers no longer work on commission paid by carriers but negotiate directly with shippers and independently compensate carriers. The margin brokers earn is based on the value they bring through market expertise, service reliability, and operational efficiency—not simply the rates they negotiate.

Forcing brokers to disclose these margins risks creating a false narrative about fairness while undermining the competitive forces that ensure carriers are appropriately compensated. Transparency does not necessitate a breakdown of costs and profit margins; it requires trust and equitable negotiation between parties.

2. Competitive Industries Protect Sensitive Financial Data
In industries like automotive sales, real estate, and retail, businesses are not required to disclose their costs or margins. Requiring freight brokers to disclose rates puts them at a disadvantage compared to these industries. Transparency does not mean requiring businesses to compromise their competitive edge by revealing trade secrets, such as contracted shipper rates.

Moreover, many shipper contracts explicitly prohibit the disclosure of negotiated rates as a means of protecting trade secrets and promoting healthy competition. Brokers adhering to these contracts would face legal and operational risks under the proposed rule, stifling innovation and efficiency in the market.

3. Rate Disclosure Does Not Account for the Full Value of Brokers
Brokers do more than facilitate transactions; they absorb market volatility, provide consistent freight volume, and streamline logistics for shippers and carriers alike. Profit margins fluctuate based on market conditions, cash flow needs, and contractual terms with shippers. Disclosing rates without context risks misrepresenting the value brokers provide and focusing attention solely on the numbers rather than the holistic service they offer.

4. Impact on Small Brokers and Carriers
Mandating rate disclosure could disproportionately harm small brokers who lack the legal and operational resources to navigate potential disputes arising from this requirement. Ironically, this could also disadvantage small carriers by limiting their access to a diverse range of brokers who provide vital opportunities in an otherwise competitive market.

5. A Modern Solution: Relationship-Based Transparency
Instead of mandating rigid rate disclosure, FMCSA should consider fostering relationship-based transparency. Brokers and carriers already have access to advanced rating tools, load boards, and market trends that empower carriers to make informed decisions about the rates they accept. Building trust through communication and service quality is a more effective approach to transparency than enforcing unnecessary regulatory disclosures.

Conclusion
I respectfully request that FMCSA reconsider the proposed amendments to CFR 371.3(c). The rule, as proposed, risks creating unnecessary burdens on brokers while failing to address the root of fairness in brokered transactions. A balanced, modernized approach to transparency that protects the interests of all parties without compromising the competitive marketplace would better serve the industry.

Thank you for your time and consideration of this perspective.

Sincerely,
Nate Cross

I strongly urge you to oppose FMCSA’s proposed rule expanding 371.3, which imposes outdated, burdensome, and unnecessary requirements on freight brokers and carriers. This regulation was formalized in 1980 under vastly different market conditions and no longer reflects how the transportation marketplace operates today.

Key reasons to oppose this rule:

Outdated Regulation: The 1980 intent was to ensure transparency when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, not the current two-separate-transactions model.
Overreach: The ICC initially aimed to remove unnecessary restrictions that could impede free market operations, not create new barriers.
No Evidence of a Problem: Even during the COVID-19 pandemic, this issue failed to generate consumer complaints. This is a solution in search of a problem.
Trade Secret Violations: The rule undermines the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, which protects financial and business information vital to competitive operations.
High Costs: Implementing electronic submissions via EDI or API could cost $2,500-$10,000 per integration, burdening carriers unnecessarily.
The FMCSA should focus on real industry concerns like highway safety and freight fraud, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually, instead of enacting this overreaching rule.

If this rule passes, Congress must use the Congressional Review Act to reverse it. The FMCSA’s actions conflict with Congressional intent to protect trade secrets, as expressed in the unanimous passage of the DTSA.

Please act now to oppose this regulation and protect the transportation marketplace from unnecessary government overreach.

As a freight broker, I’m concerned that the proposed changes to CFR 371.3(c) fail to reflect how the logistics industry has evolved. While I understand the intent of promoting transparency, these amendments could lead to unintended consequences for brokers, carriers, and shippers, potentially disrupting the industry’s delicate balance.

Outdated Perspective: The original CFR 371.3(c) was developed when brokers worked on commissions from carriers, making rate disclosure relevant for fair pay calculations. Today, the model is vastly different—brokers negotiate rates with both shippers and carriers while taking on financial risk through cash flow management. Applying an outdated framework to a modern industry adds unnecessary administrative burdens and could hinder the efficiency we’ve built over time.

Market Impact: Requiring automatic financial disclosures risks damaging the competitive neutrality that’s vital to our industry. Just as auto sales operate without revealing dealer margins, freight brokers shouldn’t be forced to disclose profit margins. Doing so undermines our ability to negotiate effectively, innovate, and provide consistent value to both carriers and shippers.

Beyond the Numbers: Brokers are far more than intermediaries—we handle logistics, mitigate risks, and manage cash flow to keep supply chains moving smoothly. Focusing only on rate transparency misses the larger picture and could violate confidentiality agreements with shippers, damaging the trust and partnerships that are essential to the industry’s success.

Leveraging Technology: Modern platforms already give carriers the tools they need to make informed decisions about rates, without requiring regulatory intervention. True transparency comes from fostering strong relationships—not from mandates that may unintentionally create friction between brokers, carriers, and shippers.

In summary, while transparency is an admirable goal, the NPRM takes an outdated approach that doesn’t align with the realities of today’s freight market. I urge FMCSA to work with industry stakeholders to develop modern, flexible solutions that uphold trust and fairness without stifling progress or imposing unnecessary burdens.

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's proposed rulemaking aimed at enhancing transparency in property broker transactions. This proposal appears to be an unnecessary expansion of regulation that does not reflect the current dynamics of the transportation industry.

The original intent behind 49 CFR 371.3, established in 1980, was to prevent freight rebating when brokers were essentially sales agents for carriers. Today, the marketplace has evolved significantly, involving distinct transactions between shippers and brokers, and between brokers and carriers. The requirement for brokers to disclose transaction details to carriers seems not only outdated but also an overreach of government into market operations that are already transparent through modern technological tools like loadboards.

Moreover, the NPRM fails to address the real issues affecting the industry. During the peak of the COVID-19 crisis, there were no significant complaints logged concerning the lack of transparency in broker transactions, suggesting that this rule is indeed a solution searching for a problem. The focus should instead be on critical areas like highway safety and tackling the rampant freight fraud that costs the supply chain billions annually.
Additionally, with the recent Supreme Court decision to overrule Chevron deference, there are legal concerns regarding the expansion of FMCSA's regulatory authority in this manner. The agency should prioritize enhancing existing protections like the prohibition of coercion rule rather than expanding into areas of commerce that function effectively without such interference.

The move toward rate transparency being a "duty" of broker means nothing unless FMCSA also says brokers cannot waive their new regulatory duty without such a waiver constituting "evasion of regulation" in furtherance of "unreasonable restraint of trade" in violation of the Sherman Antitrust Act. Without expressly prohibiting such contractual waivers, FMCSA is allowing brokers to continue to evade regulation and deregulate themselves. The status quo remains.

While Congress has explicitly allowed shippers and carriers to agree to waive certain requirements, Congress has not passed a law allowing the same for brokers. FMCSA already knows this and they have determined brokers are NOT shippers. 

FMCSA is therefore misguided in their logic and has this backwards. There does not have to be a law specifically prohibiting brokers from waiving because there already is a general law called Evasion of Regulation. There would need to be a law allowing brokers to waive, and no such law exists.

This proposed regulation is a violation of Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016. A trade secret is defined as: “all forms and types of financial, business, scientific, technical, economic, or engineering information, including patterns, plans, compilations, program devices, formulas, designs, prototypes, methods, techniques, processes, procedures, programs, or codes, whether tangible or intangible, and whether or how stored, compiled, or memorialized physically, electronically, graphically, photographically, or in writing if (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally known to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 

In addition to our trade secrets, this is a potential violation of our customer's trade secrets. It is very common for a shipper's contract to contain confidentiality clauses that prohibit discussing the terms and information contained in a contract with any other parties. The proposed rule should address those issues, when confidentiality could put a broker in breach of the contract.

As the owner of a small, independent freight brokerage, I am deeply concerned about the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) proposed expansion of 371.3 regarding rate transparency. This rule is not only outdated and burdensome but represents a significant overreach of government authority. It threatens the viability of small brokers like myself, who play a critical role in connecting shippers and carriers to keep America’s supply chains running smoothly.

When first formalized in 1980, this rule addressed a vastly different market landscape where brokers acted as commissioned sales agents for motor carriers. The regulation was aimed at preventing freight rebating—a practice involving financial collusion between brokers and shippers who were often common owners. However, the transportation marketplace has evolved dramatically since then. Today, the broker operates as a separate business entity, negotiating independent transactions with both shippers and carriers. Expanding 371.3 fails to reflect these modern realities.

Even the Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC), which originally developed this regulation, made clear its intent to support free market operations, stating: “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.” The FMCSA’s current approach runs counter to this objective, creating unnecessary burdens that impede small brokers while failing to address any documented issue in the current market.

Notably, during the height of the COVID-19 pandemic, when concerns about rate transparency were at their peak, no related complaints were found in the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database. This is a solution in search of a problem. The transportation marketplace already benefits from load boards and pricing tools that provide significant transparency, and the rule risks duplicating efforts at great expense. Implementing an electronic system for submission, such as EDI or API integration, would cost $2,500 to $10,000 per integration—costs that would ultimately burden motor carriers.

Furthermore, mandating the disclosure of proprietary pricing and business information may violate the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016. Forcing brokers to reveal sensitive contractual details jeopardizes competitive balance and puts trade secrets at risk. Such disclosure erodes trust between brokers, shippers, and carriers, and opens the door to disputes that damage relationships essential to the supply chain’s success.

The FMCSA should focus on its core mission of ensuring highway safety rather than imposing costly and unnecessary rules. Addressing the ongoing freight fraud pandemic, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually, is a far more pressing issue. Additionally, the FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers, rendering these additional measures redundant.

Finally, the U.S. Supreme Court’s recent overruling of the Chevron deference decision raises significant legal concerns about the FMCSA’s authority in this matter. Any expansion of 371.3 should be carefully reconsidered in light of these legal precedents.

Mr. President, small brokers like mine are the backbone of America’s transportation industry, providing flexibility and efficiency that larger entities often cannot match. Expanding 371.3 not only undermines our ability to compete but also threatens the free-market principles that have driven innovation and economic growth in this sector for decades. I urge your administration to reconsider this rulemaking and instead focus on policies that enhance, rather than hinder, the transportation marketplace.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Transparency in freight broker regulations is critical for ensuring fairness and equity in the transportation industry. Currently, in some situations, brokers are taking 50% or more of the load revenue, leaving carriers with significantly less despite doing the majority of the work. This imbalance not only undermines trust but also impacts the sustainability of carriers, especially small and independent operators.

Implementing a cap, such as limiting brokers to a maximum of 20% of the total revenue, alongside mandatory transparency laws, would create a fairer distribution of profits. Such measures would discourage exploitative practices, foster trust between brokers and carriers, and ensure that the hard work of carriers is adequately compensated. A transparent and equitable system benefits everyone, leading to a healthier, more competitive freight industry.

Subject: Comment on NPRM FMCSA-2023-0257: Concerns About Unintended Consequences for Carriers

Dear FMCSA Representatives,

As a carrier, I want to express my concerns about the proposed changes to freight broker transparency regulations (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257). While I understand the intention to promote transparency, I believe these measures could have negative consequences, particularly for small carriers.

Specifically, the following issues need to be addressed:
 1. Risk of retaliation and blacklisting: Requesting transaction records could expose carriers to being excluded from future business dealings by brokers who feel uncomfortable or threatened by such requests. This could lead to fewer opportunities for carriers and promote unfair practices.
 2. Administrative burden: Implementing these changes could increase tension between carriers and brokers, especially in competitive markets, making it harder to collaborate effectively and resolve disputes amicably.
 3. Limited impact on actual transparency: While requiring brokers to maintain electronic records may help, it does not directly address the power imbalance between brokers and small carriers. Additional safeguards are needed to protect carriers from retaliation when seeking transparency.

I propose that the FMCSA include provisions to prevent blacklisting and retaliation against carriers exercising their right to request records. Additionally, implementing anonymous reporting mechanisms could allow carriers to report abuses without fear of repercussions.

Thank you for the opportunity to share my concerns, and I hope the FMCSA will consider adjustments to this proposal to ensure that the regulations promote transparency without harming carriers.

Sincerely,

Orlando J. Rodriguez

I am writing in opposition to the changes being proposed under the Broker Transparency NPRM. Fair practices within the freight marketplace-handling do exist. On the other hand, this amendment oversteps into an excessive burden without regard for how our industry has evolved and creates unnecessary complications without solving an existing problem.

Expansion of 371.3 was originally promulgated as a regulation in 1980 when brokers served as commissioned sales agents of the carriers. This is no longer descriptive of today's marketplace, where a broker is an independent agent who brings together two separate, distinct business transactions involving shippers and carriers. It was intended to provide transparent practices to prevent freight rebating when there was common ownership between brokers and shippers, a business practice not indicative of today's marketplace.

The Interstate Commerce Commission described the goal of the regulation of brokered transactions themselves as an attempt to "remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace." Over forty years later, that is still accurate. There is no lack of transparency, and with the advent of pricing tools, load boards, and market technology, the carriers already have visibility. This additional requirement of electronic records from brokers for transactions would create API/EDI integration costs ranging from $2,500 to $10,000 per carrier and shift economic burdens to motor carriers.

This is further exacerbated by the fact that the proposed rulemakings violate the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 by publishing sensitive and proprietary pricing and business information. This is the last thing an industry needs that suffers over $1 billion annually from freight fraud. FMCSA should focus its resources and time to curb this fraud pandemic and improve highway safety rather than proposing regulations that offer no real-world value to carriers, brokers, or shippers.

It is telling that even during the COVID-19 pandemic, when market stresses were at their worst, there were no apparent problematic instances of broker transparency reflected in the consumer complaint database. This truly is a solution in search of a problem. The recent Chevron deference decision by the Supreme Court also raises legal issues about FMCSA's authority to make these changes.

FMCSA already forbids coercion of motor carriers and therefore already provides the necessary protections without adding this costly and unnecessary amendment. I hope you will reconsider this rulemaking and refocus your efforts on areas that truly enhance the safety and effectiveness of our supply chain.

Thank you for your consideration.

While Congress has explicitly allowed shippers and carriers to agree to waive certain requirements, Congress has not passed a law allowing the same for brokers. There does not have to be a law specifically prohibiting brokers from waiving because there already is a general law called Evasion of Regulation. I am writing to express my strong opposition to the FMCSA’s proposed rulemaking regarding the expansion of 371.3. This proposed rule represents unnecessary government overreach, relies on outdated concepts, and would impose significant burdens on the transportation industry without addressing any demonstrated problem. Below are the primary reasons for my opposition:

Outdated Regulation Misaligned with Market Evolution
The expansion of 371.3 stems from an era (1980) when brokers functioned primarily as commissioned sales agents for motor carriers. The marketplace has since evolved significantly, and the current model involves two distinct business transactions: one between the shipper and broker, and another between the broker and carrier. Applying decades-old concepts to the modern transportation industry ignores this evolution and creates unnecessary complications.

Original Intent Was to Prevent Freight Rebating
The original intent of 371.3 was to ensure financial transparency between parties to prevent freight rebating, particularly when brokers and shippers had common ownership. The proposed rule disregards this historical context and applies transparency requirements that are no longer relevant to how the market operates today.

Contradiction with the ICC's Established Goals
The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) clearly stated that the purpose of regulating broker transactions was to “remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.” This proposed rule adds layers of unnecessary bureaucracy that undermine the efficient operation of the transportation marketplace.

No Evidence of Consumer Harm
During the COVID-19 pandemic—when concerns about freight pricing were at their peak—there were no complaints related to this issue in the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database. This rule is a solution in search of a problem.

Existing Transparency and Modern Technology
The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and various pricing tools providing carriers with substantial information. Additional transparency mandates are redundant and could infringe upon pricing and business information protected under the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016.

High Implementation Costs
Safely achieving electronic submission of the proposed documentation would require costly integrations through EDI or API, with expenses ranging from $2,500 to $10,000 per integration. These costs would disproportionately burden small motor carriers, further straining an industry already under pressure.

Misaligned Priorities and Legal Concerns
FMCSA’s primary focus should be on highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic, which is costing the supply chain over $1 billion annually. The proposed rule diverts resources and attention away from these critical issues. Additionally, the U.S. Supreme Court’s recent ruling overturning the Chevron deference raises significant legal concerns about the authority for this rulemaking.

Existing Protections Are Sufficient
The FMCSA already has a coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers. Adding further regulatory burdens under the guise of transparency does not provide any additional value to carriers or the broader transportation industry.

In conclusion, the proposed expansion of 371.3 is unnecessary, costly, and inconsistent with the needs of the modern transportation marketplace. The FMCSA should prioritize addressing freight fraud and ensuring highway safety rather than pursuing this unwarranted rulemaking. I urge the agency to withdraw this proposal and focus on regulations that truly benefit the industry.

Thank you for considering my comments.

I am writing to express my disagreement with the proposed rule on broker rate transparency.

While I appreciate the agency's efforts to address concerns within the industry, I believe mandatory rate transparency would create more problems than solutions, leading to unnecessary administrative burdens, market distortions, and potentially damaging the relationships that drive this industry forward.

Increased Administrative Burden:
Requiring brokers to provide detailed transaction records on demand would impose significant additional work for brokerages, especially small to medium-sized operations like our own. Brokers already manage a myriad of responsibilities, from tireless daily sales efforts and customer service to back-office operations and compliance. These activities are crucial for maintaining service quality and growing relationships. Adding the task of constantly preparing and sharing financial details would divert resources from providing value to customers and carriers alike. The burden of this requirement risks overwhelming brokerages with additional paperwork and taking focus away from the core services we provide.

Lack of Context for Brokerage Costs:
It is no one’s business to know what a broker charges because the financial structure of each brokerage is unique and often misunderstood. Brokers incur numerous costs that are not immediately visible, such as daily sales efforts to source freight, back-office staff to ensure compliance and quality control, a multitude of insurance requirements, technology investments for transportation management systems, customer relationship management systems, load tracking and market analytics, and countless other operational expenses. Disclosing broker rates without context would paint an incomplete and misleading picture, potentially leading to unfair judgments about profit margins.

Impact on Market Dynamics:
The proposed transparency rule could disrupt the freight market by allowing carriers to decline rates based on perceived profit margins for individual loads. This would ignore the broader realities of supply and demand and create a situation where negotiations are influenced by incomplete information. Rates in the freight market are inherently variable, reflecting not just costs and margins but also service levels, operational complexity, and ever changing market conditions. Transparency on broker rates could result in fewer carriers accepting loads, thereby increasing rejection rates and destabilizing the market.

Long-Term Relationships and Sustainable Margins:
At Vantage, we prioritize long-term relationships with both our customers and carriers. These relationships are built on trust, consistency, and a shared commitment to success. There are numerous instances where we lose money on loads because maintaining a strong partnership is more important than short-term profitability. While we aim for an overall profit margin of 12%, there are many instances where we exceed this margin and others where we operate at a loss. The long-term health of our partnerships outweighs the fluctuations of individual transactions, and mandatory rate transparency could undermine these efforts by introducing unnecessary friction into those relationships.

Conclusion:
The freight industry thrives on collaboration and trust, not mandated disclosures that could sow discord. Brokers provide critical services that carriers and shippers rely on to navigate a complex market. Instead of focusing on rate transparency, the FMCSA should prioritize initiatives that promote education, communication, and support across the industry, ensuring all parties have the tools they need to succeed without jeopardizing operational efficiency or market stability.

I respectfully urge the FMCSA to reconsider this proposal and focus on fostering trust, safety, and collaboration rather than mandating transparency that could harm the very relationships that keep our industry moving.

Sincerely,

Sean Conley | Vantage Logistics 
Director of Business Development

Sconley@Vantagelgs.com

Applying decades-old concepts to the modern transportation industry ignores this evolution and creates unnecessary complications.
 
Original Intent Was to Prevent Freight Rebating
The original intent of 371.3 was to ensure financial transparency between parties to prevent freight rebating, particularly when brokers and shippers had common ownership. The proposed rule disregards this historical context and applies transparency requirements that are no longer relevant to how the market operates today.
 
Contradiction with the ICC's Established Goals
The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) clearly stated that the purpose of regulating broker transactions was to “remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.” This proposed rule adds layers of unnecessary bureaucracy that undermine the efficient operation of the transportation marketplace.
 
No Evidence of Consumer Harm
During the COVID-19 pandemic—when concerns about freight pricing were at their peak—there were no complaints related to this issue in the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database. This rule is a solution in search of a problem.
 
Existing Transparency and Modern Technology
The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and various pricing tools providing carriers with substantial information. Additional transparency mandates are redundant and could infringe upon pricing and business information protected under the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016.
 
High Implementation Costs
Safely achieving electronic submission of the proposed documentation would require costly integrations through EDI or API, with expenses ranging from $2,500 to $10,000 per integration. These costs would disproportionately burden small motor carriers, further straining an industry already under pressure.
 
Misaligned Priorities and Legal Concerns
FMCSA’s primary focus should be on highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic, which is costing the supply chain over $1 billion annually. The proposed rule diverts resources and attention away from these critical issues. Additionally, the U.S. Supreme Court’s recent ruling overturning the Chevron deference raises significant legal concerns about the authority for this rulemaking.
 
Existing Protections Are Sufficient
The FMCSA already has a coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers. Adding further regulatory burdens under the guise of transparency does not provide any additional value to carriers or the broader transportation industry.
 
In conclusion, the proposed expansion of 371.3 is unnecessary, costly, and inconsistent with the needs of the modern transportation marketplace. The FMCSA should prioritize addressing freight fraud and ensuring highway safety rather than pursuing this unwarranted rulemaking. I urge the agency to withdraw this proposal and focus on regulations that truly benefit the industry.
 
Thank you for considering my comments.

Dear FMCSA,

I am writing to express my concerns regarding the proposed amendments to property broker regulations as outlined in Docket No. FMCSA-2024-27115. While the intent of enhancing transparency is appreciated, I believe the changes may result in unintended consequences that could negatively impact the trucking and logistics industry. My key concerns are as follows:

Administrative Burden: The proposed regulations impose additional reporting requirements on brokers, leading to significant administrative challenges. This would divert resources away from core operational activities, potentially reducing service efficiency.

Confidentiality Issues: Requiring detailed financial transaction disclosures poses a risk to the confidentiality of proprietary business information. This could expose sensitive data to competitors and undermine the competitive advantage of brokers and carriers.

Market Dynamics: Altering the balance of information between brokers and carriers could disrupt existing market dynamics. These changes may lead to reduced flexibility in negotiations and potential increases in transportation costs.

Implementation Costs: Compliance with the proposed regulations will likely require investments in training, system upgrades, and other operational changes. These costs may disproportionately burden small and medium-sized businesses, potentially leading to market consolidation and reduced competition.

Impact on Broker-Carrier Relationships: The transparency measures could strain relationships between brokers and carriers. Increased scrutiny and mandatory disclosures may erode trust and hinder collaborative partnerships, creating a more transactional industry environment.

While I support measures that improve industry practices, I urge FMCSA to carefully evaluate the potential drawbacks of these proposed changes. Balancing transparency with practicality is essential to avoid unintended disruptions to the trucking and logistics sector.

Thank you for considering these concerns.

This regulation is not only an affront to American values but also a direct attack on the principles of capitalism that have driven our nation's success. The rule's imposition of mandatory transparency for freight brokers undermines the core tenets of a free-market economy and represents a dangerous overreach of government authority. Here are several reasons why this rule must be reconsidered:

1. Violation of Free Market Principles: At the heart of American enterprise is the belief in a free market where competition drives innovation, efficiency, and growth. Requiring brokers to disclose detailed transaction records for every shipment undermines this principle by exposing proprietary business information. This transparency is not beneficial to a competitive market; rather, it stifles the entrepreneurial spirit and discourages brokers from innovating and seeking new ways to add value.

2. Anti-Capitalism: The notion that every business must reveal its earnings on each transaction is antithetical to the very foundation of capitalism. In a capitalist economy, businesses succeed by differentiating themselves, securing advantageous deals, and maintaining strategic advantages. Forcing brokers to reveal their margins and operational specifics erodes these advantages, effectively penalizing those who have worked hard to build efficient and profitable operations. This level of enforced transparency will lead to price-fixing and reduced competition, which is detrimental to the market as a whole.

3. Disproportionate Impact on Small Businesses: Small and mid-sized brokerage firms, which are the backbone of our logistics industry, will be disproportionately affected by these onerous requirements. These businesses often lack the resources to comply with complex regulatory mandates, leading to potential closures and a reduction in market diversity.

4. Undermining Economic Freedom: Economic freedom is a cornerstone of the American dream. Mandating transparency requirements infringes upon the right of businesses to protect their proprietary information and operate autonomously. Such mandates are an attack on the economic liberties that are essential to our nation's prosperity.

5. Legal Concerns: The recent U.S. Supreme Court decision to overrule the Chevron deference brings significant legal concerns to this rulemaking. Without the Chevron deference, the legal justification for such extensive regulatory intervention by the FMCSA is highly questionable and likely to face intense judicial scrutiny.

6. The FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers. This existing regulation adequately addresses concerns regarding the treatment of motor carriers without imposing additional burdensome transparency requirements. Introducing the new rule is not only redundant but also an unnecessary complication that would harm the industry's efficiency.

7. Potential Violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016: The proposed transparency rule poses potential violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, which protects critical business information, including pricing and other confidential data. Mandating the disclosure of such sensitive information exposes businesses to significant risks, undermines their competitive position, and violates federal protections designed to safeguard trade secrets.

8. Costly Implementation of Electronic Submission: The only safe way to achieve electronic submission safely would be through an EDI or API integration, at the cost of roughly $2,500 - $10,000 per integration. Motor carriers would bear the cost of this as well, adding an unfair financial burden on an already challenging industry landscape.

9. Lack of Consumer Complaints During COVID-19 Pandemic: During the COVID-19 pandemic, when this issue was at its height, there were no complaints in the consumer complaint database. This fact demonstrates that the proposed transparency requirements are addressing a problem that does not exist, further highlighting the unnecessary nature of this rule.

10. Contradiction with ICC Intent: The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) made it clear in its intent of this regulation, stating, “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.” This proposed rule directly contradicts the ICC's intent by imposing additional restrictions that hinder the free market.

While the intention behind increasing transparency in freight rates is commendable, I believe this rule could have unintended consequences that may disrupt the current freight market dynamics and negatively impact small carriers and owner-operators.

Firstly, mandating the disclosure of freight rates could lead to a decrease in competition. If proprietary pricing strategies are made public, larger carriers may exploit this information to undercut smaller competitors, ultimately harming the very businesses the FMCSA aims to support. The freight industry thrives on the ability to negotiate rates based on market conditions, relationships, and individual business strategies. A blanket disclosure could stifle this essential flexibility.

Moreover, the implementation of this rule could increase the administrative burden on small carriers. The requirement to regularly collect and report rate data will necessitate additional resources, diverting focus from core business operations. This could disproportionately affect small businesses, which may lack the infrastructure to comply with these new regulations.

Additionally, there is a concern regarding the potential for misuse of publicly available rate information. Transparency can lead to price-fixing or collusion among carriers, undermining the principles of fair competition. Instead of fostering a more equitable marketplace, this rule could inadvertently create an environment where unethical practices thrive.

In conclusion, while I understand the FMCSA's goal to improve transparency in freight rates, I urge you to reconsider the implications of this proposed rule. A more balanced approach that considers the diverse needs of all stakeholders in the freight industry is essential.

On that of that, here are other reasons this should be opposed:

Expansion of 371.3 is based on an outdated, burdensome and government overreach.
Formalized in 1980 when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, not how the market currently operates.

The intent was to have financial transparency between all parties to a brokered load to ensure the illegal practice of freight rebating was not occurring, where a broker and shipper were common owners.

The current marketplace involves two separate business transactions between the shipper and broker and the broker and carrier.

The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) made it clear in its intent of this regulation, “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace."

During the COVID-19 pandemic, when this issue was at its height, there were no complaints in the consumer complaint database. 

This is a solution in search of a problem. 
The transportation marketplace is already very transparent with loadboards and other technology pricing tools. 

The only safe way to achieve electronic submission safely would be through an EDI or API integration, at the cost of roughly $2,500 - $10,000 per integration. Motor carriers would bear the cost of this as well.
 
Possible violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 which includes pricing and business information. 

FMCSA should focus on highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic that is costing the supply-chain over $1 billion annually. 

The FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers.

The U.S. Supreme Court overruled the Chevron deference decision, which brings in legal concerns of this rulemaking.

Brokers act as middlemen, facilitating the negotiation of freight rates between shippers and carriers. There is little visibility into how brokers set their fees or how much they are marking up freight costs. This lack of transparency leaves carriers, particularly smaller or independent operators, at a disadvantage when negotiating rates.

Carriers often find themselves in the dark about how brokers arrive at the rates they offer. In some cases, brokers are able to charge inflated fees while offering the carrier a much lower payout for the same load, effectively squeezing the profit margins of the trucker. The widespread use of tracking apps and ELDs has given brokers access to real-time data on routes, traffic, and load availability, but this has also led to some brokers using this information to pressure carriers into accepting lower-paying loads.

The absence of clear breakdowns for how freight rates are determined or how brokers set their commission has made the industry ripe for exploitation. Without regulatory oversight, brokers have the power to set terms that may not reflect the true value of the service, leading to potential abuses.

The trucking industry has long been criticized for its fragmented structure, with brokers often controlling much of the pricing power. This power imbalance has made it difficult for smaller carriers and independent owner-operators to negotiate fair terms. In the absence of proper regulation, some brokers have been known to engage in predatory pricing practices, using their market position to extract excessive profits at the expense of truckers.

Regulation is needed to ensure brokers operate fairly and transparently. Key areas where regulation could help include:

Brokers should be required to clearly disclose their commission structure, allowing both shippers and carriers to understand the true cost of the transaction. This could prevent brokers from inflating prices without offering value or transparent services.

Regulators could enforce guidelines to prevent brokers from setting rates that are too low, ensuring that drivers are compensated fairly for their work. This would protect smaller carriers from being pushed out of the market by large brokers who leverage their size to dictate terms.

Without adequate oversight, brokers have been known to engage in fraudulent practices, such as failing to pay carriers for services rendered or misrepresenting load availability. Clear regulations would help ensure that brokers fulfill their contractual obligations and maintain integrity in their dealings with carriers.

Brokers have a direct impact on the income of drivers. When brokers negotiate freight rates, they ultimately decide how much drivers get paid for their services. In many cases, brokers have the leverage to push down rates, sometimes to the point where drivers earn insufficient compensation for the work involved. This has contributed to a growing dissatisfaction among truckers and an increase in turnover rates in the industry.

With the increasing use of tracking apps and ELDs, brokers now have unprecedented access to data that gives them an advantage in price negotiations. However, this information is often used to the detriment of carriers, who may feel pressured into accepting lower-paying loads because brokers can easily compare rates and negotiate based on real-time data. The digital tools intended to increase efficiency are, in some cases, further consolidating power in the hands of brokers, leaving little room for small carriers to thrive.

Similar to how carriers are regulated by the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA), brokers should be subject to similar oversight. Requiring brokers to obtain licenses and adhere to specific industry standards would promote greater accountability.

Brokers should be required to provide detailed reports on their fees, rates, and payment timelines, making it easier for carriers to spot unfair practices and for regulators to intervene when necessary.

By standardizing the contracts between brokers, shippers, and carriers, there could be more consistency and fairness in how freight agreements are structured, ensuring that carriers are not subjected to predatory contract terms.

The trucking industry, vital to the movement of goods across the country, is at a crossroads. To foster a more fair and equitable environment for all stakeholders, there must be greater transparency and regulation of freight brokers. While technology has the potential to streamline operations and enhance safety, it must not come at the cost of fair pricing, driver welfare, or ethical practices. Only through better regulation and a commitment to transparency can we ensure the long-term stability and integrity of the trucking industry.

There are several concerns with this proposal, not to mention it seems unrelated to the FMCSA's primary mission of ensuring safety standards. Its involvement in this matter appears unwarranted and outside the scope of its intended purpose.

The proposal raises significant concerns about the confidentiality of economic relationships between shippers, brokers, and carriers. 

Key issues I see include:
• Back-Soliciting Risk: Brokers provide value to customers (shippers), but this proposal could enable back-soliciting, undermining those relationships.
• Collusion Potential: It might encourage customers to collaborate with carriers to uncover broker markups, pressuring brokers to reduce their margins when assigning loads.
• Administrative and Financial Burden: Brokers could face substantial time and cost burdens gathering requested data within 48 hours. Additionally, carriers might exploit this by holding freight hostage and demanding a share of the broker’s margin to deliver loads, amounting to extortion.
• Contractual Conflicts: The proposal prohibits brokers from contractually restricting carriers from requesting data, yet some customer agreements explicitly prohibit such disclosures.
• Impact on Small Carriers: Smaller carriers often rely on brokers for quick payments and cash flow, a service shippers typically do not provide. This change could drive many small carriers out of business.
I am confident there are additional unintended consequences not listed above, a major one being that this overarching transparency idea could easily spill into other industries...it's better option would be to determine a way to safely vet brokers and eliminate those bad actors, then allow the good ones to continue to provide the much needed value for shippers.

This proposal introduces risks and challenges that could destabilize industry dynamics without addressing safety—the FMCSAs presumed focus.

Brokers act as middlemen, facilitating the negotiation of freight rates between shippers and carriers. There is little visibility into how brokers set their fees or how much they are marking up freight costs. This lack of transparency leaves carriers, particularly smaller or independent operators, at a disadvantage when negotiating rates.

Carriers often find themselves in the dark about how brokers arrive at the rates they offer. In some cases, brokers are able to charge inflated fees for goods moving along a specific route, while offering the carrier a much lower payout for the same load. This practice severely limits the profit margin for the carrier. The widespread use of tracking apps and ELDs has given brokers access to real-time data on routes, traffic, and load availability, but this has also led to some brokers using this information to pressure carriers into accepting lower-paying loads or labeling the load a "Backhaul Load" to an area with higher paying loads to justify the lower rate. This practice of labeling loads as a backhaul to justify a lesser rate is far too common as can be seen by looking at rates to specific areas such as Floria. Rates for goods to Florida have historically been good, but rates leaving the state have always been very low. The brokers justification of the rate disparity is that the carrier got paid well in one direction, and the lower rate evens the gross income to a normal amount. Transparency in rates would eliminate this practice and allow carriers to move freight in a more profitable lane if so chosen, without the broker being able to skim the "excess" profits off. 

The absence of clear breakdowns for how freight rates are determined or how brokers set their commission has made the industry ripe for exploitation. Without regulatory oversight, brokers have the power to set terms that may not reflect the true value of the service, leading to potential abuses.

The trucking industry has long been criticized for its fragmented structure, with brokers often controlling much of the pricing power. This power imbalance has made it difficult for smaller carriers and independent owner-operators to negotiate fair terms. In the absence of proper regulation, some brokers have been known to engage in predatory pricing practices, using their market position to extract excessive profits at the expense of truckers.

Regulation is needed to ensure brokers operate fairly and transparently. Key areas where regulation could help include:

Brokers should be required to clearly disclose their commission structure, allowing both shippers and carriers to understand the true cost of the transaction. This could prevent brokers from inflating prices without offering value or transparent services.

Regulators could enforce guidelines to prevent brokers from setting rates that are too low, ensuring that drivers are compensated fairly for their work. This would protect smaller carriers from being pushed out of the market by large brokers who leverage their size to dictate terms.

Without adequate oversight, brokers have been known to engage in fraudulent practices, such as failing to pay carriers for services rendered or misrepresenting load availability. Clear regulations would help ensure that brokers fulfill their contractual obligations and maintain integrity in their dealings with carriers.

Brokers have a direct impact on the income of drivers. When brokers negotiate freight rates, they ultimately decide how much drivers get paid for their services. In many cases, brokers have the leverage to push down rates, sometimes to the point where drivers earn insufficient compensation for the work involved. This has contributed to a growing dissatisfaction among truckers and an increase in turnover rates in the industry.

With the increasing use of tracking apps and ELDs, brokers now have unprecedented access to data that gives them an advantage in price negotiations. However, this information is often used to the detriment of carriers, who may feel pressured into accepting lower-paying loads because brokers can easily compare rates and negotiate based on real-time data. The digital tools intended to increase efficiency are, in some cases, further consolidating power in the hands of brokers, leaving little room for small carriers to thrive.

Similar to how carriers are regulated by the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA), brokers should be subject to similar oversight. Requiring brokers to obtain licenses and adhere to specific industry standards would promote greater accountability.

By standardizing the contracts between brokers, shippers, and carriers, there could be more consistency and fairness in how freight agreements are structured, ensuring that carriers are not subjected to predatory contract terms.

Proposed Rulemaking on Broker Transparency (FMCSA-2023-0257)

To: The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA)

As a concerned stakeholder in the transportation and freight brokerage industry, I write to express my strong opposition to the proposed amendments to the property broker rules under 49 CFR Part 371, specifically the FMCSA’s Notice of Proposed Rulemaking (FMCSA-2023-0257). While the intent to increase transparency and protect motor carriers and shippers is acknowledged, the proposed rule will likely cause significant harm to the industry, create unnecessary regulatory burdens, and undermine the principles of free enterprise and contractual freedom.

1. Overregulation and Increased Administrative Burden
The proposal to require brokers to keep electronic records and provide transaction details within 48 hours of a request, while well-intentioned, imposes an undue administrative burden on an already heavily regulated industry. Many brokers, especially small and medium-sized businesses, will struggle to implement and maintain the electronic systems required for compliance. The cost of transitioning to digital systems, training employees, and ensuring compliance will disproportionately affect small businesses, which are already operating under narrow profit margins.

Rather than fostering transparency, the requirement could reduce the efficiency of brokers, forcing them to divert significant resources from business growth and customer service to meet new regulatory demands. This could stifle competition, disproportionately disadvantaging small brokers who are unable to absorb these costs.

2. Interference with Private Contractual Relations
One of the most concerning aspects of this proposal is the explicit prohibition on brokers including clauses that waive a motor carrier’s right to access transaction records. This intervention into the private contract negotiation process represents an overreach of government authority and an unwarranted infringement on the autonomy of private parties to negotiate the terms of their business relationships.

Brokers, shippers, and motor carriers should have the freedom to negotiate contracts that best suit their operational needs and risk tolerances. The proposed rule risks undermining the freedom of contract by imposing one-size-fits-all terms on industry participants, despite the diversity of business models and operational capacities within the industry. The FMCSA’s role should be to ensure fair and efficient market conditions, not to micromanage individual business transactions.

3. Confidentiality Concerns and Risk to Business Secrets
The requirement for brokers to disclose transaction details, including commission rates and payments, threatens the confidentiality of proprietary business practices. Brokers often negotiate sensitive terms with shippers and motor carriers that are vital to their competitive advantage. Requiring the disclosure of these details could compromise competitive business strategies, forcing brokers to disclose commercially sensitive information to their competitors or to the public.

In an industry that thrives on efficiency and cost control, such a regulation could encourage a race to the bottom, where the focus shifts from building relationships and optimizing operations to satisfying regulatory compliance, ultimately harming businesses and consumers alike. Brokers will be less inclined to take on high-risk transactions if their competitive position is jeopardized by mandated disclosure of every financial detail.

4. Disruption of Market Efficiency and Unintended Consequences
While transparency is an admirable goal, the unintended consequences of this proposal are far-reaching. Requiring brokers to share detailed financial records risks disrupting the delicate balance of the transportation market. The competitive dynamics of pricing and service offerings could be severely affected, leading to higher operating costs and inefficiencies.

Furthermore, the push for increased transparency may cause brokers to limit their services or become more selective in the contracts they accept, potentially reducing the overall availability of freight options for motor carriers. This could lead to higher rates for shippers, reduced access to capacity for motor carriers, and a less competitive environment overall.

5. Lack of Adequate Consideration of Industry Concerns
The FMCSA has failed to sufficiently consider the real-world implications of its proposed rule. Numerous stakeholders, including the Transportation Intermediaries Association (TIA), have voiced concerns that these regulations will harm small and mid-sized brokers, increase costs for shippers and motor carriers, and undermine the flexibility of the marketplace.

While the FMCSA’s intentions may be grounded in the desire for fairness, the proposed rule does not adequately address the complexities of the freight brokerage industry.

Transparency in brokered property transactions refers to the clear, honest, and open disclosure of all relevant information by the broker to both parties (buyer and seller). It ensures that all participants in the transaction are fully informed about key aspects of the deal, fostering trust and avoiding disputes. Below are some key areas where transparency is essential:

1. Fees and Commissions

 • Brokers must clearly disclose their fees or commission structure upfront.
 • Any additional costs or payments (e.g., referral fees or dual agency fees) should be communicated.

2. Property Details

 • All known details about the property, including defects, legal disputes, zoning restrictions, or encumbrances, must be disclosed.
 • Accurate representation of property size, condition, and amenities is crucial.

3. Conflict of Interest

 • Brokers must disclose if they are representing both the buyer and seller (dual agency) or have any personal interest in the property.
 • Transparency about relationships with third parties, such as inspectors or lenders, is essential.

4. Offers and Negotiations

 • Brokers should present all offers to the seller accurately and in a timely manner.
 • Transparency about how offers are evaluated and whether there are competing offers is key to fair dealings.

5. Regulatory Compliance

 • Adherence to local, state, and national real estate regulations ensures the transaction remains lawful.
 • Disclosures required by law (e.g., lead-based paint in older homes) must be communicated clearly.

Why Transparency Matters

 • Builds Trust: Buyers and sellers are more likely to rely on brokers who are open and honest.
 • Reduces Risks: Prevents legal disputes and ensures smoother transactions.
 • Protects Reputation: Brokers who practice transparency are more likely to gain repeat business and referrals.

As an owner-operator, I strongly support broker rate transparency as a critical measure to ensure fairness and accountability across the supply chain. Transparency not only promotes equity but also enhances trust and collaboration among all parties—brokers, carriers, and shippers.
Transparency Enhances Market Equity
CFR 371.3 was established to give carriers insight into the financial structure of loads, enabling informed decisions. Transparency does not disrupt the market but strengthens it by fostering trust, deterring fraud, and reducing practices like double brokering and the use of ghost carriers.
Records Requirement Must Be Automatic
Brokers should be required to automatically supply records related to a completed load upon receipt of the Bill of Lading (BOL) or Proof of Delivery (POD). Carriers already provide these records electronically under strict time constraints. Requiring brokers to do the same is neither burdensome nor costly, given the availability of advanced technology. Automatic compliance ensures transparency without unnecessary delays or hurdles.
Prohibiting Waivers is Essential
Brokers must not be allowed to waive their regulatory duties. Allowing such waivers constitutes an evasion of regulation, which violates the Sherman Antitrust Act. Congress has permitted shippers and carriers to waive certain obligations, but it has not authorized brokers to do so. FMCSA must make clear that brokers are intermediaries, not principals in the transaction, and their regulatory duties cannot be waived.
Transparency reforms that include automatic records requirements and the prohibition of waivers will create a more equitable and collaborative marketplace, protecting small carriers like me from exploitative practices.

As an owner-operator, I believe rate transparency is crucial for a fair and equitable marketplace. Transparency ensures that carriers—who face significant operational costs—are adequately compensated for their services.
Fair Competition Through Transparency
Transparency does not dictate broker earnings but ensures carriers have the information needed to make informed decisions. Brokers' claims of unsustainable margins are unfounded; reasonable markups will not be threatened by transparency but will instead encourage equitable competition.
Records Must Be Provided Automatically
Transparency reforms should mandate that brokers automatically supply all required records upon completion of a load. Carriers already provide BOLs and signed rate confirmations promptly, often under challenging conditions. It is only fair that brokers reciprocate by providing the financial records required under CFR 371.3 without requiring carriers to request them explicitly.
No Waivers Should Be Allowed
FMCSA must prohibit brokers from using waivers to evade their regulatory obligations. Such waivers are not authorized under law and violate principles of fair trade. Congress has made clear distinctions between the rights of shippers, carriers, and brokers, and FMCSA must enforce these distinctions to ensure compliance.
By implementing these measures, FMCSA can ensure fair competition, promote equity, and strengthen trust across the industry.

Dear FMCSA Rulemaking Team, 

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposed rulemaking titled “Transparency in Property Broker Transactions” (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257-0001). This rule represents unnecessary government overreach and fails to address the real challenges facing the transportation industry today. 

Key Concerns with the Proposed Rule: 
1. Outdated and Misaligned Framework: 
 - The expansion of 371.3 is rooted in a regulation formalized in 1980, a time when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers. The current marketplace operates fundamentally differently, with brokers facilitating two separate business transactions—one with the shipper and one with the carrier. 
 - The regulation's original intent was to prevent freight rebating, a practice no longer relevant under modern industry practices. 

2. Unnecessary and Burdensome Requirements: 
 - The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and technology tools providing real-time pricing visibility. 
 - Mandating electronic record submission through methods such as EDI or API integration would impose significant financial burdens, costing $2,500–$10,000 per integration—expenses likely to fall on motor carriers. 

3. Lack of Evidence for Regulation: 
 - During the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of heightened scrutiny, the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database recorded no complaints on this issue. This proposal addresses a non-existent problem. 

4. Legal and Confidentiality Concerns: 
 - The proposed rule risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, as it involves disclosing sensitive pricing and business information. 
 - Recent legal precedent, such as the Supreme Court’s decision to overrule the Chevron deference, raises questions about the FMCSA’s authority to enforce such a rule. 

5. Misplaced Priorities: 
 - Rather than pursuing unnecessary expansions of outdated regulations, the FMCSA should focus on critical issues impacting our industry, such as improving highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually. 

Conclusion: 
The FMCSA’s proposal is a solution in search of a problem. It imposes costly and unnecessary burdens on brokers and carriers while ignoring the true challenges facing the transportation industry. I urge the FMCSA to withdraw this proposed rule and redirect its efforts toward initiatives that enhance safety and combat fraud. 

Thank you for considering my comments. I trust the FMCSA will prioritize policies that truly support the transportation industry's success and sustainability. 

Sincerely, 
Curtis Hazel

 Proper enforcement of rules deters unethical practices and enhances safety. The FMCSA must enforce regulations to protect the supply chain's integrity and the trucking industry's operational safety. The current process & lack of enforcement has led to high levels of fraud, predatory practices, and all stakeholders involved suffering. The industry is being exploited and is vulnerable as a result. 

The FMCSA has provided no way to report general freight brokers non-compliance. As a result the integrity & safety of the general freight industry is severely compromised. 

As the industry has evolved, so has the increase of identity theft, fraud, and market manipulation. This necessitates the dire need for regulation & oversight of load boards requirements, record keeping, privacy policies, data sharing, security, rate reporting, and data collection. At this time, there is zero oversight & enforcement upon general freight brokers. 

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3. We respectfully request that they do so at this time. 

We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,
