These issues directly impact highway safety and the integrity of our nation's transportation infrastructure.

 Proper enforcement of rules deters unethical practices and enhances safety. The FMCSA must enforce regulations to protect the supply chain's integrity and the trucking industry's operational safety. The current process & lack of enforcement has led to high levels of fraud, predatory practices, and all stakeholders involved suffering. The industry is being exploited and is vulnerable as a result. 

The FMCSA has provided no way to report general freight brokers non-compliance. As a result the integrity & safety of the general freight industry is severely compromised. 

As the industry has evolved, so has the increase of identity theft, fraud, and market manipulation. This necessitates the dire need for regulation & oversight of load boards requirements, record keeping, privacy policies, data sharing, security, rate reporting, and data collection. At this time, there is zero oversight & enforcement upon general freight brokers. 

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3. We respectfully request that they do so at this time. 

We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,

When brokers operate without proper oversight, it affects carriers' ability to maintain safe equipment, afford required insurance policies, and comply with licensing and permit requirements. These issues directly impact highway safety and the integrity of our nation's transportation infrastructure.

 Proper enforcement of rules deters unethical practices and enhances safety. The FMCSA must enforce regulations to protect the supply chain's integrity and the trucking industry's operational safety. The current process & lack of enforcement has led to high levels of fraud, predatory practices, and all stakeholders involved suffering. The industry is being exploited and is vulnerable as a result. 

The FMCSA has provided no way to report general freight brokers non-compliance. As a result the integrity & safety of the general freight industry is severely compromised. 

As the industry has evolved, so has the increase of identity theft, fraud, and market manipulation. This necessitates the dire need for regulation & oversight of load boards requirements, record keeping, privacy policies, data sharing, security, rate reporting, and data collection. At this time, there is zero oversight & enforcement upon general freight brokers. 

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3. We respectfully request that they do so at this time. 

We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,

When brokers operate without proper oversight, it affects carriers' ability to maintain safe equipment, afford required insurance policies, and comply with licensing and permit requirements. These issues directly impact highway safety and the integrity of our nation's transportation infrastructure.

 Proper enforcement of rules deters unethical practices and enhances safety. The FMCSA must enforce regulations to protect the supply chain's integrity and the trucking industry's operational safety. The current process & lack of enforcement has led to high levels of fraud, predatory practices, and all stakeholders involved suffering. The industry is being exploited and is vulnerable as a result. 

The FMCSA has provided no way to report general freight brokers non-compliance. As a result the integrity & safety of the general freight industry is severely compromised. 

As the industry has evolved, so has the increase of identity theft, fraud, and market manipulation. This necessitates the dire need for regulation & oversight of load boards requirements, record keeping, privacy policies, data sharing, security, rate reporting, and data collection. At this time, there is zero oversight & enforcement upon general freight brokers. 

While we support strengthening the agency's teeth to better enforce the law and rules and regulations against brokers, we wish to point out that FMCSA already has enough teeth to immediately and diligently take a bite out of broker abuse with regards to CFR 371.3. We respectfully request that they do so at this time. 

We urge Congress to support the FMCSA in these efforts and, if necessary, provide additional legislative clarity to empower the agency to carry out its duties effectively in these specific issues. Your attention to this matter is crucial in upholding the integrity and safety of our nation’s transportation system. 

Respectfully,

As an owner-operator of both truck and trailer. Operating under leased authority, with total autonomy from the carrier. I haul both direct customer freight (my customers) and brokered freight. I don't care what the broker makes off a load, as long as I'm ok with the offered rate. 
That all said, the passage of this rule will ultimately end up with price-fixing and stifle the competitive nature of American business.

We, the transportation company absolutely support this demand. In a democratic world, there can be no financial secrets. If brokers want to bargain, then let them go to the flee market and bargain. Why do carriers have to live by the principle of luck or bad luck. Transportation is a clear business with specific numbers of income and expenses. If the brokers do not want to be clear, then maybe it is worth to remove this structure as a spacer between clients and the carriers.

FMCSA proposed rule for broker record keeping is a nice place to start. Load-brokering is a crooked as hell industry and it needs to be as transparent as stock brokering: YES IM ENTITLED TO SEE THE UNDERLYING PRICE. :-)
I am opposed
I am opposed.

This is absolutely outrageous. It takes months or years of effort for a broker to find a customer- often working nights and weekends and 60-80+ hour weeks to maintain the customer, taking on enormous amounts of stress trying to keep a customer- with most of the stressors out of our control: ie drivers not doing their job in a timely manner or equipment breakdowns. We lose money on tons of loads and make money on others. We scrape by and scrounge and swallow our pride on a daily basis to make ends meet. At any moment, we could lose a customer that we spend tens of thousands of dollars in time to acquire. 

We spend thousands on software, insurance (of which we take on a ton of risk for every shipment), equipment, office space etc.

In 17 years I’ve only been able to take two actual 5 day vacations- and I still worked somewhat during this time.

There is absolutely no excuse or reason for carriers to have access to our profits or our customers information. Absolutely zero. 

I have a small veteran owned logistics agency- and we barely squeak by month after month. Now you want a carrier who has done none of the work to acquire and maintain my customer, to have access to my customer to undercut me???

This is about the most unamerican thing I’ve seen waft from the government in a long time. Shame on you.

No other industries in this country are treated with such complete disregard as we are with this. This is insane, and everyone involved should be exceptionally ashamed of yourselves.

Dear FMCSA Rulemaking Team, 
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposed rulemaking titled “Transparency in Property Broker Transactions” (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257-0001). This rule represents unnecessary government overreach and fails to address the real challenges facing the transportation industry today. 
 
Key Concerns with the Proposed Rule: 
 
1. Outdated and Misaligned Framework:
• The expansion of C.F.R 371.3 in the proposed manner, a regulation from a time when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, fails to take account that the current marketplace operates fundamentally differently, with brokers facilitating two separate business transactions—one with the shipper and one with the carrier.
• The regulation's original intent was to prevent freight rebating, a practice no longer relevant under modern industry practices.
2. Unnecessary and Burdensome Requirements:
• The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and technology tools providing real-time pricing visibility.
• Mandating electronic record submission through methods such as EDI or API integration would impose significant financial burdens, costing $2,500–$10,000 per integration—expenses likely to fall on motor carriers.
3. Lack of Evidence for Regulation:
• During the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of heightened scrutiny, the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database recorded no complaints on this issue. This proposal addresses a non-existent problem.
4. Legal and Confidentiality Concerns:
• The proposed rule risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, as it involves disclosing sensitive pricing and business information.
• Recent legal precedent, including the Supreme Court’s decision to reverse the Chevron case, raises questions about the FMCSA’s authority to enforce such a rule.
5. Misplaced Priorities:
• Rather than pursuing unnecessary expansions of outdated regulations, the FMCSA should focus on critical issues impacting our industry, such as improving highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually.
Conclusion: 
 
The FMCSA’s proposal is a solution in search of a problem. It imposes costly and unnecessary burdens on brokers and carriers while ignoring the true challenges facing the transportation industry. I urge the FMCSA to withdraw this proposed rule and redirect its efforts toward initiatives that enhance safety and combat fraud. 
 
Thank you for considering my comments. I trust the FMCSA will prioritize policies that truly support the transportation industry's success and sustainability. 
 
Sincerely, 
Natasha Dowling

I am writing to oppose the recently proposed rulemaking for Transparency in Property Broker Transactions. Our efforts should be focused on fraud prevention in transportation instead of needless rules that no longer have application in the marketplace. In addition to being particularly burdensome from an administrative perspective, this rulemaking would also put a broker in the position of being in violation of the terms of many, if not most, shipper / broker transportation agreements. Putting this rule in place would provide fodder for attorneys to bring actions that at the end of the day,,,, would make no difference and would not level the playing field in any shape form or fashion.

Dear FMCSA Rulemaking Team,

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposed rulemaking titled “Transparency in Property Broker Transactions” (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257-0001). This proposal represents unnecessary government overreach and fails to address the critical challenges currently facing the transportation industry.

Key Concerns with the Proposed Rule

1. Outdated and Misaligned Framework

The proposed expansion of C.F.R. 371.3 stems from an era when brokers operated as commissioned sales agents for motor carriers. Today’s marketplace functions fundamentally differently, with brokers facilitating two distinct business transactions: one with the shipper and another with the carrier.
The regulation’s original intent—preventing freight rebating—is no longer relevant in modern industry practices.

2. Unnecessary and Burdensome Requirements

The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and technology tools providing real-time pricing visibility.
Mandating electronic record submissions through EDI or API integrations would impose significant financial burdens, costing $2,500–$10,000 per integration. These costs would disproportionately affect small carriers.

3. Lack of Evidence for Regulation

During the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of intense scrutiny for broker practices, the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database recorded no complaints on this issue. The proposed rule addresses a problem that simply does not exist.

4. Legal and Confidentiality Concerns

The proposed rule risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 by requiring the disclosure of sensitive pricing and business information.
Recent legal precedents, including the Supreme Court’s reversal of the Chevron case, raise questions about the FMCSA’s authority to enforce this rule.

5. Misplaced Priorities

Instead of expanding outdated regulations, the FMCSA should focus on addressing critical issues such as:

Improving highway safety.

Combating freight fraud, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually.

Conclusion
This proposal is a solution in search of a problem. It imposes unnecessary financial and operational burdens on brokers and carriers, distracting from the real challenges confronting the transportation industry. I urge the FMCSA to withdraw this proposed rule and refocus its efforts on initiatives that enhance safety, reduce fraud, and genuinely support the success and sustainability of the transportation sector.

Thank you for considering my comments. I trust the FMCSA will prioritize policies that reflect the realities of today’s industry and foster its continued growth.

Sincerely,
Robert Page

Dear FMCSA Rulemaking Team,

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposed rulemaking titled “Transparency in Property Broker Transactions” (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257-0001). This proposal represents excessive government overreach and fails to address the actual challenges faced by the transportation industry today.

Key Issues with the Proposed Rule

1. Outdated and Misaligned Framework

The proposed expansion of C.F.R. 371.3 is based on a regulatory framework created when brokers acted as commissioned sales agents for motor carriers. Today’s marketplace is vastly different, with brokers managing two independent transactions—one with the shipper and another with the carrier.
The original intent of these regulations, primarily to prevent freight rebating, is irrelevant in the context of modern industry practices.

2. Burdensome and Unnecessary Requirements

The transportation industry is already highly transparent, with load boards and technology platforms offering real-time pricing visibility to carriers and shippers.
Requiring electronic submissions through methods like EDI or API integration would impose significant financial burdens on brokers and carriers, with costs ranging from $2,500 to $10,000 per integration—expenses that smaller businesses can ill afford.

3. Lack of Supporting Evidence

During the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of heightened scrutiny, the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database did not record any complaints relevant to the issues this rule seeks to address. This proposal targets a problem that does not exist.

4. Legal and Confidentiality Concerns

Requiring the disclosure of sensitive pricing and business terms risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016.
Recent legal precedents, such as the Supreme Court’s decision to reverse the Chevron case, also cast doubt on the FMCSA’s authority to enforce such a rule.

5. Misplaced Priorities

The FMCSA should be addressing urgent challenges facing the transportation sector, such as:

Improving highway safety.

Tackling the ongoing freight fraud crisis, which costs the industry over $1 billion annually.

Developing solutions to support supply chain resilience and efficiency.

Conclusion
The FMCSA’s proposal is unnecessary, burdensome, and misaligned with the realities of today’s transportation industry. It creates significant costs for brokers and carriers while ignoring the pressing issues affecting the industry. I strongly urge the FMCSA to withdraw this rule and instead focus on initiatives that enhance safety, reduce fraud, and promote the growth and sustainability of the transportation sector.

Thank you for considering these comments. I trust the FMCSA will prioritize meaningful policies that align with the needs of the industry and the broader economy.

Sincerely,
Tiro Freight LLC

The expansion of C.F.R 371.3 in the proposed manner, a regulation from a time when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, fails to take account that the current marketplace operates fundamentally differently, with brokers facilitating two separate business transactions—one with the shipper and one with the carrier.
The regulation's original intent was to prevent freight rebating, a practice no longer relevant under modern industry practices.

2. Unnecessary and Burdensome Requirements:

The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and technology tools providing real-time pricing visibility.
Mandating electronic record submission through methods such as EDI or API integration would impose significant financial burdens, costing $2,500–$10,000 per integration—expenses likely to fall on motor carriers.

3. Lack of Evidence for Regulation:

During the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of heightened scrutiny, the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database recorded no complaints on this issue. This proposal addresses a non-existent problem.

4. Legal and Confidentiality Concerns:

The proposed rule risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, as it involves disclosing sensitive pricing and business information.
Recent legal precedent, including the Supreme Court’s decision to reverse the Chevron case, raises questions about the FMCSA’s authority to enforce such a rule.

5. Misplaced Priorities:

Rather than pursuing unnecessary expansions of outdated regulations, the FMCSA should focus on critical issues impacting our industry, such as improving highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually.
Conclusion: 

 The FMCSA’s proposal is a solution in search of a problem. It imposes costly and unnecessary burdens on brokers and carriers while ignoring the true challenges facing the transportation industry. I urge the FMCSA to withdraw this proposed rule and redirect its efforts toward initiatives that enhance safety and combat fraud. 

 
Thank you for considering my comments. I trust the FMCSA will prioritize policies that truly support the transportation industry's success and sustainability. 

 
Sincerely, 

Chance Edward Coursey

I am in strong opposition to the FMCSA’s proposed rulemaking regarding the expansion of 371.3. This proposed rule represents unnecessary government overreach, relies on outdated concepts, and would impose unbearable burdens on the transportation industry and STILL would not be addressing any demonstrated problem. Below are some reasons for my opposition:
 
Outdated Regulation Misaligned with Market Evolution
The expansion of 371.3 stems from an era (1980) when brokers functioned primarily as commissioned sales agents for motor carriers. The marketplace has since evolved significantly, and the current model involves two distinct business transactions: one between the shipper and broker, and another between the broker and carrier. Applying decades-old concepts to the modern transportation industry ignores this evolution and creates unnecessary complications.
 
Original Intent Was to Prevent Freight Rebating
The original intent of 371.3 was to ensure financial transparency between parties to prevent freight rebating, particularly when brokers and shippers had common ownership. The proposed rule disregards this historical context and applies transparency requirements that are no longer relevant to how the market operates today.
 
Contradiction with the ICC's Established Goals
The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) clearly stated that the purpose of regulating broker transactions was to “remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.” This proposed rule adds layers of unnecessary bureaucracy that undermine the efficient operation of the transportation marketplace.
 
No Evidence of Consumer Harm
During the COVID-19 pandemic—when concerns about freight pricing were at their peak—there were no complaints related to this issue in the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database. This rule is a solution in search of a problem.
 
Existing Transparency and Modern Technology
The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and various pricing tools providing carriers with substantial information. Additional transparency mandates are redundant and could infringe upon pricing and business information protected under the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016.
 
High Implementation Costs
Safely achieving electronic submission of the proposed documentation would require costly integrations through EDI or API, with expenses ranging from $2,500 to $10,000 per integration. These costs would disproportionately burden small motor carriers, further straining an industry already under pressure.
 
Misaligned Priorities and Legal Concerns
FMCSA’s primary focus should be on highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic, which is costing the supply chain over $1 billion annually. The proposed rule diverts resources and attention away from these critical issues. Additionally, the U.S. Supreme Court’s recent ruling overturning the Chevron deference raises significant legal concerns about the authority for this rulemaking.
 
Existing Protections Are Sufficient
The FMCSA already has a coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers. Adding further regulatory burdens under the guise of transparency does not provide any additional value to carriers or the broader transportation industry.
 
In conclusion, the proposed expansion of 371.3 is unnecessary, costly, and inconsistent with the needs of the modern transportation marketplace. The FMCSA should prioritize addressing freight fraud and ensuring highway safety rather than pursuing this unwarranted rulemaking. I urge the agency to withdraw this proposal and focus on regulations that truly benefit the industry.
 
Thank you for considering my comments.

My name is Jason Putney I represent a small motor carrier (18 trucks) called Downeast Shipping. 

TLDR: It is absurd to not legally have to let the service provider (carrier) know what is being paid for their services. 

Adding my small voice into the mix, I'd like to comment that this rulemaking has the potential to make a huge difference to the motor carrier community in general. For many of us, it's not about having an effect on the rates themselves. It's about the feeling of not knowing if it's a severely lopsided transaction every single time you haul a load. It's the feeling of being lied to every single day. Every motor carrier hears the lie all day every day - "sorry, that's all I've got in it".

Over time, this lie wears you down. Or maybe it's not a lie! But we'll never know! And that's the thing. Lies are an industry standard in logistics and to me this rule is at least a step in the right direction. I've become worse and worse of a negotiator over time in these situations because of this feeling of being lied to. The nature of the transaction typically goes like this...

Broker: "how much do you need to run it?"

Carrier: "this much"

Broker: "Sorry, this is all I have in it"

The carrier's answer to the brokers question "how much do you need to run it?" should only ever be "at least 90% of what the customer is paying"

Carriers should be getting AT LEAST 90% of what any customer is paying. For shouldering 98% of the liability and expenses associated with servicing the customer. For a middleman with SIGNIFICANTLY LESS liability and expense to extract 15%+ on any transaction is incredibly disheartening for the people actually involved in moving the shipments. This industry is severely lopsided with motor carriers shouldering an enormous amount of the burden of inefficiencies involved. 

The freight brokerage industry is unique in the business world. I haven't been able to find another industry where the middleman or person who works for commissions has full control of the money in the transaction, without having invested any of their own money into it, and can dole out the money in that transaction as they see fit. Every other industry that works for commissions typically has a set commission that is transparent to all parties. The "commissions" that freight brokers work for do not fit the definition of the word commission at all. It is absurd to not legally have to let the service provider (carrier) know what is being paid for their services. 

I would take this rule a step further and say that motor carriers deserve to know what customers are paying for their service every time! Not just at request, but as an industry standard. That level of transparency would lift the hearts and minds of every motor carrier out here. Knowing that the transaction is fair, knowing they are being paid a fair share of the total.

Brokers and insurance companies strip small owner-operators of their dignity. It is common that after returning home from 25 or more days on the road, there is no real rest (living at gas stations and always eating the same thing in a rush) because the income generated wasn’t enough to cover business and family expenses. As a result, drivers have to find “something” to do to meet financial obligations (Uber, Lyft, DoorDash, Instacart, construction, etc.).

On the other hand, road safety would improve if earnings were sufficient. Trucks would be in better condition, there would be no overspeeding to pick up another load, and so on. Safety is directly linked to having the financial resources to perform preventive maintenance instead of corrective repairs, and to the peace of mind of a driver who feels satisfied with their work and isn’t stressed over lack of money.

Additionally, the number of individuals transporting freight without proper authority or an apportioned plate should also be considered. Even if they are hauling under 26,000 pounds, they are conducting business and are constantly on the road with loads. This still represents a potential risk to other carriers.
The proposed regulation is overreach

Unscrupulous brokerage practices are rampant and have been for decades. They assume ZERO liability for carrier/broker agreements while placing 100% of the liability on the carrier. I have been in this industry for 4 decades, and watched it go from brick-and-mortar brokerage offices to all online business which is quite fine and acceptable...however the technology has allowed for hundreds of thousands of new, inexperienced brokerages to open for business without any depth of knowledge of how the trucking industry works as a whole. I have recently been a victim of a scam created by TQL (Total Quality Logistics) of Cincinatti, OH. back in October 2024. I could not upload the documents backing up my claim due to site restrictions...but I can certainly provide them if need be otherwise. I loaded a load at Minneapolis/St. Paul Airport on October 3rd this year with a confirmation and a pre-generated Bill of Lading provided by TQL. They added a second drop to the trip out of the blue as I was about to load to which I agreed to do, sent a new confirmation, but never sent a new (second) Bill of Lading specifying the second drop off. I agreed on the first drop off to Long Island, NY at the original rate of $4,200.00 for a 36,000 lb. piece of equipment, along with the connecting link that went with it. This equipment is known as a "pushback", and a "towbar", the rig used to push the aircraft back from the terminal gates to prepare for taxiing. The original plan all along was to drop these items off at Ronkonkoma, NY (Long Island.) The second (additional) drop would be up in Rhode Island. An additional $800.00 was agreed between me and TQL. I had the shipper sign the original BOL as seen on the document. I delivered the pushback rig to Long Island, NY on Monday 10-7-24, and delivered the towbar to the second drop off point in Rhode Island, both locations signed the BOL's and were happy. 3 days later I got a call from the TQL rep. that I had been communicating with, stating that I had delivered the load in REVERSE, and needed me to go fix it at my expense. I clearly stated to him that the original plan all along was to deliver that pushback to Long Island for that original rate agreed upon of $4,200.00, and that if for any reason they were to change it to be delivered to Rhode Island, (substantially farther northeast of Long Island,) that we would have had to renegotiate the rate given the extra mileage based on the weight and extra distance. They were adamant about having me correct this problem free of charge to switch the two items from LINY to RI. I explained to him very diplomatically that I would be glad to fix THEIR MISTAKE, or possibly the SHIPPERS mistake...for an additional $2,800.00 equating to two days of work to correct this issue. They declined my offer, and had another carrier supposedly fix the problem, and short-paid their invoice by $1,600.00. That resulted in me having to pay our factoring company the difference of $1,600.00. The bottom line is, TQL themselves had messed up, or it could also have been the shippers mistake, but neither one admitted to it and placed the blame on the carrier and had it cost the carrier out of pocket. I contacted OOIDA and discussed this in depth as I am explaining it here. They referred me to several attorneys that they have been working with to try and collect that $1,600.00. Several of the attorneys failed to reply. One actually did finally, and he explained that he has in the past won several cases against TQL for this type of foul play. However, since TQL had a team of lawyers dedicated to NOT have to pay a dime out and set a precedence of brokers admitting to guilt, they will spend $1,000.00 to not pay out $1.00. He also said since the county that they reside in has had a new wave of judges placed in offices, that they are (to be blunt) "bought" by TQL and since then he hasn't been very successful at winning any cases against them for bad business practices. He said in order for me to move forward with this case, he would require a $10,000.00 retainer and be prepared to have it cost me another $30,000.00-$40,000.00 to battle them. Obviously not going to happen...so I took it on the chin. The only recourse that I have on TQL is to go public with my story. I would also like to point out that there have been several ongoing class action suits against TQL for several years over many different issues they have created. So, to close this comment, I am just interested in making the public aware of how TQL operates, as well as so very many of these notorious brokers that are just out to make a buck and not be responsible for ONE thing. I am 100% for transparency, accountability, and the ability for carriers to go after a broker for wrong-doing as I am not capable of unfortunately due to the economics of this matter. Language needs to be added to these broker-carrier agreements as well, to limit the carrier's liability and throw some onto the brokers for their mishaps.

I am for CFR 371.3 for these reasons:
1. Fairer Market Practices:
 * Level Playing Field: By ensuring transparency, the regulation helps to level the playing field between carriers and brokers, preventing potential exploitation of smaller carriers.
 * Reduced Information Asymmetry: It reduces information asymmetry, enabling carriers to make more informed decisions about the rates they agree to.
2. Increased Trust and Collaboration:
 * Stronger Relationships: Transparent practices can foster stronger and more collaborative relationships between brokers and carriers.
 * Enhanced Trust: By providing clear information, brokers can build trust with their carrier partners.
3. Improved Efficiency:
 * Streamlined Processes: Transparent rate information can streamline processes and reduce administrative burdens for both brokers and carriers.
 * Faster Negotiations: Clear and upfront rate discussions can lead to quicker negotiations and agreements.
4. Better Decision-Making:
 * Informed Choices: Carriers can make more informed decisions about which loads to accept based on the rates offered.
 * Strategic Planning: Carriers can better plan their operations and allocate resources with accurate rate information.
5. Potential for Increased Revenue:
 * Fairer Compensation: By understanding the full rate structure, carriers may be able to negotiate for better rates and improve their profitability.
It's important to note that while CFR 371.3 has the potential to bring significant benefits to the freight industry, its effectiveness depends on its enforcement and the willingness of all parties to adhere to its provisions, which brokers have failed to do so since CFR 371.3 has been a law. Brokers are already planning on making carriers waive their right to this rule (screenshots are attached.)

Allowing brokers to make carriers waive their right to CFR 371.3 rate transparency can have several negative consequences:
 * Unfair Market Practices:
 * Reduced Negotiating Power: Without full transparency, carriers may be disadvantaged in negotiations, leading to lower rates and reduced profitability.
 * Potential for Exploitation: Carriers may be unaware of the true rate and margin, potentially leading to exploitation by brokers.
 * Decreased Trust and Collaboration:
 * Eroded Relationships: Waiving transparency can damage the trust between brokers and carriers, hindering future collaborations.
 * Increased Distrust: Carriers may become more suspicious of brokers' intentions, leading to a less cooperative industry environment.
 * Reduced Efficiency:
 * Increased Administrative Burden: Carriers may need to invest more time and resources to verify rates and negotiate fair deals.
 * Delayed Payments: Lack of transparency can lead to disputes and delays in payment.
 * Limited Innovation:
 * Reduced Incentives for Improvement: Without transparency, carriers may have less incentive to improve their services and efficiency.
 * Stifled Competition: A lack of transparency can hinder competition and innovation in the freight industry.
 * Legal and Regulatory Risks:
 * Potential for Litigation: Waiving transparency could expose brokers and carriers to legal risks and potential lawsuits.
 * Regulatory Scrutiny: Increased scrutiny from regulatory agencies may lead to stricter enforcement of transparency rules.
Allowing brokers to make carriers waive their right to CFR 371.3 rate transparency can undermine the intended purpose of the regulation and lead to negative consequences for the entire freight industry. Dear FMCSA Rulemaking Team, 

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposed rulemaking titled “Transparency in Property Broker Transactions” (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257-0001). This rule represents unnecessary government overreach and fails to address the real challenges facing the transportation industry today. 
 
Key Concerns with the Proposed Rule: 
 
1. Outdated and Misaligned Framework:
The expansion of C.F.R 371.3 in the proposed manner, a regulation from a time when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, fails to take account that the current marketplace operates fundamentally differently, with brokers facilitating two separate business transactions—one with the shipper and one with the carrier. The regulation's original intent was to prevent freight rebating, a practice no longer relevant under modern industry practices.

2. Unnecessary and Burdensome Requirements:
The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and technology tools providing real-time pricing visibility. Mandating electronic record submission through methods such as EDI or API integration would impose significant financial burdens, costing $2,500–$10,000 per integration—expenses likely to fall on motor carriers.

3. Lack of Evidence for Regulation:
During the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of heightened scrutiny, the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database recorded no complaints on this issue. This proposal addresses a non-existent problem.

4. Legal and Confidentiality Concerns:
The proposed rule risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, as it involves disclosing sensitive pricing and business information. Recent legal precedent, including the Supreme Court’s decision to reverse the Chevron case, raises questions about the FMCSA’s authority to enforce such a rule.

5. Misplaced Priorities:
Rather than pursuing unnecessary expansions of outdated regulations, the FMCSA should focus on critical issues impacting our industry, such as improving highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually.

Conclusion: 
 
The FMCSA’s proposal is a solution in search of a problem. It imposes costly and unnecessary burdens on brokers and carriers while ignoring the true challenges facing the transportation industry. I urge the FMCSA to withdraw this proposed rule and redirect its efforts toward initiatives that enhance safety and combat fraud. 
 
Thank you for considering my comments. I trust the FMCSA will prioritize policies that truly support the transportation industry's success and sustainability. 
 
Sincerely,

Stephen Linn
President
Blue Dory Enterprises, LLC
Agent for AGX Freight

**Transparency vs. Confidentiality**

### **Brokers' Concerns**

**1. Proprietary Information Protection**
**2. Operational Viability**
**3. Increased Administrative Burden**

### **Carriers' Concerns**

**1. Unfair Compensation**
**2. Risk of Exploitation**
**3. Eliminating Waivers**

### **Potential Solutions**

Finding a middle ground is critical to address these conflicting interests. Some potential compromises include:

**1. Selective Disclosure:**
**2. Caps on Broker Fees**
**3. Technology-Based Solutions**
**4. Third-Party Oversight**
-- 

**Here’s how blockchain and smart contracts could address key concerns:**

### **1. Transparency Without Compromising Confidentiality**
- **Selective Data Sharing**: Blockchain allows selective access to information. For example, a smart contract could reveal only the relevant details (e.g., the agreed freight rate and payment status) to carriers, without disclosing proprietary shipper details or other sensitive data.
- **Immutable Records**: All transaction data is stored on a decentralized ledger, ensuring accuracy and preventing unauthorized alterations. Stakeholders can trust the records without requiring full disclosure of all information.

### **2. Automated Compliance**
- **Predefined Rules**: Smart contracts execute automatically when pre-agreed conditions are met. For example:
 - A broker could set a contract where carriers are automatically paid once proof of delivery is uploaded and verified.
 - Fee structures, fuel surcharges, and payment timelines can be transparently coded into the contract, leaving no room for disputes.
- **Regulatory Compliance**: Brokers can comply with FMCSA's transparency rules by granting regulators access to transaction details stored securely on the blockchain, without impacting operational confidentiality.

### **3. Trust-Building Among Stakeholders**
- **Decentralization**: Blockchain removes the need for a central authority (e.g., the broker) to manage the transaction. Instead, all parties operate on a shared platform, reducing the potential for mistrust or exploitation.
- **Fee Transparency**: Brokers can demonstrate their value by showcasing the services provided and the fees retained, helping carriers understand the breakdown of charges without exposing customer specifics.

### **4. Enhanced Security**
- **Encryption**: Blockchain records are encrypted and can only be accessed by authorized parties. This ensures proprietary data, such as shipper identities and negotiated rates, remain confidential.
- **Fraud Prevention**: Blockchain’s decentralized nature makes it nearly impossible to manipulate data or engage in fraudulent practices, fostering a more secure ecosystem.

### **5. Dispute Resolution**
- **Audit Trails**: Every transaction on the blockchain is timestamped and immutable, creating a robust audit trail. Disputes over freight rates, payment terms, or service conditions can be resolved quickly using this transparent record.
- **Smart Contract Arbitration**: In cases of non-compliance or disputes, pre-programmed arbitration rules within the smart contract could automatically resolve issues or escalate them to a neutral third party.

### **Challenges to Implementation**
Despite its potential, adopting blockchain and smart contracts in freight brokerage transactions comes with challenges:
1. **Initial Costs**: Setting up blockchain infrastructure and developing smart contracts can be costly, particularly for smaller firms.
2. **Industry Standardization**: To be effective, blockchain requires widespread adoption and standardization of data formats and processes across the freight industry.
3. **Technological Barriers**: Stakeholders may lack the technical expertise needed to implement and maintain blockchain systems.
4. **Legal and Regulatory Alignment**: Blockchain solutions would need to align with FMCSA regulations and other legal frameworks, which may not currently account for decentralized technologies.

### **Conclusion**
Blockchain smart contracts have significant potential to bridge the gap between transparency and confidentiality. By enabling **secure, automated, and selective disclosure**, they can address concerns from both brokers and carriers while fostering trust and efficiency. However, widespread adoption will depend on overcoming technical, financial, and regulatory hurdles. 

As the freight industry evolves, blockchain could become a transformative tool in creating a more equitable and efficient brokerage ecosystem.

As a small carrier, I fully support the FMCSA’s proposed rulemaking on transparency in property broker transactions. This initiative is critical to protecting and strengthening what is essentially one of the few rights carriers have in an industry that is otherwise heavily regulated against us.

For too long, carriers have faced barriers to accessing transaction records that are essential for understanding the true financial details of our work. The practices of brokers coercing carriers into waiving their rights, or hiding behind contract clauses, create an unequal and unfair playing field that disproportionately harms small businesses. These practices not only violate the intent of existing transparency regulations but also undermine trust and accountability in the entire transportation sector.

The requirement to provide electronic transaction records within 48 hours of service completion is a simple yet powerful measure to ensure carriers can access information they are entitled to. It will give carriers—especially small, independent operators—the tools to verify they are being treated fairly. Additionally, prohibiting brokers from including waiver provisions in contracts will end a predatory practice that forces carriers to relinquish their rights just to do business.

This rule is not about undermining brokers or limiting their ability to profit—it is about fairness, transparency, and ensuring that all parties in the supply chain operate on equal footing. Carriers deserve to see the financial details of the transactions they are a part of. After all, we are the backbone of this industry, delivering the goods that keep this country moving.

I urge the FMCSA to adopt this proposal and go further, if needed, to ensure brokers comply fully and consistently with transparency requirements. Protecting this right is essential for maintaining balance and fairness in an industry that often overlooks the struggles of small carriers. Let’s ensure accountability and equity for all.

2. **Risk of shipper-carrier collusion**: Increased transparency could enable shippers and carriers to collaborate in identifying broker markups, pressuring brokers to reduce their margins.

3. **Conflicts with existing confidentiality agreements**: Prohibiting brokers from restricting carriers' data requests may clash with shippers' contractual confidentiality requirements, leading to legal dilemmas.

4. **Administrative burdens on brokers**: Complying with the 48-hour data request requirement would impose significant time and cost pressures on brokers.

A more balanced approach could focus on vetting brokers to eliminate bad actors while preserving the value provided by reputable brokers.

I am writing to express my opposition to the proposed rulemaking regarding "Transparency in Property Broker Transactions," which seeks to mandate the disclosure of certain pricing and contractual details between property brokers, shippers, and motor carriers.

While I recognize the importance of transparency in promoting fairness and reducing fraudulent practices, I believe that this proposed rule will have unintended negative consequences for the industry, especially for small businesses, motor carriers, and freight brokers. Below are the key reasons for my opposition:

Increased Burden on Small Brokers and Carriers: The proposed rule would impose significant compliance burdens on small and independent brokers and carriers, who may lack the resources to manage the additional documentation and reporting requirements. This could lead to higher operating costs, which would disproportionately affect smaller businesses and potentially force them out of the market.

Reduction in Competitive Pricing: The disclosure of specific financial terms between brokers, shippers, and motor carriers could lead to a loss of pricing confidentiality. In a competitive market, this transparency could reduce the flexibility needed to negotiate favorable terms and could drive up costs for shippers, ultimately harming consumers. Freight rates are often negotiable and depend on various factors, including route, demand, and load specifics—mandating the disclosure of these rates could stifle negotiation opportunities and lead to price inflation.

Market Distortions and Anti-Competitive Effects: Requiring transparency in all broker transactions could enable market participants to gain an unfair advantage by accessing confidential business information. This could result in anti-competitive behavior and hinder the ability of brokers and carriers to compete on a level playing field.

Unnecessary Government Interference: The FMCSA’s role in regulating brokers should focus on ensuring safety, compliance, and operational integrity. This proposed rule oversteps by interfering with the private contractual relationships between brokers and carriers, which are best left to the marketplace rather than subject to rigid government mandates.

Lack of Clear Benefits to the Industry: While the intention behind the rule may be to improve transparency and reduce abuses in the industry, there is insufficient evidence to show that such a rule would lead to meaningful improvements. Transparency in pricing does not automatically guarantee better practices, and it risks creating more red tape and administrative overhead without delivering tangible benefits.

In conclusion, I strongly urge the FMCSA to reconsider this proposed rule and seek alternative approaches that promote transparency, fairness, and competition without creating additional regulatory burdens or distorting the market. The focus should be on supporting small businesses and ensuring fair, competitive practices without unnecessary government intervention.

Thank you for considering my comments.

Dear FMCSA Representatives,

As a carrier, I want to express my concerns about the proposed changes to freight broker transparency regulations (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257). While I understand the intention to promote transparency, I believe these measures could have negative consequences, particularly for small carriers.

Specifically, the following issues need to be addressed:
1.Risk of retaliation and blacklisting: Requesting transaction records could expose carriers to being excluded from future business dealings by brokers who feel uncomfortable or threatened by such requests. This could lead to fewer opportunities for carriers and promote unfair practices.
2.Administrative burden: Implementing these changes could increase tension between carriers and brokers, especially in competitive markets, making it harder to collaborate effectively and resolve disputes amicably.
3.Limited impact on actual transparency: While requiring brokers to maintain electronic records may help, it does not directly address the power imbalance between brokers and small carriers. Additional safeguards are needed to protect carriers from retaliation when seeking transparency.

I propose that the FMCSA include provisions to prevent blacklisting and retaliation against carriers exercising their right to request records. Additionally, implementing anonymous reporting mechanisms could allow carriers to report abuses without fear of repercussions.

Thank you for the opportunity to share my concerns, and I hope the FMCSA will consider adjustments to this proposal to ensure that the regulations promote transparency without harming carriers.

Sincerely,

A concerned trucker

Comment on Transparency in Property Broker Transactions NPRM

Thank you to the FMCSA for taking the time to meet with the National Owner Operators Association (NOOA) at their headquarters in May. Your willingness to engage with industry stakeholders is vital to addressing the challenges faced by owner-operators and small carriers.

We strongly support the proposed rule to enforce transparency in property broker transactions under 49 CFR 371.3. Non-enforcement of this rule has significant safety implications and disproportionately harms small businesses. For example:

Safety risks due to financial strain: When carriers are unaware of the true value of a load, they may accept below-cost rates, leading to financial stress. This often results in delayed maintenance or unsafe practices to cut costs, compromising road safety.

Driver fatigue: Carriers under financial pressure may feel forced to work longer hours, potentially violating Hours of Service (HOS) regulations, which directly impacts safety.

Market distortion: Lack of transparency enables bad actors to exploit the system, creating an uneven playing field and driving small, safety-conscious carriers out of the market.

The cost of transparency to brokers is minimal. In fact, other sectors of the freight industry already demonstrate this effectively. For instance:

Household goods brokers and carriers routinely share all contracts they receive from customers when working under interline agreements. This ensures fairness and clarity for all parties.
Household goods brokers are legally required to disclose their commissions upfront and provide full details of what was quoted to the customer versus what was collected. Customers can easily confirm these details, creating a system of accountability.
Transparency is not a burden; it is a standard of fairness. The lack of transparency in property freight transactions stands out as a deliberate strategy by some brokers to maximize profits with minimal effort. The authors of the Motor Carrier Act of 1980 foresaw this risk and wisely included transparency provisions to protect small businesses from predatory practices.

Every industry in this nation operates with a level of enforced transparency to ensure fairness. Imagine the chaos if Wall Street suddenly decided to abandon its transparency rules. Industry does not function equitably or efficiently without rules that level the playing field.

We urge the FMCSA to finalize and enforce this critical rule. A transparent system will not only uphold safety and fairness but will also restore trust in the freight industry and strengthen its foundation for all participants.

Sincerely,
Michael Boston
President 

The National Owner Operators Association (NOOA)

Dear FMCSA Rulemaking Team, 
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposed rulemaking titled “Transparency in Property Broker Transactions” (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257-0001). This rule represents unnecessary government overreach and fails to address the real challenges facing the transportation industry today. 
 
Key Concerns with the Proposed Rule: 
 
1. Outdated and Misaligned Framework:
• The expansion of C.F.R 371.3 in the proposed manner, a regulation from a time when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, fails to take account that the current marketplace operates fundamentally differently, with brokers facilitating two separate business transactions—one with the shipper and one with the carrier.
• The regulation's original intent was to prevent freight rebating, a practice no longer relevant under modern industry practices.
2. Unnecessary and Burdensome Requirements:
• The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and technology tools providing real-time pricing visibility.
• Mandating electronic record submission through methods such as EDI or API integration would impose significant financial burdens, costing $2,500–$10,000 per integration—expenses likely to fall on motor carriers.
3. Lack of Evidence for Regulation:
• During the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of heightened scrutiny, the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database recorded no complaints on this issue. This proposal addresses a non-existent problem.
4. Legal and Confidentiality Concerns:
• The proposed rule risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, as it involves disclosing sensitive pricing and business information.
• Recent legal precedent, including the Supreme Court’s decision to reverse the Chevron case, raises questions about the FMCSA’s authority to enforce such a rule.
5. Misplaced Priorities:
• Rather than pursuing unnecessary expansions of outdated regulations, the FMCSA should focus on critical issues impacting our industry, such as improving highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually.
Conclusion: 
 
The FMCSA’s proposal is a solution in search of a problem. It imposes costly and unnecessary burdens on brokers and carriers while ignoring the true challenges facing the transportation industry. I urge the FMCSA to withdraw this proposed rule and redirect its efforts toward initiatives that enhance safety and combat fraud. 
 
Thank you for considering my comments. I trust the FMCSA will prioritize policies that truly support the transportation industry's success and sustainability. 
 
Sincerely, 
Jason Grumling

I’m writing on behalf of the hardworking people in trucking—carriers, brokers, and everyone who keeps America’s freight moving—because this proposed rule from FMCSA just doesn’t add up. While the idea of more transparency might sound good on paper, the changes being suggested here will actually hurt, not help, the industry.

Carriers already have the tools to make smart business decisions, and adding more paperwork, disclosures, and complicated requirements won’t fix the real problems they face. The last thing we need is another layer of red tape that eats up time, raises costs, and pushes good brokers out of the market. If brokers have to jump through too many hoops, small and mid-sized ones—the ones that actually get the job done in tough situations—may not survive. This doesn’t help carriers; it just narrows their options, limits competition, and raises prices.

This proposal does nothing for the public either. Freight rates aren’t going to magically stabilize, capacity issues won’t vanish, and safety isn’t improved by forcing brokers to open their books. More mandatory reporting only encourages more aggressive, hardball tactics between businesses. Instead of building trust, it’ll turn honest negotiations into drawn-out fights over who has what piece of data.

On top of that, making certain information public could give unethical players a cheat sheet. With everybody’s numbers out there, it becomes easier for bad actors to game the system. The result? More headaches, not fewer, for carriers and brokers alike.

Let’s not pretend this rule solves deep-rooted industry challenges. Driver shortages, volatile fuel costs, and tight shipping deadlines aren’t fixed by more paperwork. If the FMCSA wants to help, it should focus on enforcing the rules we have and working with industry groups on practical solutions—not piling on burdensome requirements that choke off the flexibility and innovation we rely on to keep freight moving.

In short: this proposal hurts both carriers and brokers, adds nothing to public safety or stability, and drains the industry’s time and money. I urge the FMCSA to back off from this misguided approach. We need policies that help our industry grow stronger, not ones that tie our hands.

I’m writing as someone who’s directly in the trenches of this industry. I run a small over-the-road trucking operation with 15 trucks and I also manage a mid-sized brokerage. Because I see both sides every day, I’m convinced that the FMCSA’s proposed broker transparency rule is not going to help anyone—carriers, brokers, or the public.

Let’s start with a common misconception: many small and medium-sized trucking companies blame brokers for their financial troubles, assuming brokers always hold back and set low rates on purpose. It’s an easy story to believe, but it’s not the whole truth. Rates aren’t just pulled out of thin air; they’re shaped by supply and demand, how many trucks are available, what shippers are willing to pay, and overall consumer spending habits. In other words, this industry’s pricing isn’t controlled by secret broker handshakes—it’s driven by market forces.

If there’s a rule that forces brokers to share more of their internal workings, it’s not going to boost the rates carriers get. Carriers might think “transparency” will somehow strong-arm brokers into paying more, but that’s not how the market works. Rates aren’t going to suddenly jump just because everyone can see more numbers. If anything, the carriers expecting bigger paydays will be disappointed, and that disappointment will create even more resentment and mistrust.

For brokers, this new rule is just another pile of paperwork and risk. The smaller and mid-sized brokers—just like my operation—don’t have huge back offices or unlimited staff to handle the extra red tape. Some brokers may choose not to comply at all, leaving carriers with fewer options. And if the FMCSA tries to crack down, do they really have the manpower and resources to enforce it across the entire country? Considering current enforcement struggles, it seems unlikely.

What about the public? The public isn’t going to benefit from this. They’re not going to see lower prices at the store or safer roads because brokers had to show more spreadsheets. If anything, when you weigh down the brokers and carriers with unnecessary reporting and complexity, costs can creep up and efficiency goes down. That’s not good for anybody, including the general public.

We should also consider that the FMCSA might be overstepping its authority by pushing these transparency rules. Right now, the FMCSA already struggles to manage the enforcement duties it has with brokerages, often needing to involve the Department of Justice to handle even minor issues. Adding another complicated layer will just create more confusion. If broker transparency is truly something the industry needs, that’s a conversation for Congress. Congress can define the scope of power, make sure there’s a solid legal framework, and then, if necessary, ask FMCSA to carry it out in a way that makes sense.

At the end of the day, this proposed rule doesn’t fix what’s really holding carriers back. It doesn’t solve the problem of supply and demand. It doesn’t fix driver shortages, rising fuel costs, or cut through the ups and downs of the economy. All it does is set the stage for more finger-pointing, more tension, and more frustrated trucking families wondering why the government stuck its nose in and made a mess.

I strongly urge the FMCSA to back off this path and let the market function. Let’s not fuel more blame games and false hopes. If real change in broker transparency is needed, let Congress take the lead and define the ground rules, so we have a proper, well-thought-out approach—if we need one at all. As it stands, this rule is a distraction that offers no real benefit to carriers, brokers, or the public. It’s just more red tape in an industry that’s already working hard to deliver America’s goods day in and day out.

The proposed broker transparency rule threatens to disrupt an already delicate freight market by overregulating how brokers and carriers interact. While transparency sounds beneficial in theory, in practice, this rule undermines the fundamental principles of a free market. Freight brokers provide critical value through their ability to negotiate, manage risks, and streamline logistics for shippers and carriers alike. Requiring disclosure of transaction details will hinder brokers' ability to compete, negotiate rates effectively, and attract shippers.

Furthermore, this rule does not account for the complexities of freight brokering, including overhead costs, credit risks, and operational expenses. It paints brokers as middlemen profiting unfairly without recognizing the essential role they play in stabilizing the market and connecting carriers to consistent freight opportunities.

If carriers feel dissatisfied with rates, they have the right to negotiate or work with other brokers – that’s how a free market functions. This rule would erode competition, create distrust, and ultimately harm carriers, shippers, and brokers by increasing costs and reducing flexibility.

Instead of focusing on restrictive transparency measures, efforts should prioritize fostering collaboration, fair negotiation practices, and education on market dynamics for all stakeholders. Let’s ensure the freight industry continues to thrive through innovation, competition, and trust – not unnecessary regulation.

1. Increased Operational Costs: Implementing the new transparency requirements would likely lead to higher administrative costs. Smaller brokerage firms, in particular, might struggle with the financial burden of complying with these regulations, potentially driving some out of business.
2. Loss of Competitive Edge: Mandatory transparency could force brokers to disclose sensitive pricing and business strategy information. This could erode their competitive advantage, as competitors would gain access to proprietary data that could be used to undercut their business.
3. Reduced Contractual Flexibility: The proposed rules might limit brokers' ability to negotiate flexible contract terms tailored to specific situations. This rigidity could hinder the ability to create mutually beneficial agreements between brokers and carriers.
4. Increased Legal Risks: The requirement to provide electronic transaction records within 48 hours could lead to more disputes and litigation. Brokers might face increased legal and compliance risks, as any delays or errors in providing these records could result in penalties or lawsuits.
5. Administrative Burden: The new rules would add significant administrative workload, requiring brokers to maintain and provide detailed transaction records promptly. This could divert resources away from core business activities, impacting overall efficiency and productivity.
6. Market Disruption: The proposed transparency measures could disrupt the market dynamics by creating an uneven playing field. Larger brokers with more resources might be able to comply more easily, while smaller brokers could be disproportionately affected, leading to reduced competition in the industry.

Transparency in brokered transactions is critical not only for fairness but also for addressing pressing issues like freight fraud. Strengthening transparency requirements will promote accountability and trust across the industry.

1. Fraud Prevention Through Transparency
Requiring brokers to provide clear financial records deters fraudulent practices like double brokering and hidden markups. A transparent marketplace protects carriers from exploitation and strengthens the integrity of the supply chain.

2. Mandatory and Automatic Records Requirement
Brokers must be required to automatically provide records related to each transaction upon load completion. Carriers already meet their documentation obligations under tight time constraints. Requiring the same of brokers ensures equity and simplifies compliance for all parties.

3. Prohibiting Waivers
FMCSA must explicitly prohibit brokers from waiving their regulatory duties. Allowing such waivers constitutes evasion of regulation and violates antitrust principles. Congress has not authorized brokers to waive their obligations, and FMCSA must enforce this distinction.

Transparency reforms that include automatic record provisions and waiver prohibitions will strengthen the industry and protect all stakeholders from fraud and exploitation.

To the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA),

I am writing to express my strong support for requiring freight brokers to provide rate confirmation transparency to carriers. As a [carrier/owner-operator], I believe that transparency in freight transactions is essential for fair competition, accurate accounting, and building trust between carriers and brokers.

Rate confirmation transparency would enable carriers to verify the rates negotiated with shippers, ensuring that brokers are not engaging in unfair practices, such as rate manipulation or hidden fees. This transparency would also facilitate more accurate invoicing, reducing disputes and promoting timely payment.

Furthermore, rate confirmation transparency would promote a more level playing field, allowing carriers to make informed decisions about which loads to accept and at what rates. This, in turn, would drive more efficient and competitive markets, ultimately benefiting shippers and the broader supply chain.

I urge the FMCSA to take steps to require freight brokers to provide rate confirmation transparency to carriers. This simple yet critical reform would go a long way in promoting fairness, transparency, and accountability in the freight brokerage industry.

Thank you for considering my comment.

Sincerely,

Garcia Interstate Carriers
Jessie Garcia

As an owner-operator, I believe rate transparency is crucial for a fair and equitable marketplace. Transparency ensures that carriers—who face significant operational costs—are adequately compensated for their services.

1. Fair Competition Through Transparency
Transparency does not dictate broker earnings but ensures carriers have the information needed to make informed decisions. Brokers' claims of unsustainable margins are unfounded; reasonable markups will not be threatened by transparency but will instead encourage equitable competition.

2. Records Must Be Provided Automatically
Transparency reforms should mandate that brokers automatically supply all required records upon completion of a load. Carriers already provide BOLs and signed rate confirmations promptly, often under challenging conditions. It is only fair that brokers reciprocate by providing the financial records required under CFR 371.3 without requiring carriers to request them explicitly.

3. No Waivers Should Be Allowed
FMCSA must prohibit brokers from using waivers to evade their regulatory obligations. Such waivers are not authorized under law and violate principles of fair trade. Congress has made clear distinctions between the rights of shippers, carriers, and brokers, and FMCSA must enforce these distinctions to ensure compliance.

By implementing these measures, FMCSA can ensure fair competition, promote equity, and strengthen trust across the industry.

Transparency could compromise the competitive dynamics of the market. Brokers often operate in a highly competitive environment where proprietary strategies, pricing models, and client relationships are key to maintaining a competitive edge. If brokers are required to disclose every detail of their operations, it could level the playing field to an extent that erodes their ability to innovate or negotiate effectively. Moreover, transparency could expose sensitive client information or trading strategies, potentially leading to market manipulation or the unintended disclosure of private financial data. In some cases, a balance between transparency and discretion is necessary to ensure that brokers can continue to serve their clients efficiently without undue exposure to external pressures or competitive disadvantages.

Expansion of 371.3 is based on an outdated, burdensome and government overreach.
Formalized in 1980 when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, not how the market currently operates.
The intent was to have financial transparency between all parties to a brokered load to ensure the illegal practice of freight rebating was not occurring, where a broker and shipper were common owners.
The current marketplace involves two separate business transactions between the shipper and broker and the broker and carrier.
The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) made it clear in its intent of this regulation, “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace."
During the COVID-19 pandemic, when this issue was at its height, there were no complaints in the consumer complaint database. 
This is a solution in search of a problem. 
The transportation marketplace is already very transparent with loadboards and other technology pricing tools. 
The only safe way to achieve electronic submission safely would be through an EDI or API integration, at the cost of roughly $2,500 - $10,000 per integration. Motor carriers would bear the cost of this as well. 
Possible violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 which includes pricing and business information. 
FMCSA should focus on highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic that is costing the supply-chain over $1 billion annually. 
The FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers.
The U.S. Supreme Court overruled the Chevron deference decision, which brings in legal concerns of this rulemaking.

I work as a freight broker at a smaller company in Texas and below are some of my thoughts and perspectives on why Docket No FMCSA-2023-
0257 will cause a disastrous effect on brokers everywhere, specifically the smaller companies who have worked years to build relationships with customers and carriers alike based on effective communication, fair rates and efficiency.

Brokers have the unique ability to add value through their marker expertise and risk management. Brokers are skilled at negotiating rates, ensuring that carriers and shippers both receive fair treatment in a complex marketplace. They are often able to secure better rates than a shipper could on their own due to their broader market knowledge, volume leverage, and ongoing relationships with carriers. They also help create a competitive market by connecting shippers with appropriate carriers, making it more efficient. Requiring them to disclose their earnings could undermine their ability to negotiate effectively and may discourage them from acting as intermediaries, reducing market efficiency.

Another impact this ruling would have would blur the lines of what is considered confidential between businesses and customers alike. Profit margins are often a sensitive part of any business’s strategy. Requiring brokers to disclose their profits may expose their business model to competitors, potentially leading to market pressure that could drive down earnings. This would disproportionately affect smaller, independent brokers who might not have the resources to absorb such pressures. The financial terms between a broker and a carrier are often part of a negotiated agreement based on long-term relationships. Requiring brokers to disclose how much they paid a carrier vs. how much they earned could breach the confidentiality of these agreements, potentially damaging relationships and trust between parties.

This ruling also puts a risk of undermining the role of a freight broker in general. If brokers are required to disclose their profit margins publicly, it could encourage shippers and carriers to bypass brokers and form direct relationships. While this may seem beneficial in theory, it could disrupt the balance of the supply chain and lead to inefficiencies. Smaller carriers or shippers may lack the resources or market knowledge to negotiate favorable terms on their own. The primary value that brokers provide is the service of coordinating freight in a way that benefits both the shipper and the carrier. The actual rate or profit margin is less relevant to the end customer, who is primarily interested in having goods delivered on time and at the right price. Focusing too much on margin disclosures could detract from the overall service brokerages provide.

Brokers provide transparency through other means with market rates and customer satisfaction. Many brokers are already bound by industry regulations or contracts to ensure transparency in pricing, and carriers are often provided with a breakdown of the charges they will incur. The idea of a "markup" is commonly understood in the context of third-party services, and the contract negotiation process itself typically involves sufficient transparency. Shippers and carriers already have mechanisms to assess whether they are getting value for their money, whether through contract negotiations, market rates, or customer reviews. More stringent disclosure would not necessarily lead to better outcomes for either party, but could potentially distort the market.

At the end of the day, having confidentiality encourages fair competition within the industry. Profit margins for brokers reflect the level of service and expertise they provide. These margins help sustain their operations, support their infrastructure, and ensure they can continue to offer value-added services like tracking, claims management, or customer service. Penalizing brokers for making a reasonable profit could lead to the collapse of smaller players in the market, reducing competition. The key to a fair and functioning market is regulation that ensures ethical practices, not arbitrary profit margin disclosures. Brokers should be held accountable for ensuring proper service levels and compliance with relevant regulations, but their profitability should not be subjected to public scrutiny unless there’s evidence of abuse.

In conclusion, requiring brokers to disclose exactly how much they pay carriers versus what they earn could have unintended negative consequences, such as reducing market efficiency, undermining competitive fairness, and creating undue friction with carriers. Brokers serve a vital role in connecting shippers with carriers and adding value through their expertise, and the focus of regulation should be on ensuring transparency in the services provided, rather than in profit margins.
