I have been in the logistics industry since the mid-90s. When I came into the industry there were still plenty of people who remembered deregulation. Asset based carriers were growing with quality service and pricing was acceptable. It seemed everyone (carriers / trucking companies, and shippers) were satisfied with the relationship of an open market. Shippers wanted the convenience of utilizing a bank of multi-truck fleets so that when needs (not under a guarantee of service contract) arise, the shipper was not calling many small trucking outfits that ultimately could not satisfy the truck need (i.e., too small to supply consistent capacity).

Twenty-five years later brokerage has grown to be a HUGE piece of the industry. This appears to be according to four primary reasons:

(1) drivers want independence (flexibility on when and where they want to work, and see upside in more take-home pay potential); independent drivers that don't have overhead costs that are (typically) as much as large trucking companies therefore some independent truckers are willing to take a lower rate on loads;

(2) trucking (fleet) companies have reduced driver standards so that they pay drivers less wages to keep costs lower. Today there are many drivers who do not speak or read English well because they have come from outside the US and got a truck driving job with little to no experience, and they do not command a wage that experienced truck drivers require. As such, the overhead costs of these fleets allows the carriers to accept a lower rate on truck moves, which in turn puts a lot of pressure on independent owner-operators to accept a lower rate or miss out on a load. 

(3) brokerage has grown tremendously through technology (reach has grown for independent operators); cell phones, email (not fax), texting, software, GPS, etc. are all big contributors to connecting with independent drivers, as well as brokerage startups. Even asset-based fleets have utilized brokerage to reduce empty miles and find backhauls for trucks unloading.

(4) shippers have been able to utilize the spot market that has developed under brokerage growth, and have been more accepting of using brokers to secure 'open market' capacity for inconsistent volumes that don't fit well in a contract rate. As spot pricing on trucks is typically (not always) lower than contract price, shippers are taking the lower rate on spot price more frequently than they used to do. Additionally, shippers do NOT want to contact many carriers for capacity, nor want to vet owner operators, therefore shippers have essentially 'outsourced' by using select brokers to find capacity and ensure risk issues are minimalized.

It seems that there is a shift in the market today, where independent owner operators want the freedom of driving their own truck, yet because of saturation in the market and lack of a 'sales force' to find loads (and the lack of shipper interest in having MANY owner operators calling for loads coming soon), they must use brokerage at a near-exclusive manner for accessing loads.

However, brokers often absorb risk (contingent cargo and auto liability), and must qualify truckers. In a soft rate market brokers are still looking to make some margin and manage their risks and workload, and when there are many options for trucks available, brokers may be inclined to keep a certain minimum gross profit on a shipment rather than offer more to a trucker.

What many independent trucks do not accept is that a broker has worked diligently to secure a relationship with a shipper (often investing MONTHS or longer before getting the promise to move a load with that shipper), therefore the broker is looking to maintain a margin level for past business development work, maintaining the shipper relationship, and for risks assumed.

Additionally, carriers using brokers must accept that brokerage markets are a matter of supply and demand. If a carrier agrees to cover a shipment for a given price, a broker could have (would very likely) given the load to another carrier if the carrier accepting a load award was concerned for what the broker's margin was more so than what it accepted for pay on the load. While truckers have costs to cover in operating a truck, as well desire profit, the fact that truckers accept a particular rate on a load is that trucker's choice.

While it seems the large number of truckers working under brokerage today want a rate guarantee on each load to cover costs and profit (along with maintaining independence from a large trucking company), it seems there is a lack of acceptance that that there is inevitable risks in trucking, especially in an over-abundance of supply of trucks versus loads.

I am opposed to this passing. I have been a transportation Broker for over 20 years. Here are a few Points to be made :
1. Brokers Add Value Through Market Expertise and Risk Management
• Expertise in Negotiation: Brokers are skilled at negotiating rates, ensuring that carriers and shippers both receive fair treatment in a complex marketplace. They are often able to secure better rates than a shipper could on their own due to their broader market knowledge, volume leverage, and ongoing relationships with carriers.
• Market Efficiency: Brokers help create a competitive market by connecting shippers with appropriate carriers, making it more efficient. Requiring them to disclose their earnings could undermine their ability to negotiate effectively and may discourage them from acting as intermediaries, reducing market efficiency.
2. Impact on Business Confidentiality
• Competitive Sensitivity: Profit margins are often a sensitive part of any business’s strategy. Requiring brokers to disclose their profits may expose their business model to competitors, potentially leading to market pressure that could drive down earnings. This would disproportionately affect smaller, independent brokers who might not have the resources to absorb such pressures.
• Confidentiality: The financial terms between a broker and a carrier are often part of a negotiated agreement based on long-term relationships. Requiring brokers to disclose how much they paid a carrier vs. how much they earned could breach the confidentiality of these agreements, potentially damaging relationships and trust between parties.
3. Risk of Undermining the Broker’s Role
• Incentivizing Direct Relationships: If brokers are required to disclose their profit margins publicly, it could encourage shippers and carriers to bypass brokers and form direct relationships. While this may seem beneficial in theory, it could disrupt the balance of the supply chain and lead to inefficiencies. Smaller carriers or shippers may lack the resources or market knowledge to negotiate favorable terms on their own.
• Focus on Results, Not Margins: The primary value that brokers provide is the service of coordinating freight in a way that benefits both the shipper and the carrier. The actual rate or profit margin is less relevant to the end customer, who is primarily interested in having goods delivered on time and at the right price. Focusing too much on margin disclosures could detract from the overall service brokerages provide.
4. Transparency is Already Achieved Through Other Means
• Market Rates: Many brokers are already bound by industry regulations or contracts to ensure transparency in pricing, and carriers are often provided with a breakdown of the charges they will incur. The idea of a "markup" is commonly understood in the context of third-party services, and the contract negotiation process itself typically involves sufficient transparency.
• Customer Satisfaction: Shippers and carriers already have mechanisms to assess whether they are getting value for their money, whether through contract negotiations, market rates, or customer reviews. More stringent disclosure would not necessarily lead to better outcomes for either party, but could potentially distort the market. . Expansion of 371.3 is based on an outdated, burdensome and government overreach.
Formalized in 1980 when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, not how the market currently operates.
The intent was to have financial transparency between all parties to a brokered load to ensure the illegal practice of freight rebating was not occurring, where a broker and shipper were common owners.
The current marketplace involves two separate business transactions between the shipper and broker and the broker and carrier.
The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) made it clear in its intent of this regulation, “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace."
During the COVID-19 pandemic, when this issue was at its height, there were no complaints in the consumer complaint database. 
This is a solution in search of a problem. 
The transportation marketplace is already very transparent with loadboards and other technology pricing tools. 
The only safe way to achieve electronic submission safely would be through an EDI or API integration, at the cost of roughly $2,500 - $10,000 per integration. Motor carriers would bear the cost of this as well. 
Possible violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 which includes pricing and business information. 
FMCSA should focus on highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic that is costing the supply-chain over $1 billion annually. 
The FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers.
The U.S. Supreme Court overruled the Chevron deference decision, which brings in legal concerns of this rulemaking.

Rate transparency will force shippers to take shipping in house and away from professional experts. 

The cost of standardizing load data, communicating with carriers, and adhering to safety standards will shift to the shipper and be less likely to executed at the current standard. 

Additionally, load aggregation will shift from brokers to online marketplaces, forcing truckers to source, standardize, and negotiate loads directly. 

This will require additional headcount and dispatchers, which will drive up the cost of doing business without increasing the revenue available to the industry. While this move may reduce the profitability of brokers, it will not increase the profitability of carriers, which will be subject to the same supply and demand forces keeping rates low in the current market. 

It will be a net win for shippers and end-consumers, as the cost of transport will likely be reduced by 10%. The questions is - at what cost and distraction to shippers that will now be required to add shipping operations to their headcount? Also - how will shippers know what a fair market rate is for shipping? While many may realize savings, there are just as many that may pay more as brokers are removed and the cost to price shop or be current on market data. 

Please reject this proposal as it is overreach, excessive regulation, and not in the long term best interest of shippers. 

What will be next? Requiring transactional profitability statements for ALL businesses?

In recent years, the call for greater transparency within the trucking and logistics sector has grown louder. Proposed rules would require freight brokers to disclose their billing rates to carriers, effectively revealing the margins they earn on every shipment. On the surface, this might sound fair—after all, carriers face tough operating conditions and want assurances they’re being paid equitably. But if we step back and consider the broader implications, the issue is far more nuanced than it seems.

A Complex Web of Relationships
The trucking industry isn’t just carriers and brokers—it’s an intricate ecosystem that includes shippers, repair shops, insurance providers, fuel suppliers, and more. Each player specializes in a service that others find more efficient to outsource rather than manage in-house. For instance, many carriers rely on external repair shops instead of operating their own. They recognize that outsourcing allows them to tap into the shop’s specialized expertise and equipment. Yet, you don’t see carriers demanding a full breakdown of these shops’ costs and the exact margin they earn on parts and labor. Why? Because specialization provides convenience, efficiency, and overall value—benefits that outweigh constant scrutiny of another party’s profit margins.

Brokers serve a similar role. They connect shippers, who need reliable transportation solutions, with carriers, who depend on a steady flow of freight. Just as outsourcing truck repairs can reduce a carrier’s overhead, outsourcing load sourcing and coordination to a broker lets carriers and shippers focus on their core strengths. This value exchange is what brokers bring to the table.

The Market’s Fluid Nature
Transportation markets are inherently volatile. Rates shift daily based on capacity, seasonal demand, and broader economic conditions. When capacity tightens, carriers earn more; when it loosens, brokers may earn more. Healthy market dynamics allow both sides to thrive over time. Sometimes brokers make a 20% margin—other times they may even take a loss. These fluctuations help maintain balance in the ecosystem. The profit a broker earns on one shipment can offset a loss on another, ensuring they remain stable partners for carriers and shippers through market ups and downs.

If we start capping broker margins or enforcing immediate transparency of every transaction, we risk dismantling this delicate equilibrium. Rigid rules could force carriers into equally inflexible revenue structures, ultimately harming their adaptability and potential profitability. More mandated “transparency” might unintentionally lead to more instability, not less.

Trust, Not Turbulence
At its core, this debate hinges on trust—or a lack thereof. There are, of course, bad actors on both sides: some brokers have failed to pay carriers, and some carriers have left brokers with unpaid invoices. These aren’t arguments against the brokerage model itself; they’re reminders that unethical behavior must be addressed. The solution is to enforce existing laws and hold wrongdoers accountable, not to impose sweeping mandates that undermine the market.

Carriers deserve fair pay, respectful treatment, and reliable partnerships. Dispatchers, drivers, brokers, and shippers all play integral roles in this industry’s success. The goal should be to build enduring relationships, not to pit one group against another. Healthy market principles—where good relationships, consistent service, and solid reputations matter—have always been the industry’s backbone.

A Better Path Forward
Instead of forcing transparency through one-size-fits-all mandates, Let’s ensure unethical behavior is appropriately sanctioned within the current legal framework, rather than adopting policies that reduce everyone’s flexibility.

In an industry as interconnected and specialized as logistics, success depends on trust and partnership. By understanding the market’s inherent complexities and respecting the specialized roles that each party plays, we can maintain the integrity of the industry without sacrificing the dynamic, responsive marketplace that helps us all thrive.

While the proposed changes are a step in the right direction, they are still lacking in substance addressing the issues with transparency. It should be noted that "transparency" in contracts is not anything new or unusual. Two common areas that use a "broker", which is defined as "a person or entity that acts as an intermediary between buyers and sellers to facilitate transactions and arrange contracts" are Real Estate and Stocks & Bonds trading. Both of which are required by law to disclose their charges/commisions on the transactions they are involved in. In both cases, there is no limit on the what they may charge, however by disclosing what the charges are, the market ends up setting what is an acceptable charge. By failing to provide disclosure, the broker is allowed to essentially place their finger on the scale and change what would the balance would be if there was transparency. Additionally, by not requiring non-waivable mandatory disclosure, as is required in the previous examples, it allows the broker to use contractual language that denies the carrier any chance to request the information concerning the transaction. The final rule should address this issue by requiring both mandatory disclosure and nullifying any attempt to require a waiver of that requirement in broker/carrier contracts.

Dear FMCSA Rulemaking Team,
I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposed rulemaking titled “Transparency in Property Broker Transactions” (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257-0001). This rule represents unnecessary government overreach and fails to address the real challenges facing the transportation industry today.

Key Concerns with the Proposed Rule:

1. Outdated and Misaligned Framework:
•The expansion of C.F.R 371.3 in the proposed manner, a regulation from a time when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, fails to take account that the current marketplace operates fundamentally differently, with brokers facilitating two separate business transactions—one with the shipper and one with the carrier.
•The regulation's original intent was to prevent freight rebating, a practice no longer relevant under modern industry practices.
2. Unnecessary and Burdensome Requirements:
•The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and technology tools providing real-time pricing visibility.
•Mandating electronic record submission through methods such as EDI or API integration would impose significant financial burdens, costing $2,500–$10,000 per integration—expenses likely to fall on motor carriers.
3. Lack of Evidence for Regulation:
•During the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of heightened scrutiny, the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database recorded no complaints on this issue. This proposal addresses a non-existent problem.
4. Legal and Confidentiality Concerns:
•The proposed rule risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, as it involves disclosing sensitive pricing and business information.
•Recent legal precedent, including the Supreme Court’s decision to reverse the Chevron case, raises questions about the FMCSA’s authority to enforce such a rule.
5. Misplaced Priorities:
•Rather than pursuing unnecessary expansions of outdated regulations, the FMCSA should focus on critical issues impacting our industry, such as improving highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually.
Conclusion:

The FMCSA’s proposal is a solution in search of a problem. It imposes costly and unnecessary burdens on brokers and carriers while ignoring the true challenges facing the transportation industry. I urge the FMCSA to withdraw this proposed rule and redirect its efforts toward initiatives that enhance safety and combat fraud.

Thank you for considering my comments. I trust the FMCSA will prioritize policies that truly support the transportation industry's success and sustainability.

Sincerely,

Mike Zenz

Broker transparency refers to the clear and open sharing of information between brokers, shippers, and motor carriers. This includes details about freight rates, broker commissions, and accessorial charges. The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) has regulations, such as 49 CFR §371.3, that require brokers to disclose transaction records upon request. However, enforcement and compliance have historically been inconsistent.

In an industry where motor carriers often operate on thin margins, the lack of visibility into freight transactions can lead to distrust and financial strain. Greater transparency not only ensures fairness but also fosters long-term collaboration between brokers and carriers, contributing to a healthier and more efficient trucking ecosystem.

Challenges Posed by Opaque Broker Practices
 1. Unfair Rate Distribution
Motor carriers often face uncertainty about how much of the freight rate is retained by brokers as a commission. Without transparency, carriers may be underpaid relative to the market value of their services.
 2. Reduced Trust in the Supply Chain
Opaque practices create skepticism and conflict between brokers and carriers, undermining the trust necessary for efficient operations.
 3. Market Inefficiencies
When carriers lack accurate rate information, they cannot make informed decisions about pricing, leading to inefficiencies such as deadheading or underutilized capacity.
 4. Financial Instability for Carriers
Small and medium-sized carriers, which comprise the majority of the trucking industry, are particularly vulnerable to unpredictable and unclear payment structures.

Benefits of Broker Transparency for Motor Carriers
 1. Fair Compensation
Transparency ensures that carriers receive equitable compensation for their services. When carriers understand the breakdown of freight rates, they are better positioned to negotiate fair terms.
 2. Improved Operational Planning
Access to clear rate data allows carriers to make informed decisions about route planning, load prioritization, and fleet management.
 3. Strengthened Trust and Collaboration
Transparent dealings foster stronger relationships between brokers and carriers, paving the way for long-term partnerships.
 4. Enhanced Industry Reputation
A transparent marketplace benefits all stakeholders by promoting fairness and professionalism, ultimately enhancing the industry’s reputation.
 5. Regulatory Compliance
Brokers who adopt transparent practices are more likely to comply with FMCSA regulations, reducing the risk of disputes and penalties.

Case Studies

1. Carrier A and a Transparent Broker

Carrier A, a small fleet operator, partnered with a broker offering full rate transparency. By accessing detailed freight rate breakdowns, Carrier A was able to identify profitable routes and negotiate better terms. This resulted in a 15% increase in profitability over six months.

2. Broker B’s Transparency Initiative

Broker B implemented a policy of disclosing commission rates and providing carriers with access to transaction records. This initiative reduced disputes by 30% and led to a significant increase in carrier satisfaction, improving Broker B’s reputation in the industry.

Recommendations for Implementing Broker Transparency
 1. Adopt Digital Platforms
Leverage technology to share rate information, transaction records, and payment details with carriers in real-time.
 2. Establish Clear Policies
Develop and communicate transparent policies regarding rate breakdowns, commissions, and additional charges.
 3. Educate Stakeholders
Provide training and resources for brokers and carriers to understand the benefits of transparency and how to implement it effectively.
 4. Leverage Third-Party Audits
Use independent audits to ensure compliance with transparency standards and foster accountability.
 5. Encourage Industry Collaboration
Work with industry associations to create standardized best practices. 

Conclusion

Broker transparency is not just an ethical imperative but a practical necessity for the trucking industry. It ensures fair compensation for motor carriers, fosters trust, and drives market efficiencies. By embracing transparency, brokers can position themselves as trusted partners, contributing to a more sustainable and equitable industry.

The path forward requires collective effort from all stakeholders—brokers, carriers, shippers, and regulators. Through collaboration and the adoption of transparent practices, the trucking industry can achieve greater resilience and success.

Call to Action

Motor carriers and brokers alike must advocate for greater transparency. Carriers should demand clear rate breakdowns, and brokers should proactively disclose relevant information. Together, these efforts will transform the trucking industry into a more equitable and efficient marketplace.

As a deregulated free market, logistics depends on multiple facets to determine market rates. Freight volume, availability of carriers (trucks and drivers), raw materials cost, end product cost and labor costs are all involved in determining market rates. This is simple supply and demand at the end of the day, the cornerstone of economics. When there is too much freight and not enough carriers to cover it, market rates rise. However, when there is a freight shortage, the lowest bidder wins the contract, lowering freight rates. Regulating the logistics industry through freight broker transparency would create more volatility and hostility between brokerages and shipping companies. Small businesses are the cornerstone of the American economy and a part of the American dream and general American values. Nearly 50% of private sector employees in the United States are employed by small business and they rely on the success of these businesses. This issue of "Broker Transparency" is asking for the Federal Government to violate its granted authority and destroy confidential documentation, violate trade secrets, and make public intellectual property. Chevron Doctrine was defeated in July, yet Carriers are asking these regulatory agencies to completely overstep their given authority and breach contracts, breach NDAs, and make public private companies contractually protected information that they have crafted over years of hard work.

The issue of broker transparency has become a focal point in the transportation industry again recently, especially as discussions continue around the FMCSA’s role in regulating broker rate disclosures under 49 CFR 371.3. This ongoing debate, echoing recent challenges similar to those faced by the FTC with non-compete bans, raises critical questions about the limits of regulatory authority and contract protections. Addressing these nuances can shed light on the delicate balance between legal obligations and industry practices.

In recent months, calls for “Broker Transparency” have intensified, with many carriers pushing for regulations that would require brokers to disclose the rates they receive from shippers. The primary legal basis for this debate? 49 CFR 371.3, which addresses records in brokered transactions. 

But what’s at the core here? It’s about understanding the nuances of contract law. In any legal dispute, precise verbiage reigns supreme, especially when it comes to contract disputes. Let’s dive into what that means for our industry.

The Three-Way Partnership in Brokered Transactions 

Whenever there’s a brokered load, three parties are involved, backed by two distinct contractual agreements: 

1. Client-Broker Agreement: This is a primary contract where shippers engage brokers, often with NDAs to protect sensitive information, including negotiated rates. This is a contractual agreement between a client hiring a broker to act at their transportation consultants and managers. Brokers not only deal with domestic truck drivers through this arrangement, but customs, ports, parcel shippers, LTL shipments, and anything else the client requests. How or what Brokers are paid for these contracted services is irrelevant. These relationships can take months or years to cultivate, and many more years to grow.

2. Broker-Carrier Agreement: The second contract, is where a broker subcontracts a carrier to transport the load. This is the broker acting as the physical extension of the client's transportation department, on their behalf. 

In legal terms, a subcontracting agreement generally doesn’t override a primary contractual agreement. And here’s where 371.3 comes into play: Section C specifies that “each party to a brokered transaction has the right to review the record of the transaction.” It’s crucial to understand that this refers to the brokered transaction itself — the agreement between broker and carrier. The regulation does not extend to the client-broker agreement, nor does it mandate disclosure of the client’s original rate with the broker. 

This isn’t accidental wording. “Brokered transaction” means the dealings between broker and carrier. It does not imply visibility into the “Arranged,” “Managed,” or “Client-Broker” contract. Most "Client" or "Shipper" Contracts are annual and renew in perpetuity, they are not "transactional". Demanding brokers disclose the original client-broker agreements would not only breach these contracts but would also infringe on NDAs — areas the FMCSA lacks authority over, especially given recent shifts in Chevron Deference protections.

 
The Role and Responsibility of Brokers 

Let’s also address the backlash against brokers. The truth is, brokers are often the bridge that keeps smaller carriers and owner-operators in business. Brokers carry significant insurance, often above and beyond what carriers have, providing clients a level of risk mitigation they wouldn’t have otherwise. In many cases, brokers function as a transportation department, relieving clients from managing these operations directly. 

Without brokers, we risk eliminating opportunities for smaller fleets and owner-operators who rely on them for a consistent workflow. Instead of tearing each other down, we should recognize the critical role each party plays in this three-way partnership.

In closing, the transportation industry thrives on collaboration. Addressing these challenges requires a commitment from all parties—shippers, brokers, and carriers alike—to move beyond disputes and toward mutual respect and sustainable practices. When we work together, we build a stronger, more resilient industry capable of weathering economic shifts and regulatory changes. It’s only through unity and shared purpose that we can drive lasting success for everyone involved

Dwight Young
Head of Freight Agency at USA Logistics

The following is why I am opposed to the Broker rate Transparency:

The FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers.
The U.S. Supreme Court overruled the Chevron deference decision, which brings in legal concerns of this rulemaking.

Opposition to Proposed Broker Rate Transparency Regulations (371.3 Expansion)

The expansion of 371.3 is based on an outdated and overly burdensome regulation that represents government overreach. This rule, originally formalized in 1980, no longer reflects the realities of the modern freight market. At that time, brokers functioned as commissioned sales agents for motor carriers, which is a far cry from today’s marketplace dynamics.

The regulation was originally designed to ensure financial transparency to prevent illegal freight rebating—a practice where shippers and brokers shared common ownership. However, the current market consists of two separate, independent transactions:

Between the shipper and the broker, and
Between the broker and the carrier.
The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) clearly stated its intent when addressing broker regulations: “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.” Expanding 371.3 would impose unnecessary restrictions that run contrary to this goal.

Key Points of Opposition
No Demonstrated Problem Exists:

During the height of the COVID-19 pandemic, when tensions around broker transparency were at their peak, no significant complaints were recorded in the FMCSA consumer complaint database.
This proposed expansion is a solution in search of a problem, as the transportation marketplace is already highly transparent. Loadboards and advanced pricing tools provide carriers with ample visibility into current market rates.
Costly Technology Burden:

Achieving electronic submission of rate details safely would require EDI or API integrations. These integrations come at an estimated cost of $2,500 to $10,000 per implementation, which would unfairly burden motor carriers. Many small carriers, already facing financial challenges, cannot absorb these unnecessary costs.
Potential Legal Violations:

Requiring brokers to disclose pricing information raises concerns under the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, which protects sensitive pricing and business data.
Forcing this level of transparency could expose brokers' proprietary business information, damaging competition in the marketplace and ultimately driving up costs.
Better Focus for FMCSA:

The FMCSA's focus should remain on critical issues, such as highway safety and addressing the freight fraud epidemic that costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually.
The FMCSA already has sufficient tools in place, such as the prohibition of coercion rule, to protect motor carriers from unfair practices.
Legal Concerns Post-Chevron Decision:

The U.S. Supreme Court’s recent decision overturning the Chevron deference introduces new legal uncertainties for this rulemaking process. Expanding 371.3 without clear justification or demonstrated need opens the FMCSA to legal challenges that could further delay necessary progress in addressing real issues within the freight market.
Conclusion
The current market is both functional and transparent, supported by existing tools like loadboards, technology pricing platforms, and legal protections. Imposing outdated and costly broker rate transparency requirements will do more harm than good—particularly for small carriers and brokers. Instead of burdening the supply chain with unnecessary regulations, the FMCSA should focus on tackling freight fraud and ensuring highway safety, where its efforts will have a meaningful impact.

Dear Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA),

I am writing to express my support for the enforcement of greater transparency between freight brokers and truckers, as outlined in the recent notice of proposed rulemaking. The trucking industry faces significant challenges due to the current lack of transparency, which impacts the fairness and efficiency of the transportation system.

**Current Issues:**
- **Lack of Access to Transaction Records:** Under existing regulations (49 CFR 371.3), carriers have the "right to review the record of the transaction," yet this right is seldom exercised due to brokers employing contract clauses to waive this right or making records inaccessible.
- **Economic Impact:** Without transparency, truckers often operate at a financial disadvantage, not knowing the full details of the charges and payments involved in their transactions. This can lead to carriers accepting rates that do not reflect the true value of their services, affecting their profitability and sustainability.

**Proposed Changes and Their Importance:**
- **Electronic Recordkeeping:** Requiring brokers to maintain records electronically is a critical step. It would facilitate quicker, remote access to these records, reducing the logistical barriers currently faced by carriers who might need to travel to inspect physical records.
 
- **Mandatory Disclosure:** The proposal to require brokers to provide an electronic copy of transaction records within 48 hours after a request is essential. This would ensure that carriers can promptly address any discrepancies or claims without the delays that currently exist.

- **Regulatory Obligation:** By reframing transparency as a regulatory duty rather than an optional right, FMCSA would establish a clearer expectation for brokers to comply. This change could foster a more equitable business environment where carriers are better informed and protected.

**Recommendations for Implementation:**
- **Enforcement:** Strong enforcement mechanisms should be put in place to ensure compliance. This includes penalties for non-compliance and a system for carriers to report violations easily and effectively.

- **Clarification on Waivers:** The rule should explicitly prohibit clauses in contracts that waive a carrier's right to transparency. This would close the loophole that has allowed brokers to circumvent existing regulations.

- **Public Education:** FMCSA should launch educational campaigns to inform both carriers and brokers about the new transparency rules, ensuring all parties understand their rights and responsibilities.

- **Feedback Loop:** Establish a platform for ongoing feedback from industry stakeholders to refine and adjust the transparency rules based on real-world application and outcomes.

**Conclusion:**
Enhancing transparency in broker-carrier transactions is not only about fairness but also about promoting an efficient, trustworthy, and competitive market. It would enable carriers to make informed decisions, potentially leading to better rate negotiations, fewer disputes, and an overall healthier supply chain ecosystem. I urge the FMCSA to continue with these rulemaking efforts and to commit to robust enforcement to ensure these changes are not just theoretical improvements but practical benefits for the trucking community.

Thank you for considering these points. I look forward to seeing these regulations come into effect, contributing to a more transparent and equitable industry.

Sincerely,

A concerned citizen & advocate. 

Sincerely,
Carmen A. Love
(The National Owner Operation Association)

Carriers are calling for complete transparency between brokers and carriers regarding the costs and payments of loads. We believe all parties involved in managing and selling loads should have access to the same information. Carriers bear the bulk of the liability for the loads, yet we are often unfairly compensated while brokers take a disproportionate share of the profits. When we advocate for fairer rates, we face risks such as losing freight assignments or being placed on exclusion lists like the "carrier 411" or DNU lists. These punitive actions jeopardize our performance and can even force carriers out of business. 
 It is unfair for brokers to claim a truck shortage to justify bringing in drivers on visas who are not subject to paying taxes. As a result, hundreds of carriers are being forced to halt operations or shut down their businesses due to the unsustainably low rates being offered. Operating a trucking business under these conditions is nearly impossible, as carriers bear the majority of the financial burden, including expenses for insurance, fuel, permits, and maintenance, among other responsibilities. At the very least, full transparency is essential to ensure a fair and equitable industry.

The FMCSA attempt to require market participants to reveal private business information is a broad overreach and a misinterpretation of the breadth of FMCSA’s authority.

Some of the statutes that FMCSA cites as the basis for its authority likely do not authorize required disclosure to private third parties. And while there may be a broad grant of authority, the ICC and FMCSA have not regulated broker charges and have repeatedly recognized that brokers benefit both shippers and carriers. Additionally, Congress deregulated the trucking industry with an intent to rely on market forces, which makes it peculiar for FMCSA to now find it has authority to interfere with a broker’s commercial relations.

The government does not usually require industry participants to share proprietary and trade secret information with commercial counter parties with which it will likely negotiate in the future.

This lack of information about another party’s profit margin is not unique to brokerage, indeed, it is not unique to transportation. Just as a shipper has no idea what a carrier’s margin might be, a party buying a pair of shoes at Walmart has no idea how much it cost the manufacturer to make the shoe, or how much Walmart is marking up the shoe. There is no law that requires Walmart to disclose its mark-up between what it paid the manufacturer and what it is selling the shoe for. There is no reason to treat a broker differently. Nor is there any reason to treat a broker differently than a motor carrier that might contract with a shipper directly.

The rule as proposed will put brokers at risk of having to choose between regulatory compliance or breaching commercial contracts with their customers.

Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of 410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect trade secrets.

Broker transparency is beneficial for the trucking industry because it promotes fairness, trust, and efficiency in the supply chain. Transparency allows carriers to see how much the shipper is paying the broker and how much of that is being passed on to the carrier. This helps ensure carriers receive a fair share of the shipping rate. Transparency fosters trust between brokers and carriers, encouraging long-term partnerships. Brokers who offer fair and transparent terms are more likely to attract quality carriers, driving up standards across the industry. The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) mandates certain transparency requirements under 49 CFR § 371.3, ensuring that brokers maintain and disclose transaction records upon request. Carriers can make better business decisions when they understand the full financial picture of each load. Broker transparency ultimately leads to a healthier trucking ecosystem where all parties—brokers, carriers, and shippers—can thrive.

I would like to state my support for the proposed broker transparency regulation.

I have been in a job connected to the trucking industry for 22 years, and since the beginning, this has been an issue that has caused huge problems for those owner-operators who interact with brokers.

Right now, the need for transparency is greater than ever. Scam brokers are hiring drivers to move freight, simply keeping everything paid by the shipper and then disappearing. Truckers who often operate on razor-thin margins suddenly are faced with carrying all of the costs associated with moving the freight, but no money to pay those expenses.

We learned during the pandemic the importance of truckers in the supply chain, and the vital nature of that service in relation to bringing all of us our most basic necessities. This represents an ongoing disruption to that system, and has the potential to substantially affect it in a negative way.

But it’s more than scam brokers – be it the kind I described or some other scheme. I’ve talked with one trucker after another who have picked up, hauled and delivered a load successfully who – when they receive payment – find themselves subjected to all kinds of “fees” and “penalties” that do nothing more than enrich the broker further at the expense of the trucker. 

Transparency is not a magic bullet to solving any of those problems. But it is something that will help level the playing field; it will give truckers another tool they can use to ensure they’re not being either straight up ripped off or simply subjected to bogus fees that chip away at their profitability till none of it is left.

None of these proposals put forward by FMCSA represent any significant burden on brokers. In the 21st century, there is little if any excuse for files failing to be in electronic format; email is nothing new, it’s easy to use and universally available; it takes virtually no time to send information by email, so 48 hours should be a more than reasonable period to do so; and brokers have already been required to provide this information to truckers since the 1980s – they simply found ways to avoid it, and use their superior bargaining power in the transaction to force truckers to give up that right – and the process, making a mockery of the concept of having a “right.”

I wish the proposed rule went further. Black-balling owner-operators who insist on their rights is an inherently unfair business activity, but yet is practiced regularly. It should be forbidden by regulation. Likewise, the contract provisions many brokers insist on – basically compelling truckers to “waive” their rights under regulation should be regarded as an unfair business tactic by any reasonable observer. Yet it is omnipresent.

Why can brokers get a waiver to avoid their duties under regulation? Truckers can’t simply get a waiver to violate the Hours of Service or any other regulation. 

And for those who try to say that one is safety and the other is not, I have to disagree. There is a clear nexus in trucking between income and safety, and it defies common sense to insist there is not.

causes a safe driver to go out of business, and that driver is replaced by someone with little to no experience. Statistics say there’s an obvious end game to that happening, and it is not increased safety. And if a trucker is compelled into one business transaction after another that cripples their profitability, who are they to pay for new tires? For brake maintenance? 

Most trucker prioritize that maintenance, and prioritize safety overall, but some do so by pulling resources from the very reason they have a job – and that is the money they spend to support their families, to buy food, clothing, shelter and other basics of life.

It’s time for brokers to simply give truckers the information those owner-operators already have a right to. I applaud the agency for taking this important step toward a solution, and look forward to the results.

Subject: Support for Broker Transparency Regulation (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257)

Dear FMCSA,

The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) has proposed new broker transparency regulations in response to longstanding concerns from carriers and organizations such as OOIDA and SBTC. These changes aim to address issues of unfair broker practices, including opaque rate structures and delayed access to transaction records. Transparency is critical for ensuring carriers, especially small operators, are fairly compensated and can thrive in a competitive industry.

This public comment reflects my strong support for these changes and offers recommendations. Below are my comments and suggestions for further strengthening the rule to address critical challenges truckers face daily:

1. Right to Fair Compensation
Transparency is essential to ensure carriers receive a fair share of freight payments. Rising costs, including fuel and maintenance, demand that brokers disclose their margins. This will restore fairness and prevent exploitation.

2. Timely Access to Records
The proposed 48-hour rule for providing transaction records is a step forward. However, FMCSA must establish clear enforcement mechanisms to ensure brokers comply, including penalties for delays or refusal to share records.

3. Addressing Power Imbalances
Brokers often control pricing and pressure carriers to avoid transparency requests. Mandatory record sharing will level the playing field and prevent rate manipulation, creating a fairer marketplace.

4. Combating Freight Fraud
Transparency is a powerful tool to combat fraud, especially with rising incidents of unethical intermediaries. Electronic recordkeeping and quick disclosures will help carriers identify and address fraudulent practices.

5. Enforcement of Non-Compliance
Existing rules lack effective enforcement. FMCSA should define penalties for brokers who fail to provide transaction records or attempt to circumvent regulations. Strong enforcement is critical for accountability.

6. Support for Small Carriers
Small carriers and owner-operators are the most vulnerable to broker opacity. Increased transparency will empower them to make informed decisions and negotiate better, fostering a more sustainable trucking industry.

7. Confidentiality Concerns
Brokers’ concerns over confidentiality should not override the need for transparency. FMCSA should focus on disclosing rate information relevant to carriers without compromising sensitive business strategies.

8. Access to Payment Information
Requiring brokers to disclose payment timelines and deductions from shippers to carriers will enhance visibility and help carriers identify and address delays or unjust deductions.

9. Electronic Recordkeeping Standards
Support mandatory electronic recordkeeping to simplify access but suggest FMCSA provide clear guidelines on acceptable formats to reduce compliance costs and confusion.

10. Anti-Retaliation Safeguards
Carriers often face retaliation for requesting transaction records. FMCSA must include protections against retaliation to ensure carriers can exercise their rights without fear of losing business.

I appreciate FMCSA’s effort to improve broker transparency and ensure fairness in the industry. Implementing these changes will enhance accountability, reduce fraud, and empower carriers, particularly small businesses, to thrive in an equitable market.

Thank you for considering my input.

Sincerely,
Jody Weintraub

Hello. As a small business motor carrier, I support the FMCSA Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on Broker Transparency Docket ID No. FMCSA-2023-0257. For years, many freight brokers have concealed crucial information about loads tendered to small business truckers. While as a motor carrier, operating in a free market, and competing with other carriers bidding against the available freight, I have my bottom line and ultimately, it's my responsibility to choose the loads that work best for me, myself and the other motor carriers bidding on the same loads are at a disadvantage to make informed decisions when brokers withhold information that makes it difficult, if not impossible, to make good decisions about the loads we haul. For example, in the past couple of years, spot market rates have been insanely low. Unfortunately, in addition to a weak economy and high inflation, fraud in the trucking industry is at an all time high, up by over 400%! A good portion of this fraud is due to double brokering. A shady broker/trucking company takes a load from a legitimate broker, posing as a carrier with a truck to pick up the load. Instead, the shady company posts the load on one (or many) of the available online load boards that connect carriers to brokers with freight and double brokers the load to a small business trucker, like myself. The trucker delivers the load and submits his invoice, signed bill of lading (proof of delivery) to the broker they got the load from. The shady brokers submits this proof of delivery and their invoice to the original broker as the carrier who delivered the load. Unbeknownst to the original broker that the load was double brokered, they pay the shady outfit for the delivery. The shady outfit never pays the carrier who actually delivered the load. This kind of fraud can be avoided by transparency regulations that would require brokers to include all the information on rate confirmation sheets. This information should include the total shipper charges, whose paying the broker if not the shipper (parent company for example), how much the broker is receiving (standard is 8 to 10%), and the net amount being paid to the carrier who delivers the load. This way, carriers can be assured the load they are hauling is not double brokered, and the amount they are being paid is honest and fair. There are brokers who have gouged truckers by taking 50, 60% or more of the freight rate the shipper is paying because they are not required to provide crucial information about the total freight charges. I don't haul excessively cheap freight, so I'm not nearly as concerned about that as I am the double brokering fraud. Full transparency should boost small business truckers bottom lines as they will be more able to fairly compete with access to full information, and it will encourage broker honesty in their transactions. Transparency will not hinder the bottom line of honest brokers, and should go a long ways towards shutting down the fraudulent brokers who are scamming small business truckers. Thank you!

FMCSA-2023-0257-0001
Expansion of 371.3 is based on an outdated, burdensome and government overreach.
Formalized in 1980 when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, not how the market currently operates.
The intent was to have financial transparency between all parties to a brokered load to ensure the illegal practice of freight rebating was not occurring, where a broker and shipper were common owners.
The current marketplace involves two separate business transactions between the shipper and broker and the broker and carrier.
The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) made it clear in its intent of this regulation, “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace."
During the COVID-19 pandemic, when this issue was at its height, there were no complaints in the consumer complaint database. 
This is a solution in search of a problem. 
The transportation marketplace is already very transparent with loadboards and other technology pricing tools. 
The only safe way to achieve electronic submission safely would be through an EDI or API integration, at the cost of roughly $2,500 - $10,000 per integration. Motor carriers would bear the cost of this as well. 
Possible violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 which includes pricing and business information. 
FMCSA should focus on highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic that is costing the supply-chain over $1 billion annually. 
The FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers.
The U.S. Supreme Court overruled the Chevron deference decision, which brings in legal concerns of this rulemaking.

Brokers needs to take accountability and responsibility. As the carrier picks up the freight, broker in turn washes his hands of all responsibility. In current transactions the Broker is not responsible for damages, overages, shortages, or carrier pay. At times problems arise, carrier tries his hardest to mitigate those problems. After the load is delivered , Broker says "customer did not approve detention" or "customer deducted money for damages" or "we need the lumper receipts for reimbursement(in case carrier lost the receipt)". These situations can lead to Carrier actually owing money to the broker. Example: original rate $1000, carrier has damages worth $800(according to broker), so the carrier can not file a claim with insurance due to a high deductible. However the load might have been contracted by the broker for $1500, $2000, in some cases even $2500. In this example the "damage" could have been covered by the broker or split up between broker/carrier. Which in turn would actually allow for the carrier to not loose his shirt, since the cost of the fuel and truck are astronomical. If both broker and carrier would see the transport value, then the transaction could be more fair. 
Second example is detention pay: If a carrier shows up to get loaded for a scheduled appointment , the driver might sit and wait for 4, 5, 7 hours to get loaded or unloaded. This prevents the driver from driving to his/her next load due to E-log hours of service restrictions. After the fact a broker can say that the customer did not approve detention. The carrier does not deal with the customer and is relying for the broker to be truthful. The customer might not have approved detention, however the customer gives a flat rate which might of been much higher then the carrier received. Therefore the broker could of made a smaller profit , but paid the carrier a compensation for long loading/unloading. If the contract rate was visible then both parties could agree on fair compensation. 
In general, most brokers do not own assets. However, somehow they are dictating rates for the industry. Some brokers make outrageous profit of 30%, 50% even 100% on a load which is completely handled by the carriers. While some brokers intentionally loose 10%, 20% on loads to bring in customers. As a result , a false market emerges. There is no shortage of carriers or drivers. There is definitely a false sense of reality. A transparency of the freight rates would definitely open up everyone's eyes on the real picture, at which point better business decisions could be made.

FMCSA-2023-0257-0001

 Expansion of 371.3 is based on an outdated, burdensome and government overreach.
 Formalized in 1980 when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, not how the market currently operates.
 The intent was to have financial transparency between all parties to a brokered load to ensure the illegal practice of freight rebating was not occurring, where a broker and shipper were common owners.
 The current marketplace involves two separate business transactions between the shipper and broker and the broker and carrier.
 The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) made it clear in its intent of this regulation, “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace."
 During the COVID-19 pandemic, when this issue was at its height, there were no complaints in the consumer complaint database. 
 This is a solution in search of a problem. 
 The transportation marketplace is already very transparent with loadboards and other technology pricing tools. 
 The only safe way to achieve electronic submission safely would be through an EDI or API integration, at the cost of roughly $2,500 - $10,000 per integration. Motor carriers would bear the cost of this as well. 
 Possible violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 which includes pricing and business information. 
 FMCSA should focus on highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic that is costing the supply-chain over $1 billion annually. 
 The FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers.
 The U.S. Supreme Court overruled the Chevron deference decision, which brings in legal concerns of this rulemaking.
Yes transparency is 100% needed. These shady practices of brokers needs to stop.

To Whom It May Concern,

My name is Kevin Wallack. I am the Director of Risk for R2 Logistics in Jacksonville, FL. I have been a risk manager for the entirety of my 15-year career in freight and I have worked on both the carrier side of the business and the brokerage side. Thus I feel uniquely positioned to comment on this issue with duel perspective. 

This regulation, which governs financial transparency in brokered transactions, was formalized in 1980 and is outdated, overly burdensome, and constitutes government overreach. I strongly urge the FMCSA to reconsider this proposal based on the following points:

Outdated Foundation: The original intent of §371.3 was to ensure financial transparency when brokers acted as commissioned sales agents of motor carriers. This model does not reflect how the modern freight market operates, where transactions between shippers, brokers, and carriers are conducted as separate and distinct business agreements.

Historical Intent: The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC), which established this regulation, stated its goal was to “remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.” Expanding this regulation contradicts this intent and could harm the efficient functioning of today’s transportation marketplace.

Marketplace Transparency: The transportation industry is already highly transparent, with load boards and other technology pricing tools readily available to carriers. This proposed rule appears to be a solution in search of a problem, as there were no complaints recorded in the consumer complaint database during the COVID-19 pandemic when rate transparency concerns were at their peak.

Economic and Security Implications:

Implementing electronic submission of transaction data would require expensive EDI or API integrations, costing $2,500 to $10,000 per integration. These costs would ultimately fall on motor carriers, adding unnecessary financial strain.

Mandating such disclosures risks violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, which protects sensitive pricing and business information. Such regulations could undermine the competitive landscape of the industry.

Misaligned Priorities: The FMCSA’s primary focus should be on highway safety and addressing pressing issues like the growing freight fraud pandemic, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually. Expanding §371.3 distracts from these critical priorities.

Existing Protections: The FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers, making additional transparency mandates redundant and unnecessary.

Legal Concerns: The recent U.S. Supreme Court ruling overturning the Chevron deference decision raises significant legal questions about the validity of this rulemaking. Expanding §371.3 without careful legal consideration could lead to unnecessary litigation.

In conclusion, expanding 49 CFR §371.3 would impose significant burdens on brokers and carriers without addressing any documented problems. I urge the FMCSA to focus its efforts on improving highway safety and combating freight fraud rather than implementing unnecessary and outdated regulatory measures.

Thank you for considering these comments. I am available to discuss this matter further if needed.

Sincerely,
-Kevin Wallack

I am writing to express my appreciation for the efforts made by the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) to promote transparency within the freight brokerage industry. As a vital link between shippers and carriers, freight brokers play a critical role in the logistics and transportation sector. However, this intermediary position can sometimes lead to challenges regarding transparency and trust.

In recent years, the freight brokerage landscape has evolved, and the need for clear, honest communication has become more essential than ever. Deceptive practices can undermine relationships between carriers and shippers, potentially leading to increased costs, delays, and a general lack of confidence in the services provided. Consequently, initiatives aimed at enhancing transparency are not just beneficial—they are necessary.

The FMCSA’s commitment to enforcing regulations that promote accountability among freight brokers is commendable. By implementing measures that require brokers to disclose key information, such as fees and service terms, you are fostering a more equitable environment that benefits all parties involved. This transparency not only builds trust but also empowers shippers and carriers to make informed decisions.

Furthermore, as the industry continues to adapt to technological advancements, I encourage the FMCSA to explore additional avenues for promoting transparency. The integration of digital platforms that allow real-time tracking of shipments, clear communication channels, and accessible contracts can significantly enhance the broker-carrier-shipper relationship.

I appreciate the FMCSA's ongoing commitment to ensuring fair practices in the transportation industry. Your efforts to hold freight brokers accountable and encourage transparency will ultimately contribute to a more efficient and trustworthy logistics ecosystem.

Thank you for your attention to this important issue. I look forward to seeing further developments in this area and am hopeful for continued progress in ensuring transparency within freight brokerage.

Subject: Concerned Interest in Freight Broker Transparency and Industry Regulation

Dear Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration,

I am writing to express my concern regarding the lack of transparency in the freight brokerage industry and to urge the FMCSA to implement stronger regulatory measures. The current framework often leaves motor carriers and other stakeholders at a disadvantage, creating an environment where opaque practices can thrive and fairness is not guaranteed.

Freight brokers play a critical role in the transportation and logistics ecosystem, but their operations frequently lack sufficient oversight. A lack of transparency in transactions often results in unclear payment structures, diminished trust among stakeholders, and, in some cases, reduced earnings for motor carriers who bear the operational burdens of moving freight.

To address these issues, I believe it is essential for the FMCSA to consider the following measures:

1. Enhanced Transparency Requirements: Freight brokers should be mandated to provide clear and accessible details about the financial transactions tied to freight shipments, including the gross revenue of each shipment.

2. Regulation of Broker Fees: Establishing guidelines for permissible fee structures would help ensure brokers are not unfairly profiting at the expense of carriers.

3. Strengthened Enforcement Mechanisms: The FMCSA should enhance its capacity to investigate and address noncompliance with existing regulations, ensuring that bad actors are held accountable.

4. Promoting Technological Solutions: Encouraging the use of digital tools for transparent load tracking and payment processing could help improve accountability across the board.

Ensuring a more transparent and equitable freight brokerage industry will not only benefit motor carriers but also strengthen the overall reliability and efficiency of our nation’s supply chain. I appreciate the FMCSA's continued efforts to maintain fairness and safety in the transportation industry and urge the agency to take further action in this area.

Thank you for your attention to this important matter. I look forward to seeing the FMCSA's progress on these issues.

•Would more broker transparency help your business? Yes, as an Owner Operator transparency is important to my business to prevent fraud and bad business deals.

• Is it fair that brokers can willingly evade regulations without enforcement? No, it is not fair. Brokers can easily report carriers for the smallest and silliest things but brokers can get away with anything and nothing is ever done. Carriers are always at fault for everything.

• Have you experienced brokers’ denying your rights to view transaction information? Actually, I did not know I could even ask for such information. 

• Have you been blacklisted by brokers for requesting information? Would the proposal encourage or discourage blacklisting practices? I have never requested information from a broker before. I do think it would encourage brokers to blacklist carriers that request the information but there needs to be a regulation put on that to prevent that from happening.

• What impact would these changes have on freight rates? I believe the rates on freight would improve greatly because the brokers could not be able to lie about their margins anymore.

• Do you believe that the 48-hour timeframe proposed for 371.3(c) would create a substantial burden for brokers? No, the 48-hour time frame would not be a burden on brokers. They request paperwork from carriers within 24-hours or deduct from our rate. 

• If this proposal effectively reduced instances of illegal brokering, would more bad brokers exit the market? Yes, most definitely. It would prevent double brokering by the brokers. They always go after the carriers about double brokering but what about the brokers that do it. 

• Should carriers requesting records under 371.3(c) be required to submit their request in writing? I think a simple email would be sufficient. A lot of Owner Operators do not have someone sitting behind a desk to type a formal letter for them while they are on the road.

• Should parties requesting records under 371.3(c) be required to submit their request electronically? Would requiring a specific format for submitted requests impose a cost on the parties or otherwise deter requests for transparency? I think an email to a specific person within the company should be sufficient. That person should be clearly stated on the rate cons and websites of the brokers. This would not impose an additional cost; the brokers can simply appoint someone already at the company. Another option is to require a third party be responsible for this assure complete transparency.

• Would the proposal that records be provided electronically make broker transparency more likely, as compared to not specifying a method of provided the records? Should the Agency be more specific in requiring a particular format for records? What method and/or format is preferable? Yes, they can just send the records via email. Just a simple PDF document would work across the board for everyone. Carriers and Brokers already utilize PDF documents so this would not impose any additional cost or knowledge.

December 19, 2024

U.S. Department of Transportation
Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, DC 20590

Subject: Response to Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on Broker Transparency (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257)

Dear FMCSA Rulemaking Team,

We are writing to express our opposition to the proposed expansion of 49 CFR 371.3. This rule is based on outdated principles, imposes unnecessary burdens, and represents government overreach.

The original intent of 49 CFR 371.3 was to ensure financial transparency between all parties involved in a brokered load, specifically to prevent the illegal practice of freight rebating where a broker and shipper were common owners. However, the current marketplace operates with two distinct business transactions: one between the shipper and broker, and another between the broker and carrier.

The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) clearly stated its intent with this regulation: “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.” This proposed expansion contradicts that goal by introducing new, unnecessary restrictions.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, when concerns about transparency were at their peak, there were no complaints recorded in the consumer complaint database. This indicates that the proposed rule is a solution in search of a problem. The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, thanks to load boards, rate tools, research, and other pricing technology. 

Additionally, the proposed rule could lead to potential violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, as it involves the sharing of sensitive pricing and business information. Instead of focusing on this unnecessary regulation, the FMCSA should prioritize highway safety and address the fraud crisis including dramatic increases in cargo theft, identity theft, and double brokering. This lack of regulation impacts broker, carrier, and shipper operations and revenues, and cost the supply chain over $1 billion annually.

In closing, we urge the FMCSA to reconsider this proposed rule and focus on measures that truly enhance safety and efficiency in the transportation industry without imposing undue burdens on businesses and motor carriers. FMCSA’s Registration Modernization efforts is an example of this and a great step in the right direction. In the interim, we will continue to operate with integrity and respect and build professional relationships with carriers and shippers.

Thank you for your consideration. 

Sincerely, 
FREIGHTFEM SOLUTIONS

The rule as proposed aims to provide transparency, however the method the FMCSA uses 
to approach rate transparency is not an accurate representation of anything meaningful. 
The rule as proposed would require the broker to disclose the rate the shipper pays the 
broker to arrange the transportation and the rate at which the broker hires the carrier. The 
incorrect approach is to view the di0erence in these rates as a profit. It is not. 
Brokers continually invest in technology to combat fraud as well as ine0iciencies in the 
marketplace. Brokers invest in communication systems that make doing business more 
e0ective on both the shipper and carrier ends. 
Fraud and theft prevention is the greatest challenge to the U.S. transportation system 
today. Broker investment in and focus on fraud and theft prevention is something neither 
the shipper nor the carriers have time to solve. It is the body of good broker citizens who are 
leading the way toward solving the biggest epidemic to plague the highways. We encourage 
the FMCSA to focus its e0orts in this area and discover you have a willing marketplace 
partner to support innovation in greater freight security. 
Brokers invest in sta0 who handle critical processes in the supply chain. Brokers invest in 
insurance coverages that potentially add a layer of assurance and security to both the 
shipper and carrier, depending on the circumstance.
All of those costs, and others, remain unseen in the way the FMCSA approaches rate 
transparency. To force this disclosure requirement on brokers is unconscionable because it 
is not a fair representation of the value added to the supply chain brokers provide nor does 
truthfully represent the business costs that come with operating a business that provides 
these benefits to the market.

The government does not usually require industry participants to share proprietary and 
trade secret information with commercial counter parties with which it will likely negotiate in the future.
This lack of information about another party’s profit margin is not unique to brokerage, indeed, it is not unique to transportation. Just as a shipper has no idea what a carrier’s margin might be, a party buying a pair of shoes at Walmart has no idea how much it cost the manufacturer to make the shoe, or how much Walmart is marking up the shoe. There is no law that requires Walmart to disclose its mark-up between what it paid the manufacturer and what it is selling the shoe for. There is no reason to treat a broker differently. Nor is there any reason to treat a broker differently than a motor carrier that might contract with a shipper directly.
The rule, as proposed, will put brokers at risk of having to choose between regulatory 
compliance or breaching commercial contracts with their customers.
Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent 
expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law 
counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of 410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect trade secrets.
DTSA defines “trade secrets” as “all forms and types of financial, business . . . information, 
including . . . compilations, . . . formulas, methods, techniques, processes . . . if – (A) the 
owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the 
information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being 
generally know to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another 
person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 18 
U.S.C. § 1839(3). The type of information that the proposed rule seeks to be disclosed

The government does not usually require industry participants to share proprietary and 
trade secret information with commercial counter parties with which it will likely negotiate 
in the future.
This lack of information about another party’s profit margin is not unique to brokerage, 
indeed, it is not unique to transportation. Just as a shipper has no idea what a carrier’s 
margin might be, a party buying a pair of shoes at Walmart has no idea how much it cost 
the manufacturer to make the shoe, or how much Walmart is marking up the shoe. There is 
no law that requires Walmart to disclose its mark-up between what it paid the 
manufacturer and what it is selling the shoe for. There is no reason to treat a broker 
di0erently. Nor is there any reason to treat a broker di0erently than a motor carrier that 
might contract with a shipper directly.
The rule, as proposed, will put brokers at risk of having to choose between regulatory 
compliance or breaching commercial contracts with their customers.
Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent 
expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law 
counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly 
or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of 
410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect 
trade secrets.
DTSA defines “trade secrets” as “all forms and types of financial, business . . . information, 
including . . . compilations, . . . formulas, methods, techniques, processes . . . if – (A) the 
owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the 
information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being 
generally know to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another 
person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 18 
U.S.C. § 1839(3). The type of information that the proposed rule seeks to be disclosed 
clearly fall within the definition of a “trade secret.”

Hello; My name is Robert Johnson, and I’m an American truck driver with nearly 20 years experience. I send this letter to show my support for any actions towards the passing of the Broker Transparency bill. A large number of truck drivers and carrier owners have long been wondering how freight brokers have been given the level of power that they currently possess and we feel it’s time that this power is checked, and if necessary; reduced or eliminated. As a company driver, I’m bombarded by text messages and endless phone calls from broker representatives; nearly to the point of reporting numbers as harassment due to the fact that they refuse to leave voicemail messages. Another tactic that they have chosen to force upon us are tracking apps that we are required to install on our personal devices; nobody has offered to compensate me for such usage but they’ll penalize my employer if I do not immediately comply. It is my device; not my employers, and certainly does not belong to the freight broker… it’s mine and I should not be compelled, coerced or threatened to use it for the advantage of others. Another plague on our industry are the overall freight rates. We’re hauling their freight for next to nothing, while the brokers are building headquarters that seemingly rival museums, and we don’t believe this is fair. I will have to end my letter now, only due to my memory failing and unable to remember any other points that I wanted to touch upon; I’m sure my fellow OOIDA members will pick up my slack, in this respect. 

Merry Christmas -
 Robert H Johnson, Groveport , Ohio

I have experienced double brokering, shady brokers who will book multiple trucking companies just to see who will arrive first.
