There is no positive outcome to this being passed. Every carrier that gets upset seeing a margin they are not happy with will send it directly to the customer and either backdoor the customer for their own benefit or do whatever they can to ruin the relationship the broker has with the customer. The carriers have every right and opportunity a broker has to go out and put the work in to get their own customers. No one is forcing them to only take brokered freight, and if they do not think payment is sufficient on a load, they are not obligated to take it. I don't get to go to the local grocery store and tell them I want to pay the price they got it from their supplier...but then how would that grocery store stay in business? Nor do I get to negotiate the price I am paying at the grocery store like the carriers get to do for brokered freight they see on the load board. They get to negotiate price to a point they are comfortable running the load. What gets negotiated between the broker and the supplier or customer should have nothing to do with what the carriers are negotiating. It is a breach of privacy for them to be able to look into broker records for payment purposes, but they do not have to supply any information on their costs to operate each load...what is their profit per load? The bigger issue is freight fraud and double brokering as that costs much more per load than worrying about margin per load. Lets put our resources towards making the industry safer for all parties involved, rather than how much someone is getting paid.

Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent 
expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law 
counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly 
or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of 410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect trade secrets.

DTSA defines “trade secrets” as “all forms and types of financial, business . . . information, including . . . compilations, . . . formulas, methods, techniques, processes . . . if – (A) the 
owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally know to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 18 U.S.C. § 1839(3). The type of information that the proposed rule seeks to be disclosed clearly fall within the definition of a “trade secret.

Customer then cuts ties with the relationship with the brokerage and invites the carrier to run their loads for $2250. Customer gets upset and starts seeking out brokerages again and here we are in a revolving cycle and that said carrier received no benefit from what they had intended, other than ruining previous relationships. "No one works for free" is a common phrase said by carriers when they don't want to take a load for the price its posted for. That same phrase is argumentative for all parties involved. If they are unhappy with a price, they are not obligated to take load and are free to shop around. Carriers have every right a brokerage does to finding customers, and the customers have every right to choose who they want to work with. The contractual relationship between all parties does not need to be an open book.

margin might be, a party buying a pair of shoes at Walmart has no idea how much it cost 
the manufacturer to make the shoe, or how much Walmart is marking up the shoe. There is 
no law that requires Walmart to disclose its mark-up between what it paid the 
manufacturer and what it is selling the shoe for. There is no reason to treat a broker 
di0erently. Nor is there any reason to treat a broker di0erently than a motor carrier that 
might contract with a shipper directly.

Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent 
expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law 
counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly 
or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of 
410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect 
trade secrets.
DTSA defines “trade secrets” as “all forms and types of financial, business . . . information, 
including . . . compilations, . . . formulas, methods, techniques, processes . . . if – (A) the 
owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the 
information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being 
generally know to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another 
person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 18 
U.S.C. § 1839(3). The type of information that the proposed rule seeks to be disclosed 
clearly fall within the definition of a “trade secret.

The following is a response to the petitions calling for rulemaking changes for transparency in property broker transactions. 
I have been in business for 30 years and fear this rulemaking if passed would lead to the end of this small business that supports ten families and many like me. 
Imagine this: car dealers are forced to send to you what they paid for the car they sold you 48 hours after you bought it. You have that information, and your cousin Vinny is going to buy the same vehicle, you let Vinny know. Vinny now has an upper hand to negotiate because he knows what the dealer’s margin is. This is not how the free market works and this is what OOIDA is looking for. If this regulation were to be pushed through it would not only significantly impair brokers but would negatively affect the markets and all its players as a whole.
In a free market prices are being driven up and down by supply and demand. It is the responsibility of shippers, brokers, and carriers a like to follow the free market and understand where the equilibrium of the market is at and adjust your service prices accordingly. Brokers are offering freight to the motor carriers for a specified rate and carriers have the option to think freely for themselves on if they should accept the tender or reject. No one is forcing them to take a load.
The effect of such a change will create a negative shockwave that will be irreversible throughout the transportation industry. This regulation will only impair the transportation industry from operating as it should under free market processes. Carriers compete with brokers as well as needing them to fill lanes that they don’t have direct shippers in. They have every opportunity to set their own destinies and margins when negotiating with brokers on a spot load basis. If carriers aren’t happy with what they are being paid then they do not take the load. If we as brokers are required to show our margins-what purpose will this accomplish other than give the carriers the upper hand in future negotiations as well as open the door for carriers to give the rates to our competition or try to go directly with customers themselves(backdooring). Our rates with customers are confidential agreements with proprietary information between us and our customers. Customers look to us to assure the information doesn’t get in the hands of their competition. 

Brokers spend countless dollars and hours of effort to seek out, secure, and keep customers. It is unreasonable and unfair to think that the by forcing brokers to release how much their customer is paying them to the carriers is a fair or accurate representation of how much the brokerage acutely profits. Gross margins on a load-to-load basis is not reflective of the net profit that brokers receive. By adding this unneeded regulation it will only add additional cost to the ever growing list of expenses. To achieve what OOIDA wants it will require brokers and motor carriers alike to integrate with additional software which costs anywhere from $2,500-$10,000 per integration. If carries are so enthralled with brokerages and their margins they should consider putting the same time and effort into getting and buying a sales team, a dispatch team, buy the capital assets required, TMS, insurances, wages, health care, 401(k), bonuses, etc and they would get to see what their margins anytime they would like since they so deeply care about a percentage in which does not take into account of all the expense that occur in a transaction. 
Some concerns if the proposal is enacted are:
• Carriers will back solicit the shippers because they know the rate and undercut the brokers to directly obtain the freight. This will set up a huge number of lawsuits that will harm brokers and shippers as well as carriers involved.
• Brokers go out of business because the business model is no longer sustainable
• Small Carriers run out of options for backhauls, shippers turn to their only reliable option, the largest carriers, Owner Operators and small fleets must align themselves with a large carriers and find themselves in a worse position than before the petition
In simple terms regulation drives consolidation. Small companies like ours will be the most adversely effected. We have seen it occur in other industries and OOIDA and its members will not be no different. 
We are requesting that the FMCSA does not allow this anti-free market, ill-logical, pro-government regulation, to go any further than it has. We appreciate your consideration in the matter and look forward to a positive ruling. 
Thank you for your consideration.

Best regards

I agree with every word of this comment.
The transportation industry is already highly transparent, with load boards and technology platforms offering real-time pricing visibility to carriers and shippers.
Requiring electronic submissions through methods like EDI or API integration would impose significant financial burdens on brokers and carriers, with costs ranging from $2,500 to $10,000 per integration—expenses that smaller businesses can ill afford.

3. Lack of Supporting Evidence

During the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of heightened scrutiny, the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database did not record any complaints relevant to the issues this rule seeks to address. This proposal targets a problem that does not exist.

4. Legal and Confidentiality Concerns

Requiring the disclosure of sensitive pricing and business terms risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016.
Recent legal precedents, such as the Supreme Court’s decision to reverse the Chevron case, also cast doubt on the FMCSA’s authority to enforce such a rule.

5. Misplaced Priorities

The FMCSA should be addressing urgent challenges facing the transportation sector, such as:

Improving highway safety.

Tackling the ongoing freight fraud crisis, which costs the industry over $1 billion annually.

Developing solutions to support supply chain resilience and efficiency.

Conclusion
The FMCSA’s proposal is unnecessary, burdensome, and misaligned with the realities of today’s transportation industry. It creates significant costs for brokers and carriers while ignoring the pressing issues affecting the industry. As a motor carrier, I fully support the FMCSA's proposed rulemaking on freight broker transparency because it is an essential step toward fairness in the industry. A comparison to the real estate sector makes it clear why brokers should be required to disclose their rates and earnings, much like real estate agents must disclose their commissions to their clients.

In the real estate industry, agents work as intermediaries between buyers and sellers. However, the crucial difference is that real estate agents are required by law to disclose how much they are earning on a deal. This transparency allows buyers and sellers to understand the agent's commission and make informed decisions. Similarly, brokers act as intermediaries between the shipper and the motor carrier. The shipper agrees on a rate with the broker, and the broker then negotiates a rate with the carrier to move the freight. But, unlike real estate agents, brokers often withhold the rate they’ve negotiated with the shipper from the carrier, leaving the carrier in the dark about how much the broker is profiting from the deal. Carriers should not be allowed to legally waive our rights to see the rates brokers are charging customers.

In both cases, the intermediary (the real estate agent or the freight broker) has access to all the relevant financial information but is often not transparent with their clients. Real estate agents disclose their earnings to ensure the buyer and seller understand the full scope of the deal and any conflicts of interest. Likewise, brokers should disclose the rate they are paid by the shipper so that the carrier knows the true value of the work being done. This is essential for creating a fair and transparent business relationship.

Additionally, when real estate agents negotiate a deal, the buyer and seller both know who they are dealing with—there are no hidden parties. In the same way, when a motor carrier picks up a load, we know who we’re delivering for, and we know the customer, so why should brokers be allowed to withhold this crucial financial information? The truck driver is ultimately the one performing the service, just as the buyer or seller is the one directly impacted by the real estate transaction. Brokers should not be able to hide their profits from the carrier, as it is no different from a real estate agent hiding their commission from their client.

Furthermore, real estate agents have ethical obligations to disclose conflicts of interest and ensure their clients are not being taken advantage of in the transaction. Similarly, brokers should be held accountable for their role in ensuring fair compensation for carriers, particularly when it comes to the rates they negotiate. The current system, where brokers can operate with little transparency, can lead to unfair pricing practices and undervaluation of the services provided by carriers. This ultimately harms the entire industry and undermines the trust that is critical for efficient supply chain operations.

In addition to that, stock brokers and the broader finance industry also serve as intermediaries between their clients (the investors) and the financial markets, yet they are legally obligated to disclose their fees, commissions, and other costs to their clients. Whether it's a stock trade or investment management, financial advisors must be transparent with their clients about the rates they charge and how they are compensated. This ensures that investors can make informed decisions and avoid hidden costs or conflicts of interest. The same logic should apply to freight brokers. Just as stock brokers cannot hide their earnings from their clients, freight brokers should be required to disclose the rates they are being paid by the shipper, so motor carriers can understand the full scope of the transaction.
n both the real estate and financial sectors, transparency is a cornerstone of trust and fair practice. Buyers, sellers, and investors all have a right to know how much the intermediaries are making off a deal. Motor carriers should have the same rights in their relationships with brokers. Currently, brokers often withhold information about the rates they charge shippers, leaving carriers in the dark about how much they are actually being paid for their services. This creates an imbalance in the industry, where brokers can profit disproportionately while carriers are left without the full picture.

By requiring brokers to disclose the rates they are paid by shippers, just as real estate agents and stock brokers must disclose their commissions, we will level the playing field. This will foster a more equitable environment for carriers, ensure fair compensation, and create a more transparent freight ecosystem that benefits everyone involved.

The trucking companies that we work with know we strive to get them the most money we can for every load. That keeps them doing business with us. As a trucking company and small business owner, I find freight brokers to be valuable resources. I cannot maintain relationships with every shipper, nor am I interested in contracting with every shipping entity.

I do not want additional, non-value added administrative tasks added to the work that the brokers do for my company. These are costs to their businesses and will ultimately result in less money to my trucking company.

comment_text:
December 20, 2024

U.S. Department of Transportation
Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration
1200 New Jersey Avenue SE
Washington, DC 20590

Subject: Response to Notice of Proposed Rulemaking on Broker Transparency (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257)

Dear FMCSA Rulemaking Team,

We are writing to express our opposition to the proposed expansion of 49 CFR 371.3. This rule is based on outdated principles, imposes unnecessary burdens, and represents government overreach.

The original intent of 49 CFR 371.3 was to ensure financial transparency between all parties involved in a brokered load, specifically to prevent the illegal practice of freight rebating where a broker and shipper were common owners. However, the current marketplace operates with two distinct business transactions: one between the shipper and broker, and another between the broker and carrier.

The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) clearly stated its intent with this regulation: “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace.” This proposed expansion contradicts that goal by introducing new, unnecessary restrictions.

During the COVID-19 pandemic, when concerns about transparency were at their peak, there were no complaints recorded in the consumer complaint database. This indicates that the proposed rule is a solution in search of a problem. The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, thanks to load boards, rate tools, research, and other pricing technology.

Additionally, the proposed rule could lead to potential violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, as it involves the sharing of sensitive pricing and business information. In our humble opinion, instead of focusing on this unnecessary regulation, the FMCSA should prioritize highway safety and address the fraud crisis including dramatic increases in cargo theft, identity theft, and double brokering. This lack of regulation and oversight impacts broker, carrier, and shipper operations and revenues, and cost the supply chain over $1 billion annually.

In closing, we urge the FMCSA to reconsider this proposed rule and focus on measures that truly enhance safety and efficiency in the transportation industry without imposing undue burdens on businesses and motor carriers. FMCSA’s Registration Modernization efforts is an example of this and a great step in the right direction. In the interim, we will continue to operate with integrity and respect and build professional relationships with carriers and shippers.

Thank you for your consideration.

Sincerely,
New American Group d.b.a. American Group, LLC

Brokers continually invest in technology to combat fraud as well as ine iciencies in the 
marketplace. Brokers invest in communication systems that make doing business more 
e ective on both the shipper and carrier ends. 
Fraud and theft prevention is the greatest challenge to the U.S. transportation system 
today. Broker investment in and focus on fraud and theft prevention is something neither 
the shipper nor the carriers have time to solve. It is the body of good broker citizens who are 
leading the way toward solving the biggest epidemic to plague the highways. We encourage 
the FMCSA to focus its e orts in this area and discover you have a willing marketplace 
partner to support innovation in greater freight security. 
Brokers invest in sta who handle critical processes in the supply chain. Brokers invest in 
insurance coverages that potentially add a layer of assurance and security to both the 
shipper and carrier, depending on the circumstance. 
All of those costs, and others, remain unseen in the way the FMCSA approaches rate 
transparency. To force this disclosure requirement on brokers is unconscionable because it 
is not a fair representation of the value added to the supply chain brokers provide nor does 
truthfully represent the business costs that come with operating a business that provides 
these benefits to the market.

confidentiality that form the foundation of broker-client relationships.

This proposed rule represents an overreach of FMCSA's regulatory authority. The agency's role is to oversee safety and compliance, not to dictate private business arrangements or interfere in lawful contracts.

The proposed regulation raises significant concerns under the Commerce Clause of the
U.S. Constitution. Imposing these mandates interferes with free trade and imposes undue burdens on brokers, carriers, and shippers.

Mandating rate transparency increases the risk of fraud by exposing sensitive business
information to bad actors. Brokers serve as a critical layer of protection, and this rule jeopardizes their ability to safeguard the industry.

This regulation could enable fraudulent carriers to gain access to critical business
information, deepening the fraud pandemic already harming the industry.

This rule threatens to destabilize an already fragile freight market still recovering from the e0ects of the COVID-19 pandemic and extreme inflation. The economic consequences could harm the broader American economy.

The expansion of 371.3 disregards the evolution of the transportation industry. Its original intent in 1980 was to prevent freight rebating in a very di0erent marketplace. This rule fails to align with the current operational realities of brokers.

This proposed rule adds unnecessary restrictions that impede the free market. Even the
Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) once stated its goal was to remove such barriers
to ensure a thriving marketplace.

The rule may violate the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 by compelling the disclosure of sensitive pricing and business information, which could severely damage brokers’
competitive standing.

The Supreme Court's recent decision overturning the Chevron deference raises legal

The proposed rule undermines capitalism. Shippers and carriers have the freedom to
choose brokers or other business partners based on mutually agreed terms. Imposing

The FMCSA attempt to require market participants to reveal private business information is a broad overreach and a misinterpretation of the breadth of FMCSA’s authority.
Some of the statutes that FMCSA cites as the basis for its authority likely do not authorize required disclosure to private third parties. And while there may be a broad grant of authority, the ICC and FMCSA have not regulated broker charges and have repeatedly recognized that brokers benefit both shippers and carriers. Additionally, Congress deregulated the trucking industry with an intent to rely on market forces, which makes it peculiar for FMCSA to now find it has authority to interfere with a broker’s commercial relations.

This lack of information about another party’s profit margin is not unique to brokerage, indeed, it is not unique to transportation. Just as a shipper has no idea what a carrier’s margin might be, a party buying a pair of shoes at Walmart has no idea how much it cost the manufacturer to make the shoe, or how much Walmart is marking up the shoe. There is no law that requires Walmart to disclose its mark-up between what it paid the manufacturer and what it is selling the shoe for. There is no reason to treat a broker differently. Nor is there any reason to treat a broker differently than a motor carrier that might contract with a shipper directly.

Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent
expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law
counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of 410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect trade secrets.
DTSA defines “trade secrets” as “all forms and types of financial, business . . . information, including . . . compilations, . . . formulas, methods, techniques, processes . . . if – (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally know to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 18 U.S.C. § 1839(3). The type of information that the proposed rule seeks to be disclosed clearly fall within the definition of a “trade secret.”

The government does not usually require industry participants to share proprietary and
trade secret information with commercial counter parties with which it will likely negotiate in the future.

Benefits of using brokers

Large shippers look for brokers for efficient access to the transportation marketplace and the broker pool is an amalgamated capacity in the eyes of the shipper. The shippers and carriers need brokers who have invested in technology and people power such that they can amalgamate carrier capacity that is not otherwise available to shippers. Those investments cost money and if a broker is charging too much, the broker will be priced out because the carrier can go directly to large carriers, etc. In many cases, carriers experience a cost savings and increased revenue as a result of brokers arranging the freight. Savings that the carriers can experience include through administrative functions and other e0iciencies brokers provide to the owner/operator. Shippers and carriers both benefit from transfer of transportation risk to the broker in large part. This extra layer of broker protection also comes with a market and operational expertise that cannot be replicated by either the shipper or the carrier. The largest businesses in America utilize brokers to arrange their transportation as do smaller businesses for these reasons. 

True costs and value from utilizing brokers

The rule as proposed aims to provide transparency, however the method the FMCSA uses to approach rate transparency is not an accurate representation of anything meaningful. The rule as proposed would require the broker to disclose the rate the shipper pays the broker to arrange the transportation and the rate at which the broker hires the carrier. The incorrect approach is to view the di0erence in these rates as a profit. It is not. Brokers continually invest in technology to combat fraud as well as inefficiencies in the marketplace. Brokers invest in communication systems that make doing business more effective on both the shipper and carrier ends. Fraud and theft prevention is the greatest challenge to the U.S. transportation system today. Broker investment in and focus on fraud and theft prevention is something neither the shipper nor the carriers have time to solve. It is the body of good broker citizens who are leading the way toward solving the biggest epidemic to plague the highways. We encourage the FMCSA to focus its efforts in this area and discover you have a willing marketplace partner to support innovation in greater freight security. Brokers invest in staff who handle critical processes in the supply chain. Brokers invest in insurance coverages that potentially add a layer of assurance and security to both the shipper and carrier, depending on the circumstance. All of those costs, and others, remain unseen in the way the FMCSA approaches rate transparency. To force this disclosure requirement on brokers is unconscionable because it is not a fair representation of the value added to the supply chain brokers provide nor does truthfully represent the business costs that come with operating a business that provides these benefits to the market.

We strongly support the proposed rulemaking to enhance broker transparency. These updates are a much-needed step to level the playing field for small carriers and owner-operators who keep this industry moving. While this rule-making would be a step in the right direction, we urge the FMCSA to strengthen these proposals further to ensure fairness and compliance.

For years, carriers have faced difficulty getting access to transaction records as outlined in 49 CFR § 371.3. Brokers often circumvent these requirements via contractual language that forces carriers to waive their rights to access freight. These practices break trust, disproportionately harm small businesses, and lead to inefficiencies in the market.

Positive Changes in the Proposal
-Electronic Records: Mandating electronic record-keeping is a significant improvement. It ensures that records are readily available and eliminates the loophole where brokers limit access to physical records at their principal place of business.
-48-Hour Access Rule: Giving brokers a deadline to provide transaction records ensures carriers have timely access to critical information.
-Clear Record Contents: Updating the required contents of transaction records—including clear documentation of all charges, payments, and any claims—is essential for achieving transparency and accountability.  

What Still Needs Work
-Prohibit Waivers: The FMCSA should formally prohibit brokers from including provisions in contracts that require carriers to waive their rights to transaction records.  Without such a provision, brokers will continue to evade transparency requirements, rendering the proposed rules ineffective.
-Enforcement and Penalties: The FMCSA must outline specific penalties for brokers who fail to comply with transparency regulations.  Without meaningful consequences, compliance is unlikely to improve.
-Address Blacklisting: The FMCSA should investigate and address the practice of blacklisting carriers who request transparency. This retaliatory behavior discourages carriers from exercising their rights and undermines the intent of the regulations.
-Clarify Electronic Request Formats: The FMCSA should provide explicit guidance on acceptable formats for electronic record requests. Standardizing this process will ensure that carriers can access information without unnecessary delays or procedural hurdles.

Carriers Bear the Load
It is important to emphasize that carriers carry most of the risk and liability within the trucking industry. From significant investments in expensive assets such as trucks and trailers to the ongoing costs of hiring and retaining safe and reliable drivers, carriers shoulder the bulk of the regulatory, operational, and financial risks. Additionally, carriers bear the brunt of constantly increasing insurance costs, including the looming threat of "nuclear" verdicts, which can devastate businesses and create immense financial strain.

Carriers are the foundation upon which the entire freight industry rests. Without healthy carriers to haul freight, the rest of the industry—including brokers—simply cannot exist. Despite this, carriers often find themselves operating at unreasonably thin margins while brokers, the "middlemen," benefit disproportionately without sufficient oversight or accountability. Transparency is essential to ensure that carriers can operate at profitable margins and continue to provide the critical services that keep goods moving across the country.

Importance of Transparency
Greater transparency is not only a matter of fairness but also essential for a healthy and competitive freight market. When carriers have access to transaction records, they can:
-Ensure they are fairly compensated for their work.
-Identify and report illegal brokering practices.
-Make informed decisions about future business relationships.

Conversely, the lack of transparency allows bad actors to thrive, reduces market efficiency, and drives up costs for shippers and consumers alike.

Conclusion
The FMCSA’s proposed changes are a critical first step toward addressing the transparency issues that have plagued the trucking industry for far too long. However, these changes must be strengthened to close loopholes and ensure enforcement. Specifically, prohibiting contractual waivers and implementing stronger penalties for non-compliance are essential to achieving the intended outcomes of this rulemaking.

Carriers are the backbone of the freight industry - we urge the FMCSA to take decisive action to promote fairness, transparency, and accountability in broker-carrier relationships.

Thank you for your time and consideration.

Regardless of the ethics behind this proposed rule, which I believe are in the direct disinterest of free market business, this rule acts as an attempt to fix a problem which does not exist.

If a carrier doesn't like the rate offered, wait longer, negotiate higher, choose another shipment, or sit still. If another carrier takes that shipment before carrier A's pricing demand is met, then carrier B thrives. 

If a broker goes to a customer and bids too high, they don't get awarded the shipment. If they price below market rate, they lose money on the shipment.

Say we homogenize all margin for brokers. How do they get a carrier to take a same day shipment to an area that is undesirable? Cash is the only incentive. What incentive is there for a broker to then take a loss on this shipment? Now we have a stall in freight allocation. The Shipper cannot ship, the Carrier does not want to drive for that price, and the Broker does not want to book a Carrier for a loss. This isn't a once-in-year example, these types of shipments are booked every day, every hour. The carriers, the brokers, the shippers lose all ability to negotiate a better situation for themselves if you disclose broker margins.

All that withstanding, this proposed rule represents an overreach of FMCSA's regulatory authority. The agency's role is to oversee safety and compliance, not to dictate private business arrangements or interfere in lawful contracts.

The FMCSA attempt to require market participants to reveal private business information is a broad overreach and a misinterpretation of the breadth of FMCSA’s authority.

Some of the statutes that FMCSA cites as the basis for its authority likely do not authorize required disclosure to private third parties. And while there may be a broad grant of authority, the ICC and FMCSA have not regulated broker charges and have repeatedly recognized that brokers benefit both shippers and carriers. 

Additionally, Congress deregulated the trucking industry with an intent to rely on market forces, which makes it peculiar for FMCSA to now find it has authority to interfere with a broker’s commercial relations

The government does not usually require industry participants to share proprietary and 
trade secret information with commercial counter parties with which it will likely negotiate in the future. This lack of information about another party’s profit margin is not unique to brokerage, indeed, it is not unique to transportation. Just as a shipper has no idea what a carrier’s margin might be, a party buying a pair of shoes at Walmart has no idea how much it cost the manufacturer to make the shoe, or how much Walmart is marking up the shoe. 

There is no law that requires Walmart to disclose its mark-up between what it paid the 
manufacturer and what it is selling the shoe for. There is no reason to treat a broker 
di0erently. Nor is there any reason to treat a broker di0erently than a motor carrier that might contract with a shipper directly.

The rule, as proposed, will put brokers at risk of having to choose between regulatory 
compliance or breaching commercial contracts with their customers. Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly 
or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of 410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect trade secrets.DTSA defines “trade secrets” as “all forms and types of financial, business . . . information, 
including . . . compilations, . . . formulas, methods, techniques, processes . . . if – (A) the owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being generally know to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 18 U.S.C. § 1839(3). The type of information that the proposed rule seeks to be disclosed clearly fall within the definition of a “trade secret.

This is primarily because too many hands are in the pockets of the drivers, siphoning off their earnings.

Addressing these issues is essential to fostering a more equitable and enticing working environment within the trucking industry.

Docket ID: FMCSA-2023-0257-0001
Subject: Comment in Support of Proposed Broker Transparency Rule

To Whom It May Concern:
As an owner-operator in the trucking industry, I strongly support the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) proposed amendments to broker regulations aimed at enhancing transparency in freight transactions. Brokers play a critical role in connecting carriers like myself with shippers, but the lack of accessible and timely transaction records has created a significant imbalance in the industry. This proposed rule represents a vital step toward fostering fairness and accountability in brokered freight transactions.

I fully support all the provisions outlined in the proposed rule, particularly:
• Electronic Recordkeeping: Maintaining and providing electronic records will ensure carriers like me have a clear understanding of payment terms and charges.
• Timely Access to Records: Requiring brokers to provide records within 48 hours ensures that small businesses can quickly address discrepancies or make informed decisions.
• Prohibition of Contractual Waivers: Protecting carriers from being coerced into waiving their rights to transparency is essential to preserving fairness in the industry. Also, prohibiting carriers from being blacklisted.

Operating a trucking business is already extremely costly, especially in California. Monthly average expenses include:
• Truck note: $2,000
• Insurance: $1,200
• Fuel: $6,000
• Parking: $450
• Trailer: $800
• Compliance: $600
• Additional costs like rising parts and labor expenses.

Many owner-operators are one major truck repair away from financial ruin. This makes the excessive percentages brokers sometimes take—20%, 30%, or even 40% of the load rate—particularly damaging. I believe there should also be consideration of capping broker fees at a reasonable percentage, such as 10% of the load rate, to prevent excessive profit-taking at the expense of carriers.

As an owner-operator, I rely on clear and transparent dealings to sustain my livelihood. Ensuring brokers operate with accountability will not only improve trust but also create a more equitable working environment for small business owners like myself.

I encourage FMCSA to move forward with this rulemaking and consider additional enforcement measures to ensure compliance. These changes will strengthen the trucking industry by promoting fair and transparent practices. Thank you for addressing this critical issue and for providing owner-operators the opportunity to voice their perspectives.

Jamar Dotson
O/O & Fleet Owner
TJD Trucking

Brokers in the trucking industry should be transparent about how much they make for several important reasons:

Trust and Credibility: Transparency builds trust with carriers, shippers, and other stakeholders. If brokers are open about their earnings, it fosters a sense of integrity and confidence in their business practices. This trust can lead to long-term relationships, repeat business, and positive reputations within the industry.

Fair Pricing: Clear communication about broker commissions ensures that all parties are aware of the fair value of a load. It can help avoid disputes and misunderstandings regarding pricing. Carriers can better assess the profitability of a load when they know how much the broker is making, which ensures that they aren't being taken advantage of with inflated rates.

Accountability: When brokers disclose their margins, it holds them accountable for their business practices. It encourages them to provide value for their services rather than just focusing on maximizing profits. This can lead to more efficient and ethical business operations, benefiting everyone in the supply chain.

Competitive Market: Transparency helps create a more competitive market. If brokers disclose their profit margins, carriers and shippers can compare different brokers and decide who offers the best service or price for their needs. This can drive innovation, better service quality, and more reasonable rates across the industry.

Dear FMCSA Rulemaking Team,

I am writing to express my strong opposition to the proposed rulemaking titled “Transparency in Property Broker Transactions” (Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257-0001). This rule represents unnecessary government overreach and fails to address the real challenges facing the transportation industry today.

Key Concerns with the Proposed Rule:

1. Outdated and Misaligned Framework:

The expansion of C.F.R 371.3 in the proposed manner, a regulation from a time when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, fails to take account that the current marketplace operates fundamentally differently, with brokers facilitating two separate business transactions—one with the shipper and one with the carrier.
The regulation's original intent was to prevent freight rebating, a practice no longer relevant under modern industry practices.

2. Unnecessary and Burdensome Requirements:

The transportation marketplace is already highly transparent, with load boards and technology tools providing real-time pricing visibility.
Mandating electronic record submission through methods such as EDI or API integration would impose significant financial burdens, costing $2,500–$10,000 per integration—expenses likely to fall on motor carriers.

3. Lack of Evidence for Regulation:

During the COVID-19 pandemic, a period of heightened scrutiny, the FMCSA’s consumer complaint database recorded no complaints on this issue. This proposal addresses a non-existent problem.

4. Legal and Confidentiality Concerns:

The proposed rule risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, as it involves disclosing sensitive pricing and business information.
Recent legal precedent, including the Supreme Court’s decision to reverse the Chevron case, raises questions about the FMCSA’s authority to enforce such a rule.

5. Misplaced Priorities:

Rather than pursuing unnecessary expansions of outdated regulations, the FMCSA should focus on critical issues impacting our industry, such as improving highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic, which costs the supply chain over $1 billion annually.

Conclusion:

The FMCSA’s proposal is a solution in search of a problem. It imposes costly and unnecessary burdens on brokers and carriers while ignoring the true challenges facing the transportation industry. I urge the FMCSA to withdraw this proposed rule and redirect its efforts toward initiatives that enhance safety and combat fraud.

Thank you for considering my comments. I trust the FMCSA will prioritize policies that truly support the transportation industry's success and sustainability.

Sincerely,
Prinspal singh

The FMCSA transparency rulemaking is a crucial step toward ensuring fairness and sustainability in the trucking industry, especially for owner-operators and small carriers.

Many brokers currently refuse to work with new carriers, even when those carriers have been in business for over two or three years. This practice stifles growth for small businesses and creates unnecessary barriers for hardworking carriers trying to establish themselves. By enforcing transparency, the FMCSA can level the playing field and hold brokers accountable for promoting fairness within the industry.

While not all brokers operate unfairly, the reality is that carriers often end up doing 90% or more of the work. They put their health at risk, spend weeks or months away from their families, and drive under various weather conditions—all for rates that barely cover the cost of running their business. With fuel prices at unsustainable levels and insurance premiums increasing by 20-30% annually, small carriers are being pushed to the brink.

Mega carriers, who often have close relationships with brokers, dominate the market and actively work to suppress competition from small carriers and owner-operators. They receive the best rates and terms, creating an uneven playing field that undermines small businesses. It’s clear that many mega carriers would oppose this bill to maintain their advantage, further highlighting the need for these rules to protect smaller players in the industry.

Small carriers and owner-operators are the backbone of our transportation system, yet they are forced to accept unfavorable conditions due to a lack of transparency in broker operations. The proposed rules would provide much-needed accountability and help create a system where carriers can negotiate fairly and operate profitably.

I urge the FMCSA to finalize these transparency rules to protect small carriers and owner-operators, ensuring a fair and equitable future for the trucking industry.

FMCSA’s Overreach: A Threat to Brokers’ Vital Role in the Supply Chain

On November 20, 2024, the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) published a notice of proposed rulemaking (NPRM) titled “Transparency in Property Broker Transactions” in response to petitions. The Agency claims this proposed rule is necessary to “address the lack of access to information among shippers and motor carriers that can impact fairness and efficiency of the transportation system” and to “correct imbalances in negotiating power.” However, the FMCSA’s latest proposal threatens to undermine the essential role brokers play in the supply chain. By imposing unnecessary and burdensome regulations, the FMCSA risks destabilizing the delicate balance of the freight industry, ultimately harming brokers and the broader logistics network. The NPRM includes several key provisions:

Requiring brokers to maintain electronic records to ostensibly improve access for carriers and shippers.
Eliminating the distinction between brokerage and non-brokerage services, mandating records for each shipment to include all charges, payments, and related claims.
Reframing brokers’ duty to provide records as a regulatory obligation rather than a transactional right of request.
Mandating brokers provide all requested records within 48 hours of delivery.
According to BlueGrace Logistics’ Chief Capacity Officer Mark Ford, this proposal is an attack on brokers. The FMCSA’s claim that the proposed rule is necessary to address the lack of access to information among shippers and motor carriers is flawed. Shippers and carriers already have access to market rates through intermediaries such as DAT, Freightwaves, Truckstop, and various other rate aggregators.

Additionally, the assertion that the rule will correct imbalances in negotiating power fails to account for the fact that shippers and carriers inherently possess a higher level of negotiating power since they own the assets involved in the transaction—freight and trucks—and can choose their partners freely. There are no contractual obligations for a carrier to accept a load from a broker, and shippers are only contractually bound to tender a load to a broker if they have specifically signed a "contractually" bearing agreement to do so, (ex. outsource) which is rare. The aforementioned agreements can be structured as open book, and often are. Yearly bids have no real "contractual" penalties in regard to volumes and commitments, and are primarily designed to serve as a guideline of expectations. Shippers, carriers and brokers often deviate from agreed-upon volumes without significant penalties, as the market dynamics of the trucking industry make guaranteeing exact shipment levels and capacity difficult.

Furthermore, just as shippers keep their purchasing costs and proprietary advantages confidential, brokers should not be obligated to reveal their customer costs to carriers or vice versa. Brokers often use proprietary algorithms to determine rates tailored to their business, which may not align with a carrier’s or shipper’s rates. Additionally, the rates offered to shippers and the potential profit from individual shipments do not account for market fluctuations. Lane costs vary, and rates typically incorporate periods of varying profitability. The margin covers various operational expenses including systems, integrations, transaction processing, monetary float, overhead, training.... Some modes and lanes are also priced to ensure the overall profitability of an account. Many asset carriers also operate brokerages, and revealing broker rates to a carrier that also has a brokerage arm could give that carrier an unfair advantage over a strictly non-asset broker. It is evident that the FMCSA is pursuing an alternative agenda under the guise of “transparency,” and requiring brokers to disclose their rates poses significant business risks.

The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) broker transparency issue poses significant challenges to the transportation industry. Unlike any other private business sector, this regulation would compel brokers to disclose their profit margins and operational costs, a requirement unheard of in competitive markets. Forcing such transparency undermines the principle of free-market dynamics, where shippers, brokers, and carriers willingly negotiate and enter agreements without coercion, based on mutual consent to the terms.

Moreover, the proposed regulation offers no tangible benefits to the transportation ecosystem. It risks creating unnecessary administrative burdens, fostering mistrust among stakeholders, and potentially violating the confidentiality agreements outlined in contracts between shippers, brokers, and carriers. Introducing this regulation not only disrupts the balance of private enterprise but also sets a concerning precedent that erodes the fundamental rights of businesses to operate freely and privately. This regulation is both unnecessary and potentially harmful to an industry that thrives on trust and efficient negotiation.

Subject: Protecting the Trucking Industry Through the Enforcement of 371.3

To whom it may concern,

I am writing to express my concerns regarding the current state of compliance with 371.3 and its impact on the trucking industry. To make my points clear and concise, I have organized them below:

The Distinction Between the Trucking and Brokerage Industries
The trucking industry is not synonymous with the brokerage industry. In 1980, it was widely understood that the trucking industry required protections to prevent its collapse, as such a collapse would have devastating effects on the U.S. economy. These protections were never intended to benefit brokers but rather to ensure the sustainability of motor carriers.

The Original Intent of 371.3
Section 371.3 was carefully crafted to safeguard the trucking industry and, specifically, motor carriers. The intent was clear: to create a framework that would prevent brokers from engaging in practices that could harm carriers.

Chronic Non-Compliance by Brokers
Brokers have historically failed to comply with the requirements of 371.3. Their complaints about the potential outcomes of compliance are therefore misplaced. It is noteworthy that these complaints are not based on actual hardships but on perceived ones.

Reinforcing the Intent of 371.3
The original intent of 371.3 was to protect motor carriers. However, due to the waivers that brokers routinely compel carriers to sign, these protections have never been fully realized. Rewarding brokers in their moment of defiance, by allowing continued waivers, would be both unfair and counterproductive to the survival of motor carriers.

Proposal: Make 371.3 Non-Waivable
To restore the intent and effectiveness of 371.3, I propose that this provision be made unwaivable. This would ensure that carriers receive the protections originally envisioned, without being undermined by contractual waivers.

Additional Comment
I can provide numerous examples of brokers attempting to exploit carriers by withholding payments or making false claims, such as alleging rate cuts or fabricated expenses incurred as a result of the carrier’s actions. These actions are precisely what 371.3 was designed to prevent. However, these protections are rendered meaningless when the provision is waivable.

This is one of many instances where brokers seek to sidestep their legal responsibilities, often at the expense of the motor carriers that these laws were designed to protect. I urge you to consider the detrimental effects of the forced waivability of 371.3 on the trucking industry and make this provision unwaivable. The trucking industry is already under significant financial strain, and this step would provide critical relief and support.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,
Leander Richmond

To Whom It May Concern,

I am writing to express my strong support for transparency rules requiring brokers to share transaction records with all parties to a transaction, including shippers, upon request. Transparency rules that allow shippers to access transaction records are a necessary step toward ensuring fairness and accountability in this industry. These measures will not only empower shippers to make informed choices but also help ensure that carriers are fairly compensated for their critical work.

Dear Members of the FMCSA,

I am writing to express my opposition to the proposed broker transparency rule (“Transparency in Property Broker Transactions” Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257-0001). The proposed rule changes will actually hurt the people and businesses it intends to help.

The FMCSA states it is proposing these amendments to its property broker rules “in response to petitions… from OOIDA and the SBTC”. This indicates the intent of the proposed change is to benefit owner operators and smaller motor carriers. The intention here is great. I think everyone would prefer to see more smaller businesses thrive in the transportation industry, and in all industries for that matter. The problem is the effect of the proposed changes will likely be the opposite of the intention. Most brokers rely on owner operators and smaller carriers to move their brokered freight. That is why the petitions that brought about these proposed changes were organized by OOIDA and the SBTA and not by the American Trucking Association (ATA) or the Truckload Carriers Association (TCA). The ATA and the TCA members are all medium and large sized carriers who are not very concerned with how brokers operate because they have direct relationships with their shippers and don’t haul much freight for brokers. Therefore, the more freight shippers give to brokers, the more freight there will be available for owner operators and smaller carriers. 

Shippers want the rates they pay to remain confidential to retain some negotiating power. When shippers negotiate rates with their transportation providers, the shippers expect their transportation providers to keep the agreed upon rates confidential, regardless of whether their transportation partner is a carrier or a broker. Shippers want a limited number of people to know what rates they pay. In fact, many shippers contractually require brokers to keep their negotiated rates confidential. The proposed rule would “Require that the records contain, for each shipment in the transaction, all charges and payments connected to the shipment, including a description, amount, and date…” “This amendment would ensure the parties have full visibility into the payments, fees, and charges associated with the transaction…” If the proposed rule goes into effect, shippers will pull their freight away from brokers because the shippers will want their rates to remain confidential and want to avoid the potential for their rates to be shared with dozens of additional carriers and thus dilute the shippers negotiating power. While some people might not like the fact that shippers want their rates to remain confidential, this proposed rule would not change the shippers’ ability to keep their rates confidential, it would only impact the brokers ability to keep the rates confidential. As the shippers direct more freight towards medium and large carriers and away from brokers, owner operators and small carriers will be hurt as most of them rely heavily on brokers to find their freight. 

While the intention of this proposed rule, to help owner operators and small carriers, is a good one, the proposed rule would in fact have the opposite effect. Let’s support owner operators and small carriers by rejecting the proposed rule changes. Instead, let’s focus on more pressing problems in the industry like freight fraud/theft.

Thank you

While it might sound extreme, a lack of transparency in the trucking industry could contribute to a cascade of events that could severely disrupt supply chains, cripple economies, and ultimately lead to societal breakdown. Here's how this "doomsday" scenario could unfold:

Erosion of Trust and Collaboration: If brokers and carriers continue to operate in the shadows, hiding information and engaging in unfair practices, trust will erode. This will lead to reluctance to collaborate, fragmented networks, and inefficient operations.

Rising Costs and Decreased Efficiency: Hidden fees, unpredictable pricing, and inefficient routing due to a lack of transparency will drive up transportation costs. This will impact the price of goods, making essential commodities less affordable and straining consumers' budgets.

Driver Shortages and Unreliable Service: Exploited and unfairly compensated drivers will leave the industry, exacerbating the existing driver shortage. This will lead to delays, missed deliveries, and unreliable service, further disrupting supply chains.

Breakdown of Supply Chains: As transportation costs soar and service becomes unreliable, businesses will struggle to get their products to market. This will lead to shortages of essential goods, including food, medicine, and fuel, causing panic and social unrest.

Economic Collapse: The breakdown of supply chains will cripple businesses, leading to mass layoffs and economic recession. Governments will struggle to respond effectively, further eroding public trust and potentially leading to civil unrest.

Societal Breakdown: As essential goods become scarce and economic hardship spreads, social order may begin to break down. Competition for resources could lead to conflict and violence, potentially resulting in a collapse of societal structures.

Environmental Impact: Inefficient routing and increased fuel consumption due to a lack of transparency will exacerbate environmental problems. This will contribute to climate change and further destabilize the planet, making recovery even more difficult.

While this scenario may seem far-fetched, it highlights the interconnectedness of the trucking industry with the overall health of society and the economy. By prioritizing transparency and fair practices, the industry can contribute to a more stable, efficient, and sustainable future. Ignoring these issues, however, could have dire consequences, potentially leading to a chain of events that could threaten our way of life.

Transparency in the trucking industry is crucial for ensuring fair practices, efficient operations, and building trust among stakeholders. Let's examine why it's so important and explore some worst-case scenarios that can arise from a lack of transparency:   

Why Transparency Matters in Trucking:

Fair Compensation for Drivers: Truckers deserve to know how their rates are calculated and where the money goes. Transparent brokers clearly outline all fees, deductions, and payments, ensuring drivers are paid fairly for their work.   
Preventing Exploitation: Hidden fees, unexpected charges, and "double-brokering" (brokers re-brokering loads without carrier knowledge) can exploit drivers, leading to lower earnings and financial instability. Transparency helps prevent these unethical practices.
Building Trust and Long-Term Relationships: Shippers, brokers, and carriers need to trust each other. Transparency fosters this trust, leading to stronger, more reliable partnerships.   
Efficient Operations: Clear communication and readily available information streamline operations. Knowing exactly where a shipment is, its status, and associated costs improves efficiency for everyone involved.
Safety and Accountability: Transparency in maintenance records, driver qualifications, and hours of service logs is vital for ensuring safety on the roads. It also helps hold parties accountable in case of accidents or incidents.
Worst-Case Scenarios Due to Lack of Transparency:

Driver Exploitation and Financial Ruin: Imagine a driver completes a long haul only to find out the broker took a massive cut or added hidden fees, leaving the driver with barely enough to cover fuel costs. This can lead to financial hardship, debt, and even force drivers out of business.
Safety Compromises: If brokers hide information about a shipment's contents or a truck's maintenance history, it could lead to dangerous situations on the road, potentially causing accidents and endangering lives.
Increased Freight Costs: Lack of transparency can create inefficiencies and distrust, leading to higher prices for shippers and ultimately consumers.
Legal Disputes and Damaged Reputations: Disputes over payments, contracts, and responsibilities can arise when information is hidden or unclear. This can lead to costly legal battles and damage the reputations of all parties involved.   
Loss of Trust and Industry Instability: A lack of transparency erodes trust throughout the trucking industry. This can lead to an unstable market with unpredictable pricing, unreliable service, and reluctance to collaborate, ultimately harming the entire supply chain.
Transparency is not just a nice-to-have in the trucking industry; it's a necessity. By promoting open communication, clear documentation, and accessible information, the industry can ensure fair practices, improve efficiency, and build a more sustainable and trustworthy future for everyone.

Here's how a lack of transparency in the US trucking industry could contribute to a collapse of the US economy, with a focus on the unique vulnerabilities of the American system:

1. Fueling Inflation and Consumer Panic:

Hidden Costs: If brokers obscure fees and manipulate pricing, the true cost of transportation becomes hidden. This leads to unpredictable and inflated prices for goods, impacting everything from groceries to electronics.
Hoarding and Panic Buying: As prices surge and consumers fear shortages, panic buying and hoarding could ensue. This disrupts supply chains further, leading to empty shelves and exacerbating the problem.
Breaking Point: The US economy relies heavily on consumer spending. If consumers lose confidence in the affordability and availability of essential goods, spending could plummet, triggering a recession.   
2. Crippling Critical Industries:

Just-in-Time Manufacturing: Many US manufacturers rely on "just-in-time" delivery of parts and materials. Unreliable trucking due to lack of transparency disrupts this delicate balance, halting production lines and causing massive losses.
Agriculture and Food Supply: American agriculture depends on efficient transportation to move produce from farms to consumers. Delays and disruptions could lead to spoilage, shortages, and price spikes for essential food items.   
Energy Sector: The US energy sector relies heavily on trucking for transporting fuel and equipment. Any disruption in this flow could lead to energy shortages, impacting businesses, transportation, and even basic utilities like electricity.
3. Exacerbating Existing Economic Issues:

Labor Shortages: The US already faces labor shortages in many sectors. Lack of transparency in trucking worsens this by discouraging new drivers and pushing existing ones out, further straining the workforce and hindering economic growth.   
Supply Chain Bottlenecks: The US supply chain is already stretched thin. Opaque practices in trucking create further bottlenecks and inefficiencies, slowing down the movement of goods and hindering economic recovery.
National Debt: The US has a significant national debt. Economic disruption caused by trucking industry issues could further strain government finances, potentially leading to higher taxes, cuts in public services, and economic instability.   
4. Undermining Small Businesses and Local Economies:

Small Businesses Reliance: Small businesses are particularly vulnerable to transportation disruptions and price fluctuations. They often lack the resources to absorb these shocks, potentially leading to closures and job losses.   
Rural Communities: Many rural communities depend on trucking for their economic livelihood. Disruptions in trucking services can isolate these areas, hindering access to goods, services, and healthcare.
By acknowledging the potential for economic collapse and addressing the issues of transparency and fairness within the US trucking industry, stakeholders can contribute to a more stable and resilient economy. Ignoring these issues, however, could have far-reaching consequences that impact every aspect of American life.

I strongly oppose the proposed FMCSA rule requiring freight brokers to disclose customer rates to motor carriers. I understand why the OOIDA's would advocate for this rule to benefit their constituents, but it comes with the cost of freight brokers disclosing trade information that enables them to be undercut and severely harmed. I believe that this rule has significant negative ramifications for freight brokers and the freight market as a whole.
Freight brokers play a critical role in the transportation industry by connecting shippers and motor carriers, negotiating competitive rates, and providing value-added services risk management and logistics coordination. Requiring disclosure of customer rates undermines this role in several ways:
1. Un-American and Unprecedented in Business: This rule represents a gross overreach by the government into private business practices. What other B2B industry mandates that one party disclose their margins to their vendors? Of course, vendors in any industry would love to know their buyers’ margins, it would give them tremendous leverage, but that’s not how America works. Mandating such disclosures is contrary to the principles of free enterprise that underpin our economy. What industry wouldn't be destroyed or severely damaged by government intervention requiring them to disclose margins? This proposal sets a dangerous precedent that undermines the foundation of competitive markets. Is the next step to have shippers disclose the profit margins on the goods that they ship, so that carriers can upcharge accordingly - that would be equally ridiculous.
2. Erosion of Competitive Market Dynamics: Freight brokers operate in a highly competitive market where negotiated rates reflect the service, expertise, and value provided. Disclosing customer rates eliminates brokers' ability to negotiate effectively, forcing rate conformity and stifling competition.
3. Harm to Shippers and Brokers: Confidentiality in pricing allows brokers to manage diverse customer needs while delivering value to motor carriers. Mandating disclosure jeopardizes the confidentiality of broker-shipper agreements, potentially driving shippers away from brokers altogether or toward private arrangements, which reduces opportunities for both brokers and carriers.
4. Unfair Competitive Advantage for Carrier-Brokerages: Many motor carriers also operate brokerage businesses. These hybrid businesses stand to exploit the proposed rule to gain competitive intelligence about customer rates, using this information to undercut independent brokers or directly solicit business from shippers. This creates an uneven playing field and undermines the trust and collaboration that are essential for a functioning freight market.
5. Administrative Burden: This rule adds unnecessary administrative complexity, increasing costs and inefficiencies for brokers. Such costs will ultimately ripple through the supply chain, burdening consumers.
Instead of pursuing a policy that damages freight broker operations, the FMCSA should focus on addressing the rampant fraud plaguing the transportation industry. Fraudulent activities, such as double-brokering schemes and identity theft, cost brokers, carriers, and shippers millions of dollars annually, eroding trust and stability in the marketplace. These fraudulent practices hurt everyone—brokers, motor carriers, and consumers alike—and pose a far greater threat to the transportation industry than what amounts to a non-issue and is really just a large powerful group pushing for mandatory disclosure of trade secrets by a smaller group under the guise of “transparency”.
By prioritizing measures to combat fraud and strengthen enforcement, the FMCSA could deliver meaningful protections and savings to all industry stakeholders without undermining the critical role of freight brokers. 
I urge the FMCSA to reconsider this proposal and focus on addressing fraud and systemic issues that truly harm the integrity of the freight transportation industry, rather than imposing a rule that is un-American, unfair, and detrimental to the competitive principles that drive our economy.

Privacy in contracting
The government does not usually require industry participants to share proprietary and 
trade secret information with commercial counter parties with which it will likely negotiate 
in the future.
This lack of information about another party’s profit margin is not unique to brokerage, 
indeed, it is not unique to transportation. Just as a shipper has no idea what a carrier’s 
margin might be, a party buying a pair of shoes at Walmart has no idea how much it cost 
the manufacturer to make the shoe, or how much Walmart is marking up the shoe. There is 
no law that requires Walmart to disclose its mark-up between what it paid the 
manufacturer and what it is selling the shoe for. There is no reason to treat a broker 
di0erently. Nor is there any reason to treat a broker di0erently than a motor carrier that 
might contract with a shipper directly.
The rule, as proposed, will put brokers at risk of having to choose between regulatory 
compliance or breaching commercial contracts with their customers.
Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent 
expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law 
counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly 
or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of 
410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect 
trade secrets.
DTSA defines “trade secrets” as “all forms and types of financial, business . . . information, 
including . . . compilations, . . . formulas, methods, techniques, processes . . . if – (A) the 
owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the 
information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being 
generally know to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another 
person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 18 
U.S.C. § 1839(3). The type of information that the proposed rule seeks to be disclosed 
clearly fall within the definition of a “trade secret

As an experienced logistics professional and certified transportation broker, it is my experience that an individual employed to negotiate freight contracts on behalf of a typical motor carrier is not equipped to - or elects not to - be able to properly assess their own costs associated with the performance of a service, and thus are generally not willing or able to negotiate a contracted rate that would align with healthy financial performance of their company. The result of this is that the large number of struggling owner-operators and small motor carriers do not responsibly manage their own finances and pricing strategies, resulting in these carriers struggling or exiting the market. A pertinent example would be the up-surge in market rates for transportation coinciding with the COVID-19 pandemic. Rates skyrocketed at that time, resulting in unprecedented revenues for these motor carriers. Many took the opportunity to hire more drivers, and finance or lease more equipment. When the market took a downturn, many of the motor carriers engaged in these irresponsible acquisitions exited the market. If my assessment of these motor carriers were incorrect, and instead a typical motor carrier does in fact responsibly manage their business, we would not have seen such a mass exodus of motor carriers following the pandemic. These irresponsibly-managed motor carriers are the foundation of the proposed rule change, and are supported by influential organizations and larger, more successful motor carriers who wish to leverage this movement as a shortcut to increase the profitability of their businesses without having to assess their own practices and business models.

The proposed rule changes circumvent these concerns by empowering the motor carrier to scrutinize transportation brokers' data, such that they are able to negotiate more favorable contracts than they otherwise would have been able to. In effect, the proposed rule changes would result in brokers essentially subsidizing poor-performing motor carriers, and decimating brokerage margins in the process.

In very few other industries is this level of transparency required. Brokers perform an important service for manufacturers and other shippers by helping them assess market conditions, source quality transportation suppliers, and control costs. This rule change would undermine the above brokerage services, would un-American, anti-market, and therefore I am opposed.

Expansion of 371.3 is based on an outdated, burdensome and government overreach.
Formalized in 1980 when brokers were commissioned sales agents of motor carriers, not how the market currently operates.
The intent was to have financial transparency between all parties to a brokered load to ensure the illegal practice of freight rebating was not occurring, where a broker and shipper were common owners.
The current marketplace involves two separate business transactions between the shipper and broker and the broker and carrier.
The Interstate Commerce Commission (ICC) made it clear in its intent of this regulation, “Our goal in regulating transactions between brokers, carriers, and shippers is to remove all unnecessary restrictions which might impede the free operation of the marketplace."
During the COVID-19 pandemic, when this issue was at its height, there were no complaints in the consumer complaint database. 
This is a solution in search of a problem. 
The transportation marketplace is already very transparent with loadboards and other technology pricing tools. 
The only safe way to achieve electronic submission safely would be through an EDI or API integration, at the cost of roughly $2,500 - $10,000 per integration. Motor carriers would bear the cost of this as well. 
Possible violations of the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 which includes pricing and business information. 
FMCSA should focus on highway safety and addressing the freight fraud pandemic that is costing the supply-chain over $1 billion annually. 
The FMCSA already has a prohibition of coercion rule in place to protect motor carriers.
The U.S. Supreme Court overruled the Chevron deference decision, which brings in legal concerns of this rulemaking. Please spend your time and our money working on issues that affect both brokers and carriers such as an updated safer system and combatting fraud in the industry.
