We have been involved in the freight brokerage industry for over thirty years. We appreciate the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration's (FMCSA) dedication to enhancing regulatory frameworks, but there are significant concerns regarding the proposed regulation FMCSA-2023-0257. 

Impact on Operational Efficiency and Cost: 

The proposed regulation introduces requirements that would substantially increase the administrative burden on brokers. The additional documentation, reporting mandates, and compliance checks would necessitate substantial resource allocation, potentially diverting focus from core operational tasks. This increase in administrative overhead would not only drive-up operational costs but could also affect the overall efficiency of the supply chain. 

Economic Burden on Small Businesses: 

Many brokerage firms, especially small and mid-sized businesses, operate on thin margins. The proposed regulation would impose financial strains that could be unsustainable for mid-sized and smaller businesses. This could lead to reduced competition in the market, disadvantaging shippers and carriers who benefit from the diverse service options currently available. 

Unintended Consequences on Market Dynamics: 

The regulation could inadvertently lead to market consolidation, with larger brokers more capable of absorbing the increased costs and regulatory burdens. This consolidation could reduce competition and limit choices for carriers and customers alike. A less competitive market is likely to lead to higher prices and reduced service quality across the board. 

Legal and Confidentiality Concerns: 

The proposed rule risks violating the Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016, as it involves disclosing sensitive pricing and business information. Simply enacting a rule where Brokers are required to disclose all parts of the transaction would put most brokers in breach of contract for customers who have confidentiality requirements in place. These provisions safeguard not only their sensitive contacts, pricing models, product information, distribution network, and cargo details but also the processes and implementation strategies they use on a daily basis. This could lead to an innumerable number of legal actions stemming from businesses who cannot have their confidential information disclosed. 

Recommendation for Alternative Approaches: 

Instead of enacting FMCSA-2023-0257 as proposed, we recommend a stronger approach to Fighting Freight Fraud. Simply put, this is financially impacting both brokers and carriers. The amount of stolen shipments, hacked/spoofed identities, scammers, double brokers and other nefarious actors has cost this industry $1,000,000,000.00 annually and no signs its letting up. We are looking to the FMCSA to work with both carriers, shippers and brokers, to eliminate this for the last time.

By mandating the disclosure of confidential business information, this rule would force

financial risks for all parties involved.

I strongly support increased transparency in property broker transactions as a necessary step towards fairness and accountability in the trucking industry. For too long, motor carriers and owner-operators have operated with limited visibility into brokered transactions, often resulting in disputes over payments and a lack of trust in the system.

 By requiring brokers to disclose transaction records promptly and in an accessible format, FMCSA will create a more level playing field, ensuring that carriers receive fair compensation and can make informed business decisions. Transparency will deter unethical practices, improve industry stability, and promote fair competition.

 I urge FMCSA to adopt strong enforcement measures to ensure compliance with these transparency rules. Without meaningful penalties for non-compliance, brokers may continue opaque practices that disadvantage carriers.

 Thank you for considering this much-needed reform.

 Sincerely,

I am a carrier with 3 trucks. Just a small mom/pop owned business. I have been in business for myself since 2018. I have seen freight rates up very high where a owner like me could be profitable. It was like that for the first four years of my business. During this time we were making money and the brokers were making money. Both sides were happy. Fast forward to 2022. The rates these brokers started handing out was ridiculously low in mid 2022. At this time I was pulling a dry van. These brokers rates got so bad and low I couldn't turn enough profit to cover expenses on the equipment, much less pay a driver a decent wage for his labor. I struggled for the rest of 22 and up til the begging of the 3rd quarter of 2023. In Jul of 23 I had to park the dry van trailer and bought an open deck trailer, because at the time the rates from the brokers were a lot better than the dry van freight was. For 3 months we were once again profitable with this open deck trailer running brokered freight. At the end of October these brokers started dropping the rates on open deck. I feel like the reason for this is more people got away from pulling dry van and reefers and done like I did and went out and bought a open deck trailer. I think the brokers have more open deck trailers in the market to choose from now. This being said I feel like they are driving the rates down due to this. They are getting a premium price to haul the freight, but are offering carriers like me low, low rates. While these brokers now in the marketplace are having record profits off of the back of the American trucker. They need to be transparent with all parties involved in the transaction! They are keeping the money the carriers need to operate and stay in business. The law is there for transparency, but they make us carriers wave our rights to it in every contract that is issued for us to sign. You guys need to make them stop that, make them be transparent on every transaction! Make it a law that they have to issue to both the shipper and carrier a bill of the transaction showing what they got paid to haul the load from the shipper, what they paid the carrier and what they took. I assure yall that we are going to start seeing them making up to 40-50% off the load. This is unacceptable! 10-15% is the most the aught to be making. Help with this so they can be regulated. The government regulates brokers that sell real estate, why can't freight brokers that sell freight be regulated? If something isn't done with them small carriers like me are going to have to close due to their practices.

To whom it may concern,

The proposed changes to 371.3 are inadequate and modernize the existing regulation to perform the same function. Carriers are asking for regulatory assistance to stop the abuses administered by bad actors in brokerage firms. The FMCSA claims that it lacks the authority to prevent contract waivers required by brokers. The Transportation Intermediaries Association provides model contracts encouraging every member to require contract waivers to broker transparency. This is a form of collusion among brokers to use contract waivers to broker transparency, a form of a non-compete clause in their contracts. Brokers intend to hide contact information and the true value of services provided. This disadvantages carriers in price negotiations and creates a deleterious effect on safety when carriers are ill-informed by unscrupulous brokers. The Federal Trade Commission has made similar regulations based on similar legislation provided to the FMCSA in 49 USC 13101 - Transportation Policy. Furthermore, carriers have complained that work is withheld from them when they don't sign contract waivers and they are blackballed when they exercise their right to transparency. This too is covered in 49 USC 13101 - Transportation Policy. Safety is at stake when carriers are coerced into poor business practices and the FMCSA refuses to act.

https://www.ftc.gov/system/files/ftc_gov/pdf/noncompete-rule.pdf

*I strongly support increased transparency in property broker transactions as a necessary step toward fairness and accountability in the trucking industry. For too long, motor carriers and owner-operators have operated with limited visibility into brokered transactions, often resulting in disputes over payments and a lack of trust in the system.*

*By requiring brokers to disclose transaction records promptly and in an accessible format, FMCSA will create a more level playing field, ensuring that carriers receive fair compensation and can make informed business decisions. Transparency will deter unethical practices, improve industry stability, and promote fair competition.*

*I urge FMCSA to adopt strong enforcement measures to ensure compliance with these transparency rules. Without meaningful penalties for non-compliance, brokers may continue opaque practices that disadvantage carriers.*

*Thank you for considering this much-needed reform.*

*Sincerely

I am strongly opposed to this Rulemaking. It's anticompetitive and pierces the right of private contracting in the trucking industry. As an industry participant for over thirty years, this theory transparency is an ancient leftover from times in brokerage where the broker's customers would be invoiced directly by the motor carriers, for whom the broker was acting essentially as a non-exclusive commissioned sales agent. Brokers represented multiple motor carriers as sales agents, bringing those motor carriers business, and then would collect a commission from the motor carrier after the motor carrier invoiced the manufacturing customer and collected from them. The broker needed the transparency in those early days to ensure the carrier was not dealing around the broker with the customer, and not paying the broker its due commission. That practice ended beginning in the late 1970s, as brokerage matured as an industry under a bipartisan wave of deregulation in trucking that began under President Carter's administration. At that point, brokers began contracting directly with manufacturing customers, and contracting directly and separately with the motor carriers that the broker might hire to haul its customers freight. In short, OOIDA wants to use this archaic transparency regime to pierce through private contracting in the marketplace, which is anticompetitive. In closing, this legislation is not only unnecessary, it's a huge overstep that interferes with open and fair market competition and the rights of independent business entities' contract privacy.

I am writing in opposition to the broadening of the rules for “rate transparency.” These rules should be abolished and were written and developed in a vastly different economy and market system.

This does not, to my knowledge, exist today. Typically you will have dispatch services that get paid by the carrier or brokers who get paid by the shipper.

2) Price information is readily available today for lanes and markets to know what should and is being paid in the marketplace, something that did not exist when the rules were enacted. Services such as Transcore DAT and Truckstop provide rate information for the lanes that the carrier is running. This provides sufficient information for efficient markets. How does knowing what the broker is being paid increase efficiency? It only provides an opportunity for the carrier to try to solicit the shipper directly knowing exactly what the broker is charging. You might say that this is prevented by contracts. Yes, it is, but enforcement of this in reality is normally cost prohibitive. We have been in business for 25 years and never prosecuted a case of back solicitation. We rather opt to flag and ban that carrier and not work with shippers who back solicit carriers. Increasing the visibility, will only increase the back-solicitations by carriers and undermine the structure of the trucking market.

Sincerely,

Robert Hooper Jr.
CEO Atlantic Logistics LLC
Ph.D. Economics from Indiana University

It seems there is a fundamental misunderstanding among some carriers regarding the role of freight brokers. The freight market operates on the basic principles of supply and demand. When market conditions favor carriers—i.e., when there are more loads than trucks—rates tend to rise, and carriers benefit. Conversely, when the market shifts and there are more trucks than loads, rates typically decrease.

Broker customers are well aware of these market fluctuations and often adjust their pricing accordingly when they anticipate such changes. The idea that brokerage customers are unaware of these dynamics and are consistently overpaying while brokers simultaneously suppress rates is unrealistic. In fact, market shifts that favor carriers typically benefit the brokers they work with as well. In the short term, brokers may not raise rates immediately in order to test whether a market shift is sustainable, but this is a natural part of the market cycle.

Moreover, the assertion that brokers consistently retain 40% margins on loads overlooks the fact that brokers often handle freight at break-even or even a loss to build volume—a strategy that only brokers and large carriers can afford to sustain.

The proposal to increase rate transparency with the expectation that it will drive up rates may, in fact, have the opposite effect. Many of the carriers supporting this rule discount or ignore available data that contradicts their position. Brokers have never, and will never, force carriers to accept a rate they are unwilling to agree to. This is a free market, and it is essential that we return to that principle.

Carriers run their businesses, and brokers run theirs. Any attempt to intervene beyond this framework would constitute an overreach of government authority.

I strongly support increased transparency in property broker transactions as a necessary step toward fairness and accountability in the trucking industry. For too long, motor carriers and owner-operators have operated with limited visibility into brokered transactions, often resulting in disputes over payments and a lack of trust in the system.

By requiring brokers to disclose transaction records promptly and in an accessible format, FMCSA will create a more level playing field, ensuring that carriers receive fair compensation and can make informed business decisions. Transparency will deter unethical practices, improve industry stability, and promote fair competition.

I urge FMCSA to adopt strong enforcement measures to ensure compliance with these transparency rules. Without meaningful penalties for non-compliance, brokers may continue opaque practices that disadvantage carriers.

Thank you for considering this much-needed reform.

Sincerely,
Parmvir Singh

I strongly oppose the proposed Freight Broker Transparency rulemaking due to its potential to cause significant and unwarranted damage to the freight brokerage industry. The rule, while perhaps well-intentioned, fails to account for the market's complexity and competitiveness, and its broad application could create unnecessary burdens on freight brokers and on the public. Freight brokers are critical to the efficient movement of goods across the country in the most challenging business environments - no singular trucking company or owner-operator has the reach and flexibility that major US corporations require to remain competitive. 

Freight brokers play a critical role in ensuring that goods move efficiently and cost-effectively across the supply chain. The proposed regulations would impose significant administrative and operational costs on brokers, without delivering clear or measurable benefits. These costs could be particularly challenging for smaller businesses, potentially forcing them out of the market and reducing competition.

Additionally, the rule will lead to unintended consequences such as reduced flexibility and efficiency in negotiations and movement of freight, negatively impacting both brokers and all manufacturing across the industry. This regulation appears to be a one-size-fits-all approach that fails to recognize the diverse nature of freight transactions and the existing industry standards that already promote fair practices. Instead, FMCSA should be focusing efforts on removing fraud from the industry which costs carrier, brokers, and shippers millions of dollars. 

Instead of creating new burdensome regulations, I urge the agency to work with industry stakeholders to develop solutions that ensure fairness without stifling the efficiency, competition, and flexibility that have made the freight brokerage business successful

Subject: Comment on proposed Broker Transparency Regulations to amend 49 CFR § 371.3

To Whom It May Concern,

I am writing to express my concerns regarding the proposed broker transparency regulations related to 49 CFR § 371.3. While I recognize the importance of fair and transparent operations within the freight brokerage industry, I believe that the proposed regulations impose unnecessary burdens on brokers and could have unintended negative consequences for the transportation sector as a whole. The proposed rules are yet a further distraction from the FMCSA’s core mandate related to safety. The national out of service (OOS) rate for commercial vehicles exceeds 21% all while operating a commercial vehicle remains one of the top ten most dangerous jobs in the country. 

1. Confidentiality Concerns. The proposed regulations would require brokers to provide detailed transactional information to carriers, which raises serious concerns regarding the confidentiality of sensitive business data. Freight brokers play a crucial role in negotiating fair market rates and managing logistics complexities. Releasing proprietary pricing information could undermine their ability to remain competitive, ultimately leading to market distortions and a potential reduction in the number of available brokers.

2. Administrative Burden and Compliance Costs. The new transparency requirements would impose significant administrative burdens on brokers, particularly small and mid-sized enterprises. The need to maintain and disclose transaction records in a specified format could lead to increased operational costs, diverting resources away from core business functions. This increased financial strain could force many small brokers out of the market, reducing competition and potentially driving up costs for carriers and shippers.

3. Impact on Market Efficiency. A balanced marketplace relies on the ability of brokers to facilitate transactions efficiently without excessive regulatory constraints. Over-regulation could stifle the flexibility and responsiveness that brokers provide to both carriers and shippers. By mandating transparency beyond reasonable levels, the proposed rules risk creating a less dynamic and more rigid market structure that could hinder innovation and adaptability.

4. Existing Oversight Measures. It is important to recognize that existing regulations already provide sufficient oversight of broker operations. The FMCSA has mechanisms in place to address fraudulent activities and ensure compliance with fair business practices. Enhancing enforcement of current regulations rather than introducing additional layers of transparency would be a more effective approach to achieving the intended objectives.

5. Potential for Unintended Consequences. While transparency is crucial, overly stringent regulations could create unintended negative consequences such as reduced competition, higher freight costs, and potential conflicts between carriers and brokers. A more collaborative approach that involves stakeholders from all sides of the industry would be beneficial in crafting balanced regulations that protect the interests of all parties involved.

6. FMCSA's Core Mission and Safety Concerns. The core mission of the FMCSA is to ensure the safety of the nation’s highways by reducing crashes, injuries, and fatalities involving large trucks and buses. However, the agency's focus on broker transparency seems to divert attention from more pressing safety concerns that directly impact public well-being. The FMCSA should prioritize initiatives that enhance driver training, enforce hours-of-service regulations, and improve vehicle maintenance compliance rather than imposing additional administrative burdens on brokers.

In conclusion, I urge the FMCSA to reconsider the proposed changes to 49 CFR § 371.3 and to engage in further dialogue with industry stakeholders to develop solutions that promote transparency without unduly burdening brokers or disrupting market dynamics.

Thank you for your attention to this matter.

Sincerely,
Matthew M. Leffler

I would like to offer my thoughts on the issue of rate transparency. The concept of rate transparency imposes costly and difficult to deliver requirements on transportation brokers and 3rd party logistics providers. It also may force information, which is expected to be confidential by the shipping public, to be shared with unwanted parties. Rate Transparency does not reflect the realities of the transportation market, some of which are listed below.

• Most carriers engaged by brokers and 3rd parties are small independent operations. 
• Small carriers often do not have active sales teams, marketing teams, or overhead expenses that brokers and 3rd party logistics providers employee to reach the shipping public.
• Brokers and 3 party logistics providers have client bases that carriers would never be able to reach without significant investments that 3rd parties already make.
• Brokers and 3rd party logistics providers’ sales and marketing efforts allow them to find customers that have a variety of needs and place them with many small carriers who can handle small pieces of the end customer’s business.
• Typical brokers employee customer service teams that service end customers in a way that most small trucking companies cannot.
• Brokers often employee software that allows for direct connections to the shipping public and allows for visibility solutions that carriers do not offer. Even if carriers offered that type of solution, they could not offer a view to the shipping public that would aggregate all their shipments across many carriers. 
• Brokers and 3r party logistics provider play a vital role in keeping the marketplace competitive. The rates they often offer the shipping public are market driven and include risks that carriers are insulated from by the very existence of brokers. A broker may propose rates to the shipping public some time before the shipments are executed, and often have to pay more to carriers than the quoted rate to the shipping public at the time the shipment is picked up. The carriers are insulated from this due to the nature of the market in which brokers must become the buffer between the shipping public and carriers that are unable establish relationships with the shipping public.
• Brokers also bear significant risk in that they often have to offer lenient payment terms to the shipping public, while carriers often enjoy payment upon delivery thanks to the broker/3 party. Thereby making the broker an invaluable middleman to the small independent carrier, by keeping them solvent while the broker must put significant amounts of money at risk in carrying large accounts receivable for long periods of time.
• The dynamic between brokers/3rd party logistics providers and carriers are examples of a free-market environment driven by supply and demand. Rate Transparency would effectively force one party to reveal rate information on one side of the equation without anything in return. It imposes an unfair requirement on the party (broker) who already bears costs that benefits the other party (the carrier).

For all the reasons stated above, the thought of having to share client rates with carriers seems unreasonable and un-American. Carriers do not incur the costs or shoulder the risks and expenses of employing sales staff, marketing staff, software costs, accounting costs, and various overhead expenses. Rates negotiated with the shipping public should remain confidential because the rates include an enormous amount of overhead that is required to gain the business but is not born in any way by the carriers contracted to handle the pick-ups and deliveries of individual shipments. 

Best regards,
Gerry McDevitt, CTB
Senior Vice President, Domestic Services
Mohawk Global

As the owner of a small trucking company for 20+ years, I have no desire or need to see the charges or additional information about the load from a broker. I can easily look at the financials of public brokers and recognize that margins are 8-12%. While the trucking market is very depreciated right now and survival is difficult, knowing actual rates and additional details or contact information is not going to solve my issues. All this will do is push toward a ‘fixed’ price market and further industry consolidation. During the covid pandemic when prices rapidly escalated we didn’t care about transparency as it was a free market to our benefit and if ‘we got our rate’ then the broker was fine to get whatever he could from the shipper. When I buy a car, I don’t need to know what GM bought the parts for or who from or the deal they negotiated, I shop the market for the best price and terms. That being said, let the free market be the free market and the transaction terms between carriers and brokers be the terms. I often ask myself why my fellow carriers want to request rate and contact information? My feeling is that if they do not like the terms of a shipment, they will use this information to try and hurt or intimidate brokers threatening to harm their reputation in the industry or damage their direct customer relationship. In the end, putting more animosity between the two parties when each party needs to recognize that we are partners, not adversaries. This proposed transparency regulation doesn’t move the industry forward.

Trucking is the backbone of our supply chain and we need to get it under control. Transparency in Property Broker Transactions is a good idea on paper, but an untenable idea in practice. If enacted, this regulation will cause an enormous administrative burden on the property brokers, potentially causing many small or individually operated companies to cease operations. In that circumstance, it will have the unintended effect leaving only the large national companies in place, reducing competition and increasing leverage against the motor carrier community. Many property broker are already moving away from transactional based agreements and more toward to transparent agreements with both shipper and motor carriers. Another negative motor carrier impact this regulation will likely have is have property brokers pass along an administrative fee to support the cost of complying. Through long term, mutually beneficial relationships and the good work of industry associations such as the Transportation Intermediaries' Association and the Food Shippers of America the property brokers continue to adhere to good and fair business practices without the need for any further regulation.

In the business I run, we interface with brokers of many kinds. All other forms of brokerage - securities trading, 401k administration, employee benefits, and property & casualty insurance - require that the broker commission/markup be transparent for all parties. This is the result of common sense regulations that have been implemented over time. The result of transparency has been a common standard of ethics and knowledge by all parties involved in the transaction. I have worked in trucking for 35 years. Most freight brokers are ethical, but unfortunately many are unscrupulous. I believe that this effort to improve transparency would get transportation brokers in line with all the other types of brokerage we deal with, and shine a light on bad actors - particularly those who conduct predatory practices and demonstrate a disregard for safety. Above-board brokers would not be affected much in my opinion. We support fair and reasonable increases in transparency.

This is in regards to the "NPRM" entitled "Transparency in Property Broker Transactions."
Thank you for your time and attention given to the comments that I would like to share regarding this proposed rulemaking. I am an after-payment freight cost auditor and have worked in carrier pricing/contracts/negotiations for over 16 years now. From my perspective I can see all of the different roles, whether they be shippers, carriers, brokers, consignees, etc., and how all parties interplay with one another. There obviously needs to be partnering and transparency in these relationships...but to a point. Not to the point where a vital player, brokers/3PLs, can be overrun by an agency that should not be reaching into this area to begin with. We saw with COVID how vital, and yet vulnerable, our supply chain is in America. Why cripple the supply chain via brokers with a requirement that is not necessary and not applicable to how the market now operates.
This proposed rulemaking is misguided as it is it is being sold as a continuation of the broker transparency regulation that was birthed in the 1980s. Anyone working in the transportation market/supply chain would know immediately that this was applicable at that time, post deregulation by MCA of 1980, when brokers operated as commissioned sales agents for motor carriers. That is not how they operate now so why try to expand on a rule that is applicable to services that are not provided as such?
FMCSA stands for Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration. "Safety" being the key word. From their website www.fmcsa.dot.gov/mission, the list "Our Mission: The primary mission of the Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (FMCSA) is to reduce crashes, injuries and fatalities involving large trucks and buses." And it continues in the following paragraph, "...all dedicated to improving the safety of commercial motor vehicles (CMV) and saving lives." None of what they were created for, or they state as their mission, has anything to do with trying to control private contracts that do not involve them or the need for their oversight. What is being proposed as a requirement is for two business parties to expose what exists in a private contract. FMCSA should be focusing on the safety of our carriers, including addressing fraudulent bad actors that are jumping in a driver seat and recklessly driving on the highways with our families and children.
Again, these are private contracts. If a shipper or carrier do not want to partner with a broker, they do not have to. If a shipper or carrier wants to obtain a cheaper rate, then they can seek service from a different broker. There is no loss of opportunities for carriers in these scenarios and therefore no logical reason for exposing the contractual information that is not meant to be for consumption outside of the contracted parties.
Finally, we must take a moment to reflect on the broader picture of what this proposed rulemaking can impact. Yes it will impact brokers and likely cripple their business. However, that too will impact the American Supply Chain which can not be crippled yet again like we saw during COVID. We should have learned about the absolute need to protect the vulnerable ecosystem of our supply chain. But, we also must consider other industries. What will happen in the real estate/brokerage industry if they were to try and adopt some similar regulation? What about the banking industry? It would terrify all consumers, as it should. But because so many are removed from the relationships and working within the transportation industry, they do not realize how concerned they should be regarding this current proposal. What precedence are we about to set for the other industries and how far are we going to cripple business and consumers in America?
In conclusion, this proposed rulemaking is unjust and not necessary. It is a gross administrative overreach by the FMCSA that should not have any authority to involve themselves in. Even for the simple reason that this is not a safety concern, this proposed rulemaking should be stopped. If that is not enough of a reason, how about the crippling of our supply chain or the precedence we are about to set for other industries? It's unjustified and quite honestly, terrifying.

Of course, we never want to come to the table with an issue and not offer a solution, we must all do our part. The solution here though is simple...shut it down and recognize the character of the transportation and brokerage market have changed beyond the days of "commissioned sales agents." Let us get our focus back on the items that are truly threatening to the supply chain, such as fraud.

Thank you again for your time and consideration of this comment.

I am afraid that the proponents for this rule change miss out on the unintended consequences of this bill & don't realize why we are in this position. The supply & demand freight market we are in has ebbed & flowed since deregulation. Rates are like a pendulum swinging back & forth, sometimes in the favor of the carrier, others in that of the broker/customer, & can even swing in many markets throughout the day. This "Us vs. Them" mentality is perpetuated with each swing, causing longer & harder swings each time. Carriers, proponents of this rule & the good folks deciding on this should also note that when the rates are high, there is ZERO push for this rule change. It is only when the demand is lower than supply that there is this demand for change. Granted, when the US shut down during the Covid Pandemic, as the supply of loads crashed, so did the prices. Carriers either went out of business, re-negotiated loans & cut costs to get operating costs down & dug into savings, & the smart carriers worked with their direct customers & leveraged relationships with good brokers to keep rates at a livable wage so they could stay in business. When the economy opened back up, carriers came out of the woodwork, reinstated or started new authorities & were demanding $4-6+ a mile for loads with zero regard for what brokers with contract rates or customers had to lose to get their products shipped. Naturally, the supply & demand leveled out & began to slip again, the customers & brokers had a very bad taste in their mouth & "stuck it" to those carriers that demanded the ridiculous rates & drove freight rates back down. When you look at what happened during the high rates, those customers that had to pay these exorbitant rates either were forced out of business, and/or, were forced to pass the prices on to the American consumer, causing inflation. I saw all of these happen as I am both an asset-based carrier as well as a freight broker, so I saw both sides. The carriers that are complaining live off the spot market & have few, if any, direct customers of their own. They don't understand that ALL sides of the transaction need to make a profit. Carriers close their doors and the market swings due to low supply of trucks. Customers/brokers consistently have to lose money because carriers demand high rates, then they close and there's less freight to ship, causing rates to fall again. It’s a viscous cycle.
Luckily, there are carriers & brokers like myself. My trucks, my customers, & the carriers that I use have all kept each other alive through multiple lengthy market swings, & because of this we have had consistent rates for the better part of 10 years, only making rate adjustments when fuel makes major swings. I broker out several million dollars of freight a year & post out less than 100 loads every year, because I have built good enough relationships with my customers & the carriers that haul for me that I am able to keep the rates fair. With my trucks, I don't care how much a broker or customer makes as long as I get a fair, profitable rate. If not, I don't haul the load, simple as that. If I am in a bad freight area, I will run a truck empty a few hundred miles before taking a losing load. 
A few years ago in the trucking world there was a campaign "Say NO to cheap freight", encouraging drivers to leave the freight on the dock until shippers got desperate enough to bring the rates up to a livable wage. Carriers need to start doing that again, but make it FAIR rates. I can't tell you how many times after President Trump was announced the winner on November 6th, 2024 that I had carriers calling on my posted spot market loads demanding outrageously high rates and saying, verbatim, "You better get used to these rates because Daddy Trump is back in office". Carriers demanding outrageous rates will only destroy our economy again & perpetuate the “Us vs. Them” mentality.
Furthermore, the carriers don't understand the unintended consequences of this rule change. The common belief is that the broker has to reveal what the all-in rate is at the time of booking, which is false. The rate isn't required to be proven until 48 hours after it is requested, which means if the carrier accepts the load, there is a high likelihood that the load will be delivered before the carrier knows the all-in rate, so the argument of negotiations on current loads is moot. Additionally, it opens the door for big carriers to come in & cut current rates & take these loads that the proponents of this bill would normally haul.
Next, rates to customers are proprietary. As a carrier, I do not know what rates are that other carriers haul for. Everyone bids their rates, & that is what keeps the market competitive. It prevents price gouging and inflation.
Finally, why does one entity have to open up their books, yet the entity demanding this not have to open theirs? How does one side get to dictate what one entity profits with no equal repercussion?

As an owner-operator with years of experience in the logistics industry, I strongly support the FMCSA's proposed transparency rule. Here's why this law is crucial for our industry:
Transparency Benefits All Parties
Fair Competition: Revealing broker margins ensures a level playing field for all carriers.
Informed Decision-Making: Access to complete transaction records allows us to make better business choices.
Accountability: Transparency discourages fraudulent practices and double/triple brokering.
Personal Experiences Highlighting the Need for Change
I've encountered multiple instances of double-brokered loads across various load boards (DAT, Selectus, Omnitrax, Solara, My Virtual Fleet).
Excessive broker margins, sometimes reaching 30% or more, significantly impact our bottom line.
Lack of transparency has led to payment disputes and delayed compensation.
Proposed Solutions
Implement a standardized electronic record-keeping system accessible to all parties.
Cap broker margins at a reasonable percentage, similar to credit card processing fees (3-4%).
Create an industry-wide rating system for both brokers and carriers to promote accountability.
Establish clear guidelines for backpay in cases of misrepresentation or failed services.
By supporting this transparency law, we're not trying to vilify anyone. Instead, we're advocating for a system that promotes trust, fairness, and sustainable business practices for all stakeholders in the logistics industry.

I am afraid that the proponents for this rule change miss out on the unintended consequences of this bill & don't realize why we are in this position. The supply & demand freight market we are in has ebbed & flowed since deregulation. Rates are like a pendulum swinging back & forth, sometimes in the favor of the carrier, others in that of the broker/customer, & can even swing in many markets throughout the day. This "Us vs. Them" mentality is perpetuated with each swing, causing longer & harder swings each time. Carriers, proponents of this rule & the good folks deciding on this should also note that when the rates are high, there is ZERO push for this rule change. It is only when the demand is lower than supply that there is this demand for change. Granted, when the US shut down during the Covid Pandemic, as the supply of loads crashed, so did the prices. Carriers either went out of business, re-negotiated loans & cut costs to get operating costs down & dug into savings, & the smart carriers worked with their direct customers & leveraged relationships with good brokers to keep rates at a livable wage so they could stay in business. When the economy opened back up, carriers came out of the woodwork, reinstated or started new authorities & were demanding $4-6+ a mile for loads with zero regard for what brokers with contract rates or customers had to lose to get their products shipped. Naturally, the supply & demand leveled out & began to slip again, the customers & brokers had a very bad taste in their mouth & "stuck it" to those carriers that demanded the ridiculous rates & drove freight rates back down. When you look at what happened during the high rates, those customers that had to pay these exorbitant rates either were forced out of business, and/or, were forced to pass the prices on to the American consumer, causing inflation. I saw all of these happen as I am both an asset-based carrier as well as a freight broker, so I saw both sides. The carriers that are complaining live off the spot market & have few, if any, direct customers of their own. They don't understand that ALL sides of the transaction need to make a profit. Carriers close their doors and the market swings due to low supply of trucks. Customers/brokers consistently have to lose money because carriers demand high rates, then they close and there's less freight to ship, causing rates to fall again. It’s a viscous cycle.
Luckily, there are carriers & brokers like myself. My trucks, my customers, & the carriers that I use have all kept each other alive through multiple lengthy market swings, & because of this we have had consistent rates for the better part of 10 years, only making rate adjustments when fuel makes major swings. I broker out several million dollars of freight a year & post out less than 100 loads every year, because I have built good enough relationships with my customers & the carriers that haul for me that I am able to keep the rates fair. With my trucks, I don't care how much a broker or customer makes as long as I get a fair, profitable rate. If not, I don't haul the load, simple as that. If I am in a bad freight area, I will run a truck empty a few hundred miles before taking a losing load.
A few years ago in the trucking world there was a campaign "Say NO to cheap freight", encouraging drivers to leave the freight on the dock until shippers got desperate enough to bring the rates up to a livable wage. Carriers need to start doing that again, but make it FAIR rates. 
Furthermore, the carriers don't understand the unintended consequences of this rule change. The common belief is that the broker has to reveal what the all-in rate is at the time of booking, which is false. The rate isn't required to be proven until 48 hours after it is requested, which means if the carrier accepts the load, there is a high likelihood that the load will be delivered before the carrier knows the all-in rate, so the argument of negotiations on current loads is moot. Additionally, it opens the door for big carriers to come in & cut current rates & take these loads that the proponents of this bill would normally haul.
Next, rates to customers are proprietary. As a carrier, I do not know what rates are that other carriers haul for. Everyone bids their rates, & that is what keeps the market competitive. It prevents price gouging and inflation.
Finally, why does one entity have to open up their books, yet the entity demanding this not have to open theirs? How does one side get to dictate what one entity profits with no equal repercussion?

Opposition to FMCSA Broker Transparency Rule
The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration’s (FMCSA) proposed rule on broker transparency raises serious concerns for freight brokers. If implemented, it would impose additional administrative and financial burdens on legitimate businesses and could disrupt the delicate balance of the freight industry.

Time, Effort, and Financial Investments in Operating as a Freight Broker
Operating a freight brokerage demands substantial time, effort, and capital. Key cost factors include:

Vetting Carriers

Subscription fees for vetting tools and databases may cost hundreds or even thousands of dollars per month, and thorough background checks add to the workload.
Securing Customers

Hiring a dedicated sales team to attract shippers significantly increases overhead. Salaries, commissions, and marketing expenses can total thousands monthly.
Phone and Customer Service

Employing skilled dispatchers and customer service representatives is essential for smooth operations, but salaries and benefits are a large portion of the budget.
Technology and Infrastructure

Websites, software, and subscription costs can range widely, while office rent and utilities add another layer of monthly expenses.
Regulatory Compliance and Insurance

Brokers must hold a $75,000 surety bond (FMCSA requirement), and insurance premiums can run from $1,000 to $5,000 annually, depending on coverage.
Why the Proposed Rule Is Misguided
Under the new rule, brokers would be required to disclose financial transaction details, including carrier payments and the broker’s margins. This poses challenges in three key areas:

Administrative Burden

Maintaining detailed records and providing them upon request diverts resources from core operations.
Exposure of Sensitive Information

Revealing rate structures and profit margins undermines a broker’s ability to negotiate competitive deals.
Misplaced Focus

Instead of addressing fraud or unsafe carrier practices, the rule targets brokers with additional regulations that disproportionately impact smaller operations.
Potential Effects of the Proposed Rule
Increased Administrative Work
Mandatory electronic record-keeping and timely retrieval demands more staff, technology, and funding.
Competitive Disadvantage
Disclosing transaction details erodes proprietary business strategies, making it harder to maintain profitability and balance.
Market Disruption
Smaller brokers may not withstand the rising operational costs, leading to reduced options for shippers and carriers, ultimately driving up prices.
FMCSA’s Role in Safety
The FMCSA’s primary mandate is to regulate carrier safety. Factors such as unqualified drivers, unsafe vehicles, and non-compliance with existing regulations represent real public risks. Freight brokers, by contrast, do not operate vehicles—they serve as intermediaries who vet carriers and facilitate transactions.

Fraud Prevention: Clamping down on double brokering and identity theft would help brokers locate trustworthy carriers more efficiently, lowering business costs and enhancing industry integrity.
Carrier Safety Compliance: Ensuring carriers meet FMCSA safety standards protects the public and preserves confidence in the logistics chain.
Misunderstanding Negotiation Dynamics
The proposed rule overlooks how brokers negotiate separately with shippers and carriers. Shipper contracts and broker-carrier agreements operate on different terms, so requiring disclosure of the broker’s margins disrupts these private negotiations and can limit competitive flexibility.

Exposure of Sensitive Business Practices
Demanding that brokers publicize their financials:

Compromises Fair Negotiations: Making internal profit margins visible lets shippers and carriers leverage that information against brokers.
Reduces Market Competitiveness: Proprietary strategies, carrier relationships, and operational efficiencies become accessible to competitors.
Conclusion
Focusing on carrier safety and preventing fraud would yield far more benefits than imposing additional transparency obligations on brokers. By concentrating on carriers’ compliance and vehicle safety, the FMCSA can better protect the public without burdening brokers with rules that threaten competition and raise costs. Ultimately, the proposed broker transparency rule risks undermining the very market efficiencies and innovative practices it claims to protect.

Dear Sir or Madam:

Axle Logistics, LLC ("Axle Logistics" or "Axle") submits these comments in response to the notice of proposed rulemaking (“NPRM”) that was published in the Federal Register on Wednesday, November 20, 2024. The Federal Motor Carrier Safety Administration (“FMCSA”) is seeking comments on proposed amendments to its property broker regulations in response to petitions for rulemaking received from the Owner-Operators Independent Drivers Association (“OOIDA”) and the Small Business in Transportation Coalition (“SBTC”). These petitions seek to significantly expand certain regulations that currently govern property brokers; specifically, the “right to review” certain records for business transactions set forth in 49 CFR 371.3. 

As more particularly described in the attached materials, Axle Logistics opposes the proposed changes contained in this NPRM. As contemplated, the proposed rule fundamentally confuses a broker’s role in modern supply chains. That confusion, coupled with the forced disclosure of raw pricing information without context, will create misinformation regarding pricing to the detriment of the entire transportation community, including the motor carriers it seeks to benefit. Furthermore, the sheer operational impact of the rule would cripple broker operations, like Axle, that work with a voluminous carrier network. Finally, Axle Logistics asserts that FMCSA is not authorized by Congress to promulgate this proposed rule, particularly in light of the United States Supreme Court’s overturning of Chevron deference in its recent decision in Loper Bright Enterprises v. Raimondo, 603 U.S. ___ (2024), 144 S. Ct. 2244.

Thank you for your consideration,

Axle Logistics, LLC

The FMCSA’s proposed rule on Transparency in Property Broker’s Transactions raises significant concerns. Allowing unrestricted access to sensitive broker data risks undermining the competitive advantage of established leaders in this industry. While anyone can technically become a broker, successful brokerage operations rely on far more than simple rate and margin data. Pricing strategies are the result of deep industry expertise, market presence, and the ability to navigate complex variables, including business development, human capital, backend operations, and advanced technology.
The proposed transparency provision relies on isolated data points that are vague and potentially misleading without proper context, correlation, or normalization. Sharing this incomplete and sensitive information could harm competition, enabling cherry-picking of data and reducing market efficiency. Worse, it could lead to monopolistic behavior, drive successful brokerages out of business, and create barriers to entry that hinder the principles of free trade—one of the foundational values of this country.
This provision has not been standard practice in the industry for over 45 years for good reason. It does not enhance transparency; instead, it risks disrupting the delicate balance of competition, innovation, and deep expertise that supports our nation’s critical infrastructure. I urge the FMCSA to reconsider this proposal in favor of policies that promote competition, innovation, and growth across the entire supply chain ecosystem.

Here are some reasons why broker transparency—specifically, knowing how much money brokers are making on loads—could benefit owner-operators:

1. **Fair Compensation**: When owner-operators know how much brokers are earning, they can assess whether they’re being paid fairly for their work. Transparency can lead to better negotiations and prevent brokers from taking an excessive margin, ensuring that operators are adequately compensated for their time, effort, and expenses.

2. **Better Decision-Making**: Understanding the broker's cut can help owner-operators make informed decisions about which loads to accept. If they know the rate being offered by the broker compared to what the broker is earning, they can choose loads that align better with their financial goals.

3. **Building Trust**: Transparency fosters trust between brokers and owner-operators. When brokers are open about how much they’re making, it helps eliminate suspicion and creates a more collaborative working relationship, leading to long-term partnerships.

4. **Industry Standardization**: Having consistent transparency across brokers can standardize the industry, making it easier for owner-operators to compare rates and services. This would contribute to a more competitive market where brokers are incentivized to offer fair rates.

5. **Fighting Exploitation**: Without transparency, there’s a risk that brokers may take advantage of owner-operators by inflating their cut or hiding behind complex pricing structures. Transparency reduces this risk and ensures everyone in the transaction is treated fairly.

6. **Improved Sustainability**: If owner-operators are paid more fairly, they’re likely to have a more sustainable business. This benefits the entire freight and transportation ecosystem by maintaining a healthy and competitive market.

These points provide a solid argument for why more broker transparency is beneficial to both owner-operators and the industry as a whole!

The Transparency in Property Broker Transactions rule is necessary. I understand freight brokers think this is a rule in search of a problem but their secrecy around transactions and failure to disclose their margins has far-reaching consequences beyond the trucking industry. Far too often carriers, shippers, and brokers complained about double brokering and blind transactions that have caused fraud. Yet, the brokers are fighting the one regulation that will put an end to the very fraud they are complaining about. The truth is that brokers don't want this rule because not only will it require the disclosure of their margins, but it will also require all parties to disclose and be truthful about how many parties are involved in a transaction.

Double brokering is a huge problem in this industry, it raises the cost of doing business, it lowers the rates the actual carrier hauling a load gets paid and it makes it easier for fraudsters to take off with a load because all parties involved are unaware of each other and who is doing the work. Last year the carrier I am employed with was doing business with a broker that had us dropping trailers at their customer. This is a shipper where drivers are not supposed to go inside and talk to the employees. When I would drop our trailers and come back to pick them up loaded our cargo bars were going missing. These bars are not cheap anymore and after my employer spent several hundred dollars replacing cargo bars one month and after repeatedly notifying the broker, I decided to go inside and confront the employees. When I spoke to someone inside they told me the broker was supposed to send them an email when there were cargo bars in a trailer so they could ensure they were accounted for. The only problem is that the broker they mentioned was not the broker we were doing business with. 

It turns out that because the shipper places all the paperwork for loaded trailers in a mailbox outside of the building and doesn't interact with the drivers, they were unaware that the broker they give every single load to was outsourcing the loads to other brokers. In some cases, the loads were being brokered multiple times before getting posted to load boards. Because of the transaction record waiver in every single contract the shipper was unaware of all the subcontracting and the carriers were unaware of the double brokering. All the drivers knew was to show up, hook a trailer, and pick up your paperwork. All the shipper knew was that someone was picking up the load. I wish I could provide specifics but due to do not disclose agreements I can't name names, I can only tell you this practice is far more common than anyone is aware of due to transparency waivers.

The Transparency rule would have prevented this kind of dishonesty because everyone involved in a transaction would automatically receive copies of the transaction records. The shipper would instantly see more than three parties involved in the transaction either admonish the broker they contracted with or shop around for a better deal.

This problem is not just relegated to the trucking industry. Far too often many people pay way too much for products or services because they order something on the internet or call a company to inquire about a service thinking the company they are calling provides the service only to unwittingly be involved in a brokered transaction as the person on the other end tells them they can accommodate them, quotes them a price, sends them a receipt showing they provided the product or service and then instantly outsources everything to someone else after shaving money off the top. This is incredibly dishonest because if I am hiring someone to provide a service, I expect them or someone working directly for them to perform the service I paid for, I don't expect to overpay for it and have it subcontracted multiple ts at great expense to me. The FMCSA may not be able to stop this in every industry but we can give the transportation industry the ability to detect it through transaction record disclosure.

The TIA keeps telling you to fight fraud, that fraud is the bigger enemy, I agree with them that fraud is a bigger enemy so start by eliminating the fraud by requiring passing this rule. If everyone has a right to see the transaction records and no one can waiver that right then everyone is aware of every party in a transaction and fraudsters can be detected and then kicked out of the market. Finally, a regulation that can be waived is not a regulation because a party negatively affected by it will always require it to be waived to do business with them. You must prohibit waivers or the regulation will be ignored.

I just don’t see how this benefits anyone. The rate on any given day is determined by the supply and demand of trucks in a specific area for a particular lane.

This does nothing to help carriers negotiate better rates, since the transaction has already taken place. From what I’ve gathered, a carrier’s satisfaction with a rate seems to depend more on the broker’s margin than the actual market conditions. If a broker makes a 10% margin, the carrier might walk away feeling like they got a fair deal. But if the broker makes 30%, the carrier may feel like they got shortchanged—even though the rate was set by the market for that specific day and lane. In that case, the carrier isn’t necessarily underpaid, but the customer likely got overcharged.

Beyond that, there’s already a strong bogeyman mentality toward brokers in this industry, particularly from carriers. Many people don’t fully understand basic economics and blame brokers for all their financial struggles. They also may not realize that brokers aim to make money over a six-month to year-long period rather than on a per-load basis. So 30% today is -20% next month.

All I see this doing is creating more hostility. It’s not going to change rates—it’s only going to change how carriers feel about the rates they receive.

Broker transparency is particularly vital for owner-operators, as it directly impacts their ability to make informed decisions regarding their financial and investment strategies. Owner-operators often engage in significant financial transactions and rely on brokers for expert guidance and market insights. When brokers provide clear, transparent information about their fees, commissions, trading practices, and potential conflicts of interest, owner-operators can assess the true cost of their investment decisions and avoid hidden charges that could erode their profits. This transparency enables owner-operators to evaluate whether the advice they receive aligns with their business goals, ensuring they are not being steered toward products that benefit the broker more than themselves. Moreover, transparent communication fosters a sense of trust and accountability, allowing owner-operators to build stronger relationships with their brokers, which is essential for collaboration in achieving long-term financial objectives. In an industry where decisions can significantly impact the financial health of a business, having access to accurate and open information is crucial for owner-operators to navigate the complexities of the market effectively, mitigate risks, and capitalize on opportunities. Ultimately, broker transparency empowers owner-operators to take control of their financial futures, ensuring that their interests are prioritized in all dealings.

Dear FMCSA Administrator,

I am writing to express my deep concern about the lack of transparency in the freight brokerage industry. As a truck driver I have experienced firsthand the unfair practices of freight brokers who prioritize profits over honesty and transparency.

The issue is straightforward: freight brokers often fail to provide accurate and timely information about loads, including rates, weights, dimensions, and special requirements. This lack of transparency leads to delays, fines, and even accidents. Moreover, brokers frequently charge hidden fees, reducing the already slim margins of carriers and owner-operators.

To address this issue, I urge the FMCSA to implement regulations that ensure freight brokers provide transparent and honest load information. This includes:

- Clear and accurate load descriptions
- Transparent pricing, including disclosure of all fees and charges
Furthermore, I request that the FMCSA prohibit freight brokers from blacklisting carriers and owner-operators who request transparency paperwork or question the accuracy of load information. This practice is unfair and retaliatory, and it undermines the integrity of the transportation industry.

The FMCSA has the authority to regulate freight brokers and ensure they operate with transparency and honesty. I urge you to take the following actions:

- Develop and enforce regulations that require freight brokers to provide transparent load information
- Establish a system for carriers and owner-operators to report instances of non-compliance
- Investigate and penalize freight brokers who engage in unfair practices, including blacklisting

Thank you for considering my request. I believe that transparency and honesty are essential for a safe and efficient transportation industry. I look forward to your response.

Sincerely,

FMCSA Public Comment on Broker Transparency
To: FMCSA Docket No. FMCSA-2023-0257
Subject: Need for Broker Margin Regulation and Freight Platform Oversight
Dear FMCSA Members,
I appreciate the opportunity to provide comments on broker transaction transparency. However,
transparency alone will not resolve the structural issues within the freight industry.
As an independent contractor, I have worked on both sides of the industry-as a broker and as a
carrier-negotiating freight rates with shippers and brokers. I have seen firsthand the lack of
regulation and the significant financial gap that affects both carriers and shippers.
Shippers pay fixed rates, but carriers have no visibility into actual transaction prices and little power
to negotiate fairly. Brokers freely mark up loads without limits, often leaving carriers with rates that
do not cover operational costs.
This is not a transparency issue. This is a regulation issue.
1. Transparency Alone Will Not Solve the Problem
Brokers currently have no limits on their margins, leading to:
- Carriers receiving unsustainably low rates.
- No accountability for broker markups.
- Rate manipulation harming both carriers and shippers.
Proposal: FMCSA should:
- Cap broker profits at 10-15% of the freight rate.
- Ensure carriers receive at least 85-90% of the total freight charge.
- Prohibit brokers from keeping fuel surcharge payments meant for carriers.

Example: If a load pays $2,000, the broker should not take more than $200-$300, ensuring the
carrier gets at least $1,700-$1,800.
2. Detention and Layover Fees
Brokers profit from detention and layover fees when only carriers suffer the loss.
- If a shipper pays a $50/hour detention fee, it should go entirely to the carrier.
- FMCSA must prohibit brokers from taking any portion of detention or layover payments.
3. Regulating Freight Platforms like DAT and Truckstop
These platforms dominate the market but operate without oversight.
Problems:
- Brokers manipulate rates without regulation.
- Double brokerage continues unchecked.
- Shippers lack visibility into carrier payments.
Proposal: FMCSA should regulate freight platforms by requiring:
- Registration and compliance with FMCSA rules.
- Reporting of shipper payments and actual carrier rates.
- Integration with FMCSA to detect abusive pricing.
4. FMCSA Should Develop a Government-Regulated Freight Portal
Proposal: FMCSA should create a platform where all loads are registered with real pricing data:
- Shippers report what they paid and to whom.
- Brokers log their sales prices and buyers.
- Carriers record their final payment.
Benefits:

- Eliminates double brokerage and fraud.
- Prevents rate manipulation.
- Provides FMCSA real-time visibility.
Conclusion: Regulation is the Key
FMCSA must implement:
1. Broker margin regulations to protect carriers.
2. A ban on brokers taking detention and layover payments.
3. Regulation of freight platforms to prevent rate manipulation.
4. A mandatory system for reporting freight transactions.
Without these changes, transparency will be meaningless, and industry abuses will continue.
FMCSA must take action to protect fair competition.
Thank you for your time and consideration.

In many cases, carriers experience a cost savings and increased revenue as a result of

Shippers and carriers both benefit from transfer of transportation risk to the broker in large part. The largest businesses in America utilize brokers to arrange their transportation as do smaller businesses for these reasons.

The rule as proposed aims to provide transparency, however the method the FMCSA uses
to approach rate transparency is not an accurate representation of anything meaningful.
The rule as proposed would require the broker to disclose the rate the shipper pays the
broker to arrange the transportation and the rate at which the broker hires the carrier. The
incorrect approach is to view the di0erence in these rates as a profit. It is not.
Brokers continually invest in technology to combat fraud as well as ine0iciencies in the
marketplace. Brokers invest in communication systems that make doing business more
e0ective on both the shipper and carrier ends.
Fraud and theft prevention is the greatest challenge to the U.S. transportation system
today. Broker investment in and focus on fraud and theft prevention is something neither
the shipper nor the carriers have time to solve. It is the body of good broker citizens who are
leading the way toward solving the biggest epidemic to plague the highways. We encourage
the FMCSA to focus its e0orts in this area and discover you have a willing marketplace
partner to support innovation in greater freight security.
Brokers invest in sta0 who handle critical processes in the supply chain. Brokers invest in
insurance coverages that potentially add a layer of assurance and security to both the
shipper and carrier, depending on the circumstance.
All of those costs, and others, remain unseen in the way the FMCSA approaches rate
transparency. To force this disclosure requirement on brokers is unconscionable because it
is not a fair representation of the value added to the supply chain brokers provide nor does
truthfully represent the business costs that come with operating a business that provides
these benefits to the market.

The FMCSA attempt to require market participants to reveal private business information is
a broad overreach and a misinterpretation of the breadth of FMCSA’s authority.
Some of the statutes that FMCSA cites as the basis for its authority likely do not authorize
required disclosure to private third parties. And while there may be a broad grant of
authority, the ICC and FMCSA have not regulated broker charges and have repeatedly
recognized that brokers benefit both shippers and carriers. Additionally, Congress
deregulated the trucking industry with an intent to rely on market forces, which makes it
peculiar for FMCSA to now find it has authority to interfere with a broker’s commercial
relations.

The government does not usually require industry participants to share proprietary and
trade secret information with commercial counter parties with which it will likely negotiate
in the future.
This lack of information about another party’s profit margin is not unique to brokerage,
indeed, it is not unique to transportation. Just as a shipper has no idea what a carrier’s
margin might be, a party buying a pair of shoes at Walmart has no idea how much it cost
the manufacturer to make the shoe, or how much Walmart is marking up the shoe. There is
no law that requires Walmart to disclose its mark-up between what it paid the
manufacturer and what it is selling the shoe for. There is no reason to treat a broker
di0erently. Nor is there any reason to treat a broker di0erently than a motor carrier that
might contract with a shipper directly.
The rule, as proposed, will put brokers at risk of having to choose between regulatory
compliance or breaching commercial contracts with their customers.
Both OOIDA’s and SBTC’s proposed rules run counter to the clear Congressional intent
expressed in the federal Defend Trade Secrets Act of 2016 (“DTSA”) and its state law
counterpart the Uniform Trade Secrets Act (“UTSA”). The UTSA has been adopted in nearly
or perhaps every state. And, the DTSA passed unanimously in the Senate and by a vote of
410-2 in the House. That is a compelling argument that Congressional intent is to protect
trade secrets.
DTSA defines “trade secrets” as “all forms and types of financial, business . . . information,
including . . . compilations, . . . formulas, methods, techniques, processes . . . if – (A) the
owner thereof has taken reasonable measures to keep such information secret; and (B) the
information derives independent economic value, actual or potential, from not being
generally know to, and not being readily ascertainable through proper means by, another
person who can obtain economic value from the disclosure or use of the information.” 18
U.S.C. § 1839(3). The type of information that the proposed rule seeks to be disclosed
clearly fall within the definition of a “trade secret.”

sensitive pricing and business information, which could severely damage brokers’
competitive standing.

This proposed rule adds unnecessary restrictions that impede the free market. Even the

Brokers circumvent 49 CFR § 371.3 by requiring carriers to sign waivers that nullify their right to review transactional records. This regulation mandates brokers to maintain and provide access to financial records for transparency and to prevent price manipulation. However, these waivers effectively strip carriers of this protection, undermining fair market practices.

By restricting access to transaction details, brokers have created an environment where freight rates are manipulated without oversight. This distorts fair competition, suppresses carrier earnings, and consolidates market power in favor of brokers. This is dangerous. Such practices erode trust and stability within the trucking industry, harming over 900,000 small carriers the most. A continuation of non compliance could eventually destabilize the trucking industry. 

To address this issue, the FMCSA should amend 49 CFR § 371.3 to explicitly state that this law is unwaivable. Strict enforcement should be implemented for four years to ensure compliance. This period would allow for data collection to evaluate the impact and determine if further adjustments are necessary.

Additionally, the FMCSA should establish a clear list of consequences for violations, with escalating penalties for repeat offenses. These should include fines, increased scrutiny, and eventual suspension or termination of broker authority in extreme cases. Strong enforcement measures will deter noncompliance and help restore fairness to the industry.

Everyone should be deeply concerned about what happens when brokers, double brokers, and dispatch services outnumber carriers and drivers. As these intermediaries continue to grow while the balance of power shifts entirely away from those who physically move the freight. Carriers will struggle to survive under excessive rate manipulation and shrinking margins. If this trend continues unchecked, the trucking industry could face a complete collapse, leaving the economy in turmoil as the nation loses its most vital transportation backbone.

Transparency between brokers and trucking companies is crucial for building trust and ensuring smooth business operations. Clear communication about rates, load details, payment terms, and any potential issues prevents misunderstandings and reduces the risk of disputes. When both parties are upfront, it promotes fairness, ensures drivers are properly compensated, and helps avoid hidden fees or unexpected deductions. Transparency also leads to better load planning and on-time deliveries, ultimately benefiting everyone in the supply chain. In an industry where margins can be tight, honesty and openness create long-term partnerships and a more reliable, efficient logistics network.

As a driver of a 26-foot box truck making deliveries throughout the USA, I have witnessed firsthand the significant challenges posed by the lack of broker transparency. The current system often leaves drivers in the dark about what shippers are actually paying for freight, and brokers typically try to negotiate as low as possible, even when the rates are far below a fair and sustainable level.

For example, shippers may be paying $4000 for a load, but brokers will try to offer drivers as little as $1000. This practice not only undervalues the work and expenses involved in transporting freight but also leads to unnecessary frustration and time lost in searching for loads. There have been countless times when I've spent up to 1-3 days waiting for a load, only to find brokers offering rates below $1 per mile, which is unsustainable for any driver.

It's clear that no driver should be expected to operate at a rate lower than $2 per mile, especially for a 26-foot box truck. The lack of transparency makes it difficult to negotiate better pay or make informed decisions about which loads are truly worth accepting. Drivers deserve the right to know what the shipper is paying for freight before they book a load, which would allow for fairer negotiations and a better chance at earning a living wage.

Broker transparency will help level the playing field, foster better relationships between brokers and drivers, and ensure that freight rates reflect the true value of the work being done. I strongly urge the FMCSA to implement measures that require brokers to disclose the rates they are being paid by shippers before a load is booked. This would allow drivers to make better decisions and be paid fairly for their hard work.

I work as a dispatcher for an auto carrier and this broker transparency act will significantly improve trucking industry overall. Sometimes we see brokers pocking more money than carriers simply because they can. On top of that, more often than not, some brokers will try to start a bidding war with a rate that's already low to begin with. Knowing the full price the shipper is paying will reduce carriers bidding against each other. 
Having access to itemized charges the brokers have with the shipper will also provide clearer understanding which brokers are ethical. We often hear that brokers charge various fees including re-routing, change of dates, fuel surcharge, overweight fee, top load option etc. Instead of disclosing such fees and giving it to the carriers, who actually do the work, brokers keep the fees themselves. These fees could easily range from $100 to $1000. 
Sometimes, we also hear from customers of the total balance they were charged by the brokers. The amount of money some of these brokers make is truly mind blowing, when carriers are making just enough money to break even during slow season with huge amounts of payments that come naturally with being a trucking carrier company. 
Some might argue that this is just the result of "supply and demand" fluctuation in pricing. However, I believe that carriers are entitled to brokers' records of charges and payments. Trucking industry cannot exist without harmonious relationship between carriers and brokers. Broker transparency is crucial for the industry.

I am a small business owner operating since 2012. I run a fleet of 6 trucks and use brokers to find freight. I am writing to you today because of the unbearable conditions in this market. I have many good relationships with brokers that I consistently work with, this gives me a clear picture of who the bad and good actors are. Brokers use my equipment ($300,000+ investment for each truck and trailer) to haul their loads. They pay the carrier an unrealistic amount of money that doesn't compensate for our expenses. They are not regulated in any way of what "fair" is to the carriers and try to rip us off to make money. When we talk to these brokers, they say "You chose to be in this business". Most brokerages hire employees out of high school and only care about their commission, thus threatening the survival of the carrier. They do not understand the expense that is required to operate fleets safely and abide by all laws/regulations set by the DOT/FMCSA. We are hit with sky high operating costs as repair costs and parts pricing are COVID rates high, they did not go down. Driver pay is higher, and yet loads paying are half the price. Requiring brokers to provide requests will not put any oversee on them to save carriers and keep them in business. The statistics show the traumatizing amount of carriers that left this industry, yet brokerages are expanding and opening new offices. I have nothing against brokerages making a living, we are all in the market to do so. I understand that markets fluctuate, but after working 13 years in the business, customers do pay for fuel and enough to cover expenses of carriers. There is no regulation on brokers except the $75,000 bond that does not help in much situations. I have been a victim of broker fraud, a brokerage that opens up, sells loads and goes bankrupt within a short period of time. The money owed is way over the $75,000 bond, and yet again the carrier is punished for doing the job. We support so many industries- fuel, repair shops, fuel taxes, ELD, etc, and yet we are not compensated for doing our job. There are many regulations on carriers both office and for our equipment, yet brokers do what they want and rip us off. 

FMCSA, I ask you to please regulate brokerages to keep carriers in business. If we close down, trucks won't be on the road and food will not be on our tables. One player is only benefiting here, not the customer or carrier, just the brokers because there is no set regulations yet they are a third party. Having brokers show tenders will not change if they give us sufficient money or not, most of them do not want to talk to you if you ask for more money.
